speeches.speech: I'll pheeze you, in faith.
lines: I'll pheeze you, in faith.

speeches.speech: A pair of stocks, you rogue!
lines: A pair of stocks, you rogue!

speeches.speech: Y'are a baggage; the Slys are no rogues. Look in the chronicles: we came in with Richard Conqueror. Therefore, paucas pallabris; let the world slide. Sessa!
lines: Y'are a baggage; the Slys are no rogues. Look in the
chronicles: we came in with Richard Conqueror. Therefore, paucas
pallabris; let the world slide. Sessa!

speeches.speech: You will not pay for the glasses you have burst?
lines: You will not pay for the glasses you have burst?

speeches.speech: No, not a denier. Go by, Saint Jeronimy, go to thy cold bed and warm thee.
lines: No, not a denier. Go by, Saint Jeronimy, go to thy cold bed
and warm thee.

speeches.speech: I know my remedy; I must go fetch the third-borough.
lines: I know my remedy; I must go fetch the third-borough.

speeches.speech: Third, or fourth, or fifth borough, I'll answer him by law. I'll not budge an inch, boy; let him come, and kindly. [Falls asleep] Wind horns. Enter a LORD from hunting, with his train
lines: Third, or fourth, or fifth borough, I'll answer him by law.
I'll not budge an inch, boy; let him come, and kindly.
[Falls asleep]
Wind horns. Enter a LORD from hunting, with his train

speeches.speech: Huntsman, I charge thee, tender well my hounds; Brach Merriman, the poor cur, is emboss'd; And couple Clowder with the deep-mouth'd brach. Saw'st thou not, boy, how Silver made it good At the hedge corner, in the coldest fault? I would not lose the dog for twenty pound.
lines: Huntsman, I charge thee, tender well my hounds;
Brach Merriman, the poor cur, is emboss'd;
And couple Clowder with the deep-mouth'd brach.
Saw'st thou not, boy, how Silver made it good
At the hedge corner, in the coldest fault?
I would not lose the dog for twenty pound.

speeches.speech: Why, Belman is as good as he, my lord; He cried upon it at the merest loss, And twice to-day pick'd out the dullest scent; Trust me, I take him for the better dog.
lines: Why, Belman is as good as he, my lord;
He cried upon it at the merest loss,
And twice to-day pick'd out the dullest scent;
Trust me, I take him for the better dog.

speeches.speech: Thou art a fool; if Echo were as fleet, I would esteem him worth a dozen such. But sup them well, and look unto them all; To-morrow I intend to hunt again.
lines: Thou art a fool; if Echo were as fleet,
I would esteem him worth a dozen such.
But sup them well, and look unto them all;
To-morrow I intend to hunt again.

speeches.speech: I will, my lord.
lines: I will, my lord.

speeches.speech: What's here? One dead, or drunk? See, doth he breathe?
lines: What's here? One dead, or drunk?
See, doth he breathe?

speeches.speech: He breathes, my lord. Were he not warm'd with ale, This were a bed but cold to sleep so soundly.
lines: He breathes, my lord. Were he not warm'd with ale,
This were a bed but cold to sleep so soundly.

speeches.speech: O monstrous beast, how like a swine he lies! Grim death, how foul and loathsome is thine image! Sirs, I will practise on this drunken man. What think you, if he were convey'd to bed, Wrapp'd in sweet clothes, rings put upon his fingers, A most delicious banquet by his bed, And brave attendants near him when he wakes, Would not the beggar then forget himself?
lines: O monstrous beast, how like a swine he lies!
Grim death, how foul and loathsome is thine image!
Sirs, I will practise on this drunken man.
What think you, if he were convey'd to bed,
Wrapp'd in sweet clothes, rings put upon his fingers,
A most delicious banquet by his bed,
And brave attendants near him when he wakes,
Would not the beggar then forget himself?

speeches.speech: Believe me, lord, I think he cannot choose.
lines: Believe me, lord, I think he cannot choose.

speeches.speech: It would seem strange unto him when he wak'd.
lines: It would seem strange unto him when he wak'd.

speeches.speech: Even as a flatt'ring dream or worthless fancy. Then take him up, and manage well the jest: Carry him gently to my fairest chamber, And hang it round with all my wanton pictures; Balm his foul head in warm distilled waters, And burn sweet wood to make the lodging sweet; Procure me music ready when he wakes, To make a dulcet and a heavenly sound; And if he chance to speak, be ready straight, And with a low submissive reverence Say 'What is it your honour will command?' Let one attend him with a silver basin Full of rose-water and bestrew'd with flowers; Another bear the ewer, the third a diaper, And say 'Will't please your lordship cool your hands?' Some one be ready with a costly suit, And ask him what apparel he will wear; Another tell him of his hounds and horse, And that his lady mourns at his disease; Persuade him that he hath been lunatic, And, when he says he is, say that he dreams, For he is nothing but a mighty lord. This do, and do it kindly, gentle sirs; It will be pastime passing excellent, If it be husbanded with modesty.
lines: Even as a flatt'ring dream or worthless fancy.
Then take him up, and manage well the jest:
Carry him gently to my fairest chamber,
And hang it round with all my wanton pictures;
Balm his foul head in warm distilled waters,
And burn sweet wood to make the lodging sweet;
Procure me music ready when he wakes,
To make a dulcet and a heavenly sound;
And if he chance to speak, be ready straight,
And with a low submissive reverence
Say 'What is it your honour will command?'
Let one attend him with a silver basin
Full of rose-water and bestrew'd with flowers;
Another bear the ewer, the third a diaper,
And say 'Will't please your lordship cool your hands?'
Some one be ready with a costly suit,
And ask him what apparel he will wear;
Another tell him of his hounds and horse,
And that his lady mourns at his disease;
Persuade him that he hath been lunatic,
And, when he says he is, say that he dreams,
For he is nothing but a mighty lord.
This do, and do it kindly, gentle sirs;
It will be pastime passing excellent,
If it be husbanded with modesty.

speeches.speech: My lord, I warrant you we will play our part As he shall think by our true diligence He is no less than what we say he is.
lines: My lord, I warrant you we will play our part
As he shall think by our true diligence
He is no less than what we say he is.

speeches.speech: Take him up gently, and to bed with him; And each one to his office when he wakes. [SLY is carried out. A trumpet sounds] Sirrah, go see what trumpet 'tis that sounds- [Exit SERVANT] Belike some noble gentleman that means, Travelling some journey, to repose him here. [Re-enter a SERVINGMAN] How now! who is it?
lines: Take him up gently, and to bed with him;
And each one to his office when he wakes.
[SLY is carried out. A trumpet sounds]
Sirrah, go see what trumpet 'tis that sounds-
[Exit SERVANT]
Belike some noble gentleman that means,
Travelling some journey, to repose him here.
[Re-enter a SERVINGMAN]
How now! who is it?

speeches.speech: An't please your honour, players That offer service to your lordship.
lines: An't please your honour, players
That offer service to your lordship.

speeches.speech: Bid them come near. Now, fellows, you are welcome.
lines: Bid them come near.
Now, fellows, you are welcome.

speeches.speech: We thank your honour.
lines: We thank your honour.

speeches.speech: Do you intend to stay with me to-night?
lines: Do you intend to stay with me to-night?

speeches.speech: So please your lordship to accept our duty.
lines: So please your lordship to accept our duty.

speeches.speech: With all my heart. This fellow I remember Since once he play'd a farmer's eldest son; 'Twas where you woo'd the gentlewoman so well. I have forgot your name; but, sure, that part Was aptly fitted and naturally perform'd.
lines: With all my heart. This fellow I remember
Since once he play'd a farmer's eldest son;
'Twas where you woo'd the gentlewoman so well.
I have forgot your name; but, sure, that part
Was aptly fitted and naturally perform'd.

speeches.speech: I think 'twas Soto that your honour means.
lines: I think 'twas Soto that your honour means.

speeches.speech: 'Tis very true; thou didst it excellent. Well, you are come to me in happy time, The rather for I have some sport in hand Wherein your cunning can assist me much. There is a lord will hear you play to-night; But I am doubtful of your modesties, Lest, over-eying of his odd behaviour, For yet his honour never heard a play, You break into some merry passion And so offend him; for I tell you, sirs, If you should smile, he grows impatient.
lines: 'Tis very true; thou didst it excellent.
Well, you are come to me in happy time,
The rather for I have some sport in hand
Wherein your cunning can assist me much.
There is a lord will hear you play to-night;
But I am doubtful of your modesties,
Lest, over-eying of his odd behaviour,
For yet his honour never heard a play,
You break into some merry passion
And so offend him; for I tell you, sirs,
If you should smile, he grows impatient.

speeches.speech: Fear not, my lord; we can contain ourselves, Were he the veriest antic in the world.
lines: Fear not, my lord; we can contain ourselves,
Were he the veriest antic in the world.

speeches.speech: Go, sirrah, take them to the buttery, And give them friendly welcome every one; Let them want nothing that my house affords. [Exit one with the PLAYERS] Sirrah, go you to Bartholomew my page, And see him dress'd in all suits like a lady; That done, conduct him to the drunkard's chamber, And call him 'madam,' do him obeisance. Tell him from me- as he will win my love- He bear himself with honourable action, Such as he hath observ'd in noble ladies Unto their lords, by them accomplished; Such duty to the drunkard let him do, With soft low tongue and lowly courtesy, And say 'What is't your honour will command, Wherein your lady and your humble wife May show her duty and make known her love?' And then with kind embracements, tempting kisses, And with declining head into his bosom, Bid him shed tears, as being overjoyed To see her noble lord restor'd to health, Who for this seven years hath esteemed him No better than a poor and loathsome beggar. And if the boy have not a woman's gift To rain a shower of commanded tears, An onion will do well for such a shift, Which, in a napkin being close convey'd, Shall in despite enforce a watery eye. See this dispatch'd with all the haste thou canst; Anon I'll give thee more instructions. Exit a SERVINGMAN I know the boy will well usurp the grace, Voice, gait, and action, of a gentlewoman; I long to hear him call the drunkard 'husband'; And how my men will stay themselves from laughter When they do homage to this simple peasant. I'll in to counsel them; haply my presence May well abate the over-merry spleen, Which otherwise would grow into extremes.        Exeunt
lines: Go, sirrah, take them to the buttery,
And give them friendly welcome every one;
Let them want nothing that my house affords.
[Exit one with the PLAYERS]
Sirrah, go you to Bartholomew my page,
And see him dress'd in all suits like a lady;
That done, conduct him to the drunkard's chamber,
And call him 'madam,' do him obeisance.
Tell him from me- as he will win my love-
He bear himself with honourable action,
Such as he hath observ'd in noble ladies
Unto their lords, by them accomplished;
Such duty to the drunkard let him do,
With soft low tongue and lowly courtesy,
And say 'What is't your honour will command,
Wherein your lady and your humble wife
May show her duty and make known her love?'
And then with kind embracements, tempting kisses,
And with declining head into his bosom,
Bid him shed tears, as being overjoyed
To see her noble lord restor'd to health,
Who for this seven years hath esteemed him
No better than a poor and loathsome beggar.
And if the boy have not a woman's gift
To rain a shower of commanded tears,
An onion will do well for such a shift,
Which, in a napkin being close convey'd,
Shall in despite enforce a watery eye.
See this dispatch'd with all the haste thou canst;
Anon I'll give thee more instructions. Exit a SERVINGMAN
I know the boy will well usurp the grace,
Voice, gait, and action, of a gentlewoman;
I long to hear him call the drunkard 'husband';
And how my men will stay themselves from laughter
When they do homage to this simple peasant.
I'll in to counsel them; haply my presence
May well abate the over-merry spleen,
Which otherwise would grow into extremes.        Exeunt

speeches.speech: For God's sake, a pot of small ale.
lines: For God's sake, a pot of small ale.

speeches.speech: Will't please your lordship drink a cup of sack?
lines: Will't please your lordship drink a cup of sack?

speeches.speech: Will't please your honour taste of these conserves?
lines: Will't please your honour taste of these conserves?

speeches.speech: What raiment will your honour wear to-day?
lines: What raiment will your honour wear to-day?

speeches.speech: I am Christophero Sly; call not me 'honour' nor 'lordship.' I ne'er drank sack in my life; and if you give me any conserves, give me conserves of beef. Ne'er ask me what raiment I'll wear, for I have no more doublets than backs, no more stockings than legs, nor no more shoes than feet- nay, sometime more feet than shoes, or such shoes as my toes look through the overleather.
lines: I am Christophero Sly; call not me 'honour' nor 'lordship.' I
ne'er drank sack in my life; and if you give me any conserves,
give me conserves of beef. Ne'er ask me what raiment I'll wear,
for I have no more doublets than backs, no more stockings than
legs, nor no more shoes than feet- nay, sometime more feet than
shoes, or such shoes as my toes look through the overleather.

speeches.speech: Heaven cease this idle humour in your honour! O, that a mighty man of such descent, Of such possessions, and so high esteem, Should be infused with so foul a spirit!
lines: Heaven cease this idle humour in your honour!
O, that a mighty man of such descent,
Of such possessions, and so high esteem,
Should be infused with so foul a spirit!

speeches.speech: What, would you make me mad? Am not I Christopher Sly, old Sly's son of Burton Heath; by birth a pedlar, by education a cardmaker, by transmutation a bear-herd, and now by present profession a tinker? Ask Marian Hacket, the fat ale-wife of Wincot, if she know me not; if she say I am not fourteen pence on the score for sheer ale, score me up for the lying'st knave in Christendom. What! I am not bestraught. [Taking a pot of ale] Here's-
lines: What, would you make me mad? Am not I Christopher Sly, old
Sly's son of Burton Heath; by birth a pedlar, by education a
cardmaker, by transmutation a bear-herd, and now by present
profession a tinker? Ask Marian Hacket, the fat ale-wife of
Wincot, if she know me not; if she say I am not fourteen pence on
the score for sheer ale, score me up for the lying'st knave in
Christendom. What! I am not bestraught. [Taking a pot of ale]
Here's-

speeches.speech: O, this it is that makes your lady mourn!
lines: O, this it is that makes your lady mourn!

speeches.speech: O, this is it that makes your servants droop!
lines: O, this is it that makes your servants droop!

speeches.speech: Hence comes it that your kindred shuns your house, As beaten hence by your strange lunacy. O noble lord, bethink thee of thy birth! Call home thy ancient thoughts from banishment, And banish hence these abject lowly dreams. Look how thy servants do attend on thee, Each in his office ready at thy beck. Wilt thou have music? Hark! Apollo plays,            [Music] And twenty caged nightingales do sing. Or wilt thou sleep? We'll have thee to a couch Softer and sweeter than the lustful bed On purpose trimm'd up for Semiramis. Say thou wilt walk: we will bestrew the ground. Or wilt thou ride? Thy horses shall be trapp'd, Their harness studded all with gold and pearl. Dost thou love hawking? Thou hast hawks will soar Above the morning lark. Or wilt thou hunt? Thy hounds shall make the welkin answer them And fetch shall echoes from the hollow earth.
lines: Hence comes it that your kindred shuns your house,
As beaten hence by your strange lunacy.
O noble lord, bethink thee of thy birth!
Call home thy ancient thoughts from banishment,
And banish hence these abject lowly dreams.
Look how thy servants do attend on thee,
Each in his office ready at thy beck.
Wilt thou have music? Hark! Apollo plays,            [Music]
And twenty caged nightingales do sing.
Or wilt thou sleep? We'll have thee to a couch
Softer and sweeter than the lustful bed
On purpose trimm'd up for Semiramis.
Say thou wilt walk: we will bestrew the ground.
Or wilt thou ride? Thy horses shall be trapp'd,
Their harness studded all with gold and pearl.
Dost thou love hawking? Thou hast hawks will soar
Above the morning lark. Or wilt thou hunt?
Thy hounds shall make the welkin answer them
And fetch shall echoes from the hollow earth.

speeches.speech: Say thou wilt course; thy greyhounds are as swift As breathed stags; ay, fleeter than the roe.
lines: Say thou wilt course; thy greyhounds are as swift
As breathed stags; ay, fleeter than the roe.

speeches.speech: Dost thou love pictures? We will fetch thee straight Adonis painted by a running brook, And Cytherea all in sedges hid, Which seem to move and wanton with her breath Even as the waving sedges play wi' th' wind.
lines: Dost thou love pictures? We will fetch thee
straight
Adonis painted by a running brook,
And Cytherea all in sedges hid,
Which seem to move and wanton with her breath
Even as the waving sedges play wi' th' wind.

speeches.speech: We'll show thee Io as she was a maid And how she was beguiled and surpris'd, As lively painted as the deed was done.
lines: We'll show thee Io as she was a maid
And how she was beguiled and surpris'd,
As lively painted as the deed was done.

speeches.speech: Or Daphne roaming through a thorny wood, Scratching her legs, that one shall swear she bleeds And at that sight shall sad Apollo weep, So workmanly the blood and tears are drawn.
lines: Or Daphne roaming through a thorny wood,
Scratching her legs, that one shall swear she bleeds
And at that sight shall sad Apollo weep,
So workmanly the blood and tears are drawn.

speeches.speech: Thou art a lord, and nothing but a lord. Thou hast a lady far more beautiful Than any woman in this waning age.
lines: Thou art a lord, and nothing but a lord.
Thou hast a lady far more beautiful
Than any woman in this waning age.

speeches.speech: And, till the tears that she hath shed for thee Like envious floods o'er-run her lovely face, She was the fairest creature in the world; And yet she is inferior to none.
lines: And, till the tears that she hath shed for thee
Like envious floods o'er-run her lovely face,
She was the fairest creature in the world;
And yet she is inferior to none.

speeches.speech: Am I a lord and have I such a lady? Or do I dream? Or have I dream'd till now? I do not sleep: I see, I hear, I speak; I smell sweet savours, and I feel soft things. Upon my life, I am a lord indeed, And not a tinker, nor Christopher Sly. Well, bring our lady hither to our sight; And once again, a pot o' th' smallest ale.
lines: Am I a lord and have I such a lady?
Or do I dream? Or have I dream'd till now?
I do not sleep: I see, I hear, I speak;
I smell sweet savours, and I feel soft things.
Upon my life, I am a lord indeed,
And not a tinker, nor Christopher Sly.
Well, bring our lady hither to our sight;
And once again, a pot o' th' smallest ale.

speeches.speech: Will't please your Mightiness to wash your hands? O, how we joy to see your wit restor'd! O, that once more you knew but what you are! These fifteen years you have been in a dream; Or, when you wak'd, so wak'd as if you slept.
lines: Will't please your Mightiness to wash your hands?
O, how we joy to see your wit restor'd!
O, that once more you knew but what you are!
These fifteen years you have been in a dream;
Or, when you wak'd, so wak'd as if you slept.

speeches.speech: These fifteen years! by my fay, a goodly nap. But did I never speak of all that time?
lines: These fifteen years! by my fay, a goodly nap.
But did I never speak of all that time?

speeches.speech: O, yes, my lord, but very idle words; For though you lay here in this goodly chamber, Yet would you say ye were beaten out of door; And rail upon the hostess of the house, And say you would present her at the leet, Because she brought stone jugs and no seal'd quarts. Sometimes you would call out for Cicely Hacket.
lines: O, yes, my lord, but very idle words;
For though you lay here in this goodly chamber,
Yet would you say ye were beaten out of door;
And rail upon the hostess of the house,
And say you would present her at the leet,
Because she brought stone jugs and no seal'd quarts.
Sometimes you would call out for Cicely Hacket.

speeches.speech: Ay, the woman's maid of the house.
lines: Ay, the woman's maid of the house.

speeches.speech: Why, sir, you know no house nor no such maid, Nor no such men as you have reckon'd up, As Stephen Sly, and old John Naps of Greece, And Peter Turph, and Henry Pimpernell; And twenty more such names and men as these, Which never were, nor no man ever saw.
lines: Why, sir, you know no house nor no such maid,
Nor no such men as you have reckon'd up,
As Stephen Sly, and old John Naps of Greece,
And Peter Turph, and Henry Pimpernell;
And twenty more such names and men as these,
Which never were, nor no man ever saw.

speeches.speech: Now, Lord be thanked for my good amends!
lines: Now, Lord be thanked for my good amends!

speeches.speech: Amen.
lines: Amen.

speeches.speech: I thank thee; thou shalt not lose by it.
lines: I thank thee; thou shalt not lose by it.

speeches.speech: How fares my noble lord?
lines: How fares my noble lord?

speeches.speech: Marry, I fare well; for here is cheer enough. Where is my wife?
lines: Marry, I fare well; for here is cheer enough.
Where is my wife?

speeches.speech: Here, noble lord; what is thy will with her?
lines: Here, noble lord; what is thy will with her?

speeches.speech: Are you my wife, and will not call me husband? My men should call me 'lord'; I am your goodman.
lines: Are you my wife, and will not call me husband?
My men should call me 'lord'; I am your goodman.

speeches.speech: My husband and my lord, my lord and husband; I am your wife in all obedience.
lines: My husband and my lord, my lord and husband;
I am your wife in all obedience.

speeches.speech: I know it well. What must I call her?
lines: I know it well. What must I call her?

speeches.speech: Madam.
lines: Madam.

speeches.speech: Al'ce madam, or Joan madam?
lines: Al'ce madam, or Joan madam?

speeches.speech: Madam, and nothing else; so lords call ladies.
lines: Madam, and nothing else; so lords call ladies.

speeches.speech: Madam wife, they say that I have dream'd And slept above some fifteen year or more.
lines: Madam wife, they say that I have dream'd
And slept above some fifteen year or more.

speeches.speech: Ay, and the time seems thirty unto me, Being all this time abandon'd from your bed.
lines: Ay, and the time seems thirty unto me,
Being all this time abandon'd from your bed.

speeches.speech: 'Tis much. Servants, leave me and her alone. [Exeunt SERVANTS] Madam, undress you, and come now to bed.
lines: 'Tis much. Servants, leave me and her alone.
[Exeunt SERVANTS]
Madam, undress you, and come now to bed.

speeches.speech: Thrice noble lord, let me entreat of you To pardon me yet for a night or two; Or, if not so, until the sun be set. For your physicians have expressly charg'd, In peril to incur your former malady, That I should yet absent me from your bed. I hope this reason stands for my excuse.
lines: Thrice noble lord, let me entreat of you
To pardon me yet for a night or two;
Or, if not so, until the sun be set.
For your physicians have expressly charg'd,
In peril to incur your former malady,
That I should yet absent me from your bed.
I hope this reason stands for my excuse.

speeches.speech: Ay, it stands so that I may hardly tarry so long. But I would be loath to fall into my dreams again. I will therefore tarry in despite of the flesh and the blood.
lines: Ay, it stands so that I may hardly tarry so long. But I would be
loath to fall into my dreams again. I will therefore tarry in
despite of the flesh and the blood.

speeches.speech: Your honour's players, hearing your amendment, Are come to play a pleasant comedy; For so your doctors hold it very meet, Seeing too much sadness hath congeal'd your blood, And melancholy is the nurse of frenzy. Therefore they thought it good you hear a play And frame your mind to mirth and merriment, Which bars a thousand harms and lengthens life.
lines: Your honour's players, hearing your amendment,
Are come to play a pleasant comedy;
For so your doctors hold it very meet,
Seeing too much sadness hath congeal'd your blood,
And melancholy is the nurse of frenzy.
Therefore they thought it good you hear a play
And frame your mind to mirth and merriment,
Which bars a thousand harms and lengthens life.

speeches.speech: Marry, I will; let them play it. Is not a comonty a Christmas gambold or a tumbling-trick?
lines: Marry, I will; let them play it. Is not a comonty a
Christmas gambold or a tumbling-trick?

speeches.speech: No, my good lord, it is more pleasing stuff.
lines: No, my good lord, it is more pleasing stuff.

speeches.speech: What, household stuff?
lines: What, household stuff?

speeches.speech: It is a kind of history.
lines: It is a kind of history.

speeches.speech: Well, we'll see't. Come, madam wife, sit by my side and let the world slip;-we shall ne'er be younger.
lines: Well, we'll see't. Come, madam wife, sit by my side and let
the world slip;-we shall ne'er be younger.

speeches.speech: Tranio, since for the great desire I had To see fair Padua, nursery of arts, I am arriv'd for fruitful Lombardy, The pleasant garden of great Italy, And by my father's love and leave am arm'd With his good will and thy good company, My trusty servant well approv'd in all, Here let us breathe, and haply institute A course of learning and ingenious studies. Pisa, renowned for grave citizens, Gave me my being and my father first, A merchant of great traffic through the world, Vincentio, come of the Bentivolii; Vincentio's son, brought up in Florence, It shall become to serve all hopes conceiv'd, To deck his fortune with his virtuous deeds. And therefore, Tranio, for the time I study, Virtue and that part of philosophy Will I apply that treats of happiness By virtue specially to be achiev'd. Tell me thy mind; for I have Pisa left And am to Padua come as he that leaves A shallow plash to plunge him in the deep, And with satiety seeks to quench his thirst.
lines: Tranio, since for the great desire I had
To see fair Padua, nursery of arts,
I am arriv'd for fruitful Lombardy,
The pleasant garden of great Italy,
And by my father's love and leave am arm'd
With his good will and thy good company,
My trusty servant well approv'd in all,
Here let us breathe, and haply institute
A course of learning and ingenious studies.
Pisa, renowned for grave citizens,
Gave me my being and my father first,
A merchant of great traffic through the world,
Vincentio, come of the Bentivolii;
Vincentio's son, brought up in Florence,
It shall become to serve all hopes conceiv'd,
To deck his fortune with his virtuous deeds.
And therefore, Tranio, for the time I study,
Virtue and that part of philosophy
Will I apply that treats of happiness
By virtue specially to be achiev'd.
Tell me thy mind; for I have Pisa left
And am to Padua come as he that leaves
A shallow plash to plunge him in the deep,
And with satiety seeks to quench his thirst.

speeches.speech: Mi perdonato, gentle master mine; I am in all affected as yourself; Glad that you thus continue your resolve To suck the sweets of sweet philosophy. Only, good master, while we do admire This virtue and this moral discipline, Let's be no Stoics nor no stocks, I pray, Or so devote to Aristotle's checks As Ovid be an outcast quite abjur'd. Balk logic with acquaintance that you have, And practise rhetoric in your common talk; Music and poesy use to quicken you; The mathematics and the metaphysics, Fall to them as you find your stomach serves you. No profit grows where is no pleasure ta'en; In brief, sir, study what you most affect.
lines: Mi perdonato, gentle master mine;
I am in all affected as yourself;
Glad that you thus continue your resolve
To suck the sweets of sweet philosophy.
Only, good master, while we do admire
This virtue and this moral discipline,
Let's be no Stoics nor no stocks, I pray,
Or so devote to Aristotle's checks
As Ovid be an outcast quite abjur'd.
Balk logic with acquaintance that you have,
And practise rhetoric in your common talk;
Music and poesy use to quicken you;
The mathematics and the metaphysics,
Fall to them as you find your stomach serves you.
No profit grows where is no pleasure ta'en;
In brief, sir, study what you most affect.

speeches.speech: Gramercies, Tranio, well dost thou advise. If, Biondello, thou wert come ashore, We could at once put us in readiness, And take a lodging fit to entertain Such friends as time in Padua shall beget. Enter BAPTISTA with his two daughters, KATHERINA and BIANCA; GREMIO, a pantaloon; HORTENSIO, suitor to BIANCA. LUCENTIO and TRANIO stand by But stay awhile; what company is this?
lines: Gramercies, Tranio, well dost thou advise.
If, Biondello, thou wert come ashore,
We could at once put us in readiness,
And take a lodging fit to entertain
Such friends as time in Padua shall beget.
Enter BAPTISTA with his two daughters, KATHERINA
and BIANCA; GREMIO, a pantaloon; HORTENSIO,
suitor to BIANCA. LUCENTIO and TRANIO stand by
But stay awhile; what company is this?

speeches.speech: Master, some show to welcome us to town.
lines: Master, some show to welcome us to town.

speeches.speech: Gentlemen, importune me no farther, For how I firmly am resolv'd you know; That is, not to bestow my youngest daughter Before I have a husband for the elder. If either of you both love Katherina, Because I know you well and love you well, Leave shall you have to court her at your pleasure.
lines: Gentlemen, importune me no farther,
For how I firmly am resolv'd you know;
That is, not to bestow my youngest daughter
Before I have a husband for the elder.
If either of you both love Katherina,
Because I know you well and love you well,
Leave shall you have to court her at your pleasure.

speeches.speech: To cart her rather. She's too rough for me. There, there, Hortensio, will you any wife?
lines: To cart her rather. She's too rough for me.
There, there, Hortensio, will you any wife?

speeches.speech: [To BAPTISTA]  I pray you, sir, is it your will To make a stale of me amongst these mates?
lines: [To BAPTISTA]  I pray you, sir, is it your will
To make a stale of me amongst these mates?

speeches.speech: Mates, maid! How mean you that? No mates for you, Unless you were of gentler, milder mould.
lines: Mates, maid! How mean you that? No mates for you,
Unless you were of gentler, milder mould.

speeches.speech: I' faith, sir, you shall never need to fear; Iwis it is not halfway to her heart; But if it were, doubt not her care should be To comb your noddle with a three-legg'd stool, And paint your face, and use you like a fool.
lines: I' faith, sir, you shall never need to fear;
Iwis it is not halfway to her heart;
But if it were, doubt not her care should be
To comb your noddle with a three-legg'd stool,
And paint your face, and use you like a fool.

speeches.speech: From all such devils, good Lord deliver us!
lines: From all such devils, good Lord deliver us!

speeches.speech: And me, too, good Lord!
lines: And me, too, good Lord!

speeches.speech: Husht, master! Here's some good pastime toward; That wench is stark mad or wonderful froward.
lines: Husht, master! Here's some good pastime toward;
That wench is stark mad or wonderful froward.

speeches.speech: But in the other's silence do I see Maid's mild behaviour and sobriety. Peace, Tranio!
lines: But in the other's silence do I see
Maid's mild behaviour and sobriety.
Peace, Tranio!

speeches.speech: Well said, master; mum! and gaze your fill.
lines: Well said, master; mum! and gaze your fill.

speeches.speech: Gentlemen, that I may soon make good What I have said- Bianca, get you in; And let it not displease thee, good Bianca, For I will love thee ne'er the less, my girl.
lines: Gentlemen, that I may soon make good
What I have said- Bianca, get you in;
And let it not displease thee, good Bianca,
For I will love thee ne'er the less, my girl.

speeches.speech: A pretty peat! it is best Put finger in the eye, an she knew why.
lines: A pretty peat! it is best
Put finger in the eye, an she knew why.

speeches.speech: Sister, content you in my discontent. Sir, to your pleasure humbly I subscribe; My books and instruments shall be my company, On them to look, and practise by myself.
lines: Sister, content you in my discontent.
Sir, to your pleasure humbly I subscribe;
My books and instruments shall be my company,
On them to look, and practise by myself.

speeches.speech: Hark, Tranio, thou mayst hear Minerva speak!
lines: Hark, Tranio, thou mayst hear Minerva speak!

speeches.speech: Signior Baptista, will you be so strange? Sorry am I that our good will effects Bianca's grief.
lines: Signior Baptista, will you be so strange?
Sorry am I that our good will effects
Bianca's grief.

speeches.speech: Why will you mew her up, Signior Baptista, for this fiend of hell, And make her bear the penance of her tongue?
lines: Why will you mew her up,
Signior Baptista, for this fiend of hell,
And make her bear the penance of her tongue?

speeches.speech: Gentlemen, content ye; I am resolv'd. Go in, Bianca.                              Exit BIANCA And for I know she taketh most delight In music, instruments, and poetry, Schoolmasters will I keep within my house Fit to instruct her youth. If you, Hortensio, Or, Signior Gremio, you, know any such, Prefer them hither; for to cunning men I will be very kind, and liberal To mine own children in good bringing-up; And so, farewell. Katherina, you may stay; For I have more to commune with Bianca.            Exit
lines: Gentlemen, content ye; I am resolv'd.
Go in, Bianca.                              Exit BIANCA
And for I know she taketh most delight
In music, instruments, and poetry,
Schoolmasters will I keep within my house
Fit to instruct her youth. If you, Hortensio,
Or, Signior Gremio, you, know any such,
Prefer them hither; for to cunning men
I will be very kind, and liberal
To mine own children in good bringing-up;
And so, farewell. Katherina, you may stay;
For I have more to commune with Bianca.            Exit

speeches.speech: Why, and I trust I may go too, may I not? What! shall I be appointed hours, as though, belike, I knew not what to take and what to leave? Ha!          Exit
lines: Why, and I trust I may go too, may I not?
What! shall I be appointed hours, as though, belike,
I knew not what to take and what to leave? Ha!          Exit

speeches.speech: You may go to the devil's dam; your gifts are so good here's none will hold you. There! Love is not so great, Hortensio, but we may blow our nails together, and fast it fairly out; our cake's dough on both sides. Farewell; yet, for the love I bear my sweet Bianca, if I can by any means light on a fit man to teach her that wherein she delights, I will wish him to her father.
lines: You may go to the devil's dam; your gifts are so good
here's none will hold you. There! Love is not so great,
Hortensio, but we may blow our nails together, and fast it fairly
out; our cake's dough on both sides. Farewell; yet, for the love
I bear my sweet Bianca, if I can by any means light on a fit man
to teach her that wherein she delights, I will wish him to her
father.

speeches.speech: So Will I, Signior Gremio; but a word, I pray. Though the nature of our quarrel yet never brook'd parle, know now, upon advice, it toucheth us both- that we may yet again have access to our fair mistress, and be happy rivals in Bianca's love- to labour and effect one thing specially.
lines: So Will I, Signior Gremio; but a word, I pray. Though
the nature of our quarrel yet never brook'd parle, know now, upon
advice, it toucheth us both- that we may yet again have access to
our fair mistress, and be happy rivals in Bianca's love- to
labour and effect one thing specially.

speeches.speech: What's that, I pray?
lines: What's that, I pray?

speeches.speech: Marry, sir, to get a husband for her sister.
lines: Marry, sir, to get a husband for her sister.

speeches.speech: A husband? a devil.
lines: A husband? a devil.

speeches.speech: I say a husband.
lines: I say a husband.

speeches.speech: I say a devil. Think'st thou, Hortensio, though her father be very rich, any man is so very a fool to be married to hell?
lines: I say a devil. Think'st thou, Hortensio, though her father
be very rich, any man is so very a fool to be married to hell?

speeches.speech: Tush, Gremio! Though it pass your patience and mine to endure her loud alarums, why, man, there be good fellows in the world, an a man could light on them, would take her with all faults, and money enough.
lines: Tush, Gremio! Though it pass your patience and mine to
endure her loud alarums, why, man, there be good fellows in the
world, an a man could light on them, would take her with all
faults, and money enough.

speeches.speech: I cannot tell; but I had as lief take her dowry with this condition: to be whipp'd at the high cross every morning.
lines: I cannot tell; but I had as lief take her dowry with this
condition: to be whipp'd at the high cross every morning.

speeches.speech: Faith, as you say, there's small choice in rotten apples. But, come; since this bar in law makes us friends, it shall be so far forth friendly maintain'd till by helping Baptista's eldest daughter to a husband we set his youngest free for a husband, and then have to't afresh. Sweet Bianca! Happy man be his dole! He that runs fastest gets the ring. How say you, Signior Gremio?
lines: Faith, as you say, there's small choice in rotten
apples. But, come; since this bar in law makes us friends, it
shall be so far forth friendly maintain'd till by helping
Baptista's eldest daughter to a husband we set his youngest free
for a husband, and then have to't afresh. Sweet Bianca! Happy man
be his dole! He that runs fastest gets the ring. How say you,
Signior Gremio?

speeches.speech: I am agreed; and would I had given him the best horse in Padua to begin his wooing that would thoroughly woo her, wed her, and bed her, and rid the house of her! Come on.
lines: I am agreed; and would I had given him the best horse in
Padua to begin his wooing that would thoroughly woo her, wed her,
and bed her, and rid the house of her! Come on.

speeches.speech: I pray, sir, tell me, is it possible That love should of a sudden take such hold?
lines: I pray, sir, tell me, is it possible
That love should of a sudden take such hold?

speeches.speech: O Tranio, till I found it to be true, I never thought it possible or likely. But see! while idly I stood looking on, I found the effect of love in idleness; And now in plainness do confess to thee, That art to me as secret and as dear As Anna to the Queen of Carthage was- Tranio, I burn, I pine, I perish, Tranio, If I achieve not this young modest girl. Counsel me, Tranio, for I know thou canst; Assist me, Tranio, for I know thou wilt.
lines: O Tranio, till I found it to be true,
I never thought it possible or likely.
But see! while idly I stood looking on,
I found the effect of love in idleness;
And now in plainness do confess to thee,
That art to me as secret and as dear
As Anna to the Queen of Carthage was-
Tranio, I burn, I pine, I perish, Tranio,
If I achieve not this young modest girl.
Counsel me, Tranio, for I know thou canst;
Assist me, Tranio, for I know thou wilt.

speeches.speech: Master, it is no time to chide you now; Affection is not rated from the heart; If love have touch'd you, nought remains but so: 'Redime te captum quam queas minimo.'
lines: Master, it is no time to chide you now;
Affection is not rated from the heart;
If love have touch'd you, nought remains but so:
'Redime te captum quam queas minimo.'

speeches.speech: Gramercies, lad. Go forward; this contents; The rest will comfort, for thy counsel's sound.
lines: Gramercies, lad. Go forward; this contents;
The rest will comfort, for thy counsel's sound.

speeches.speech: Master, you look'd so longly on the maid. Perhaps you mark'd not what's the pith of all.
lines: Master, you look'd so longly on the maid.
Perhaps you mark'd not what's the pith of all.

speeches.speech: O, yes, I saw sweet beauty in her face, Such as the daughter of Agenor had, That made great Jove to humble him to her hand, When with his knees he kiss'd the Cretan strand.
lines: O, yes, I saw sweet beauty in her face,
Such as the daughter of Agenor had,
That made great Jove to humble him to her hand,
When with his knees he kiss'd the Cretan strand.

speeches.speech: Saw you no more? Mark'd you not how her sister Began to scold and raise up such a storm That mortal ears might hardly endure the din?
lines: Saw you no more? Mark'd you not how her sister
Began to scold and raise up such a storm
That mortal ears might hardly endure the din?

speeches.speech: Tranio, I saw her coral lips to move, And with her breath she did perfume the air; Sacred and sweet was all I saw in her.
lines: Tranio, I saw her coral lips to move,
And with her breath she did perfume the air;
Sacred and sweet was all I saw in her.

speeches.speech: Nay, then 'tis time to stir him from his trance. I pray, awake, sir. If you love the maid, Bend thoughts and wits to achieve her. Thus it stands: Her elder sister is so curst and shrewd That, till the father rid his hands of her, Master, your love must live a maid at home; And therefore has he closely mew'd her up, Because she will not be annoy'd with suitors.
lines: Nay, then 'tis time to stir him from his trance.
I pray, awake, sir. If you love the maid,
Bend thoughts and wits to achieve her. Thus it stands:
Her elder sister is so curst and shrewd
That, till the father rid his hands of her,
Master, your love must live a maid at home;
And therefore has he closely mew'd her up,
Because she will not be annoy'd with suitors.

speeches.speech: Ah, Tranio, what a cruel father's he! But art thou not advis'd he took some care To get her cunning schoolmasters to instruct her?
lines: Ah, Tranio, what a cruel father's he!
But art thou not advis'd he took some care
To get her cunning schoolmasters to instruct her?

speeches.speech: Ay, marry, am I, sir, and now 'tis plotted.
lines: Ay, marry, am I, sir, and now 'tis plotted.

speeches.speech: I have it, Tranio.
lines: I have it, Tranio.

speeches.speech: Master, for my hand, Both our inventions meet and jump in one.
lines: Master, for my hand,
Both our inventions meet and jump in one.

speeches.speech: Tell me thine first.
lines: Tell me thine first.

speeches.speech: You will be schoolmaster, And undertake the teaching of the maid- That's your device.
lines: You will be schoolmaster,
And undertake the teaching of the maid-
That's your device.

speeches.speech: It is. May it be done?
lines: It is. May it be done?

speeches.speech: Not possible; for who shall bear your part And be in Padua here Vincentio's son; Keep house and ply his book, welcome his friends, Visit his countrymen, and banquet them?
lines: Not possible; for who shall bear your part
And be in Padua here Vincentio's son;
Keep house and ply his book, welcome his friends,
Visit his countrymen, and banquet them?

speeches.speech: Basta, content thee, for I have it full. We have not yet been seen in any house, Nor can we be distinguish'd by our faces For man or master. Then it follows thus: Thou shalt be master, Tranio, in my stead, Keep house and port and servants, as I should; I will some other be- some Florentine, Some Neapolitan, or meaner man of Pisa. 'Tis hatch'd, and shall be so. Tranio, at once Uncase thee; take my colour'd hat and cloak. When Biondello comes, he waits on thee; But I will charm him first to keep his tongue.
lines: Basta, content thee, for I have it full.
We have not yet been seen in any house,
Nor can we be distinguish'd by our faces
For man or master. Then it follows thus:
Thou shalt be master, Tranio, in my stead,
Keep house and port and servants, as I should;
I will some other be- some Florentine,
Some Neapolitan, or meaner man of Pisa.
'Tis hatch'd, and shall be so. Tranio, at once
Uncase thee; take my colour'd hat and cloak.
When Biondello comes, he waits on thee;
But I will charm him first to keep his tongue.

speeches.speech: So had you need.           [They exchange habits] In brief, sir, sith it your pleasure is, And I am tied to be obedient- For so your father charg'd me at our parting: 'Be serviceable to my son' quoth he, Although I think 'twas in another sense- I am content to be Lucentio, Because so well I love Lucentio.
lines: So had you need.           [They exchange habits]
In brief, sir, sith it your pleasure is,
And I am tied to be obedient-
For so your father charg'd me at our parting:
'Be serviceable to my son' quoth he,
Although I think 'twas in another sense-
I am content to be Lucentio,
Because so well I love Lucentio.

speeches.speech: Tranio, be so because Lucentio loves; And let me be a slave t' achieve that maid Whose sudden sight hath thrall'd my wounded eye. [Enter BIONDELLO.] Here comes the rogue. Sirrah, where have you been?
lines: Tranio, be so because Lucentio loves;
And let me be a slave t' achieve that maid
Whose sudden sight hath thrall'd my wounded eye.
[Enter BIONDELLO.]
Here comes the rogue. Sirrah, where have you been?

speeches.speech: Where have I been! Nay, how now! where are you? Master, has my fellow Tranio stol'n your clothes? Or you stol'n his? or both? Pray, what's the news?
lines: Where have I been! Nay, how now! where are you?
Master, has my fellow Tranio stol'n your clothes?
Or you stol'n his? or both? Pray, what's the news?

speeches.speech: Sirrah, come hither; 'tis no time to jest, And therefore frame your manners to the time. Your fellow Tranio here, to save my life, Puts my apparel and my count'nance on, And I for my escape have put on his; For in a quarrel since I came ashore I kill'd a man, and fear I was descried. Wait you on him, I charge you, as becomes, While I make way from hence to save my life. You understand me?
lines: Sirrah, come hither; 'tis no time to jest,
And therefore frame your manners to the time.
Your fellow Tranio here, to save my life,
Puts my apparel and my count'nance on,
And I for my escape have put on his;
For in a quarrel since I came ashore
I kill'd a man, and fear I was descried.
Wait you on him, I charge you, as becomes,
While I make way from hence to save my life.
You understand me?

speeches.speech: I, sir? Ne'er a whit.
lines: I, sir? Ne'er a whit.

speeches.speech: And not a jot of Tranio in your mouth: Tranio is chang'd into Lucentio.
lines: And not a jot of Tranio in your mouth:
Tranio is chang'd into Lucentio.

speeches.speech: The better for him; would I were so too!
lines: The better for him; would I were so too!

speeches.speech: So could I, faith, boy, to have the next wish after, That Lucentio indeed had Baptista's youngest daughter. But, sirrah, not for my sake but your master's, I advise You use your manners discreetly in all kind of companies. When I am alone, why, then I am Tranio; But in all places else your master Lucentio.
lines: So could I, faith, boy, to have the next wish after,
That Lucentio indeed had Baptista's youngest daughter.
But, sirrah, not for my sake but your master's, I advise
You use your manners discreetly in all kind of companies.
When I am alone, why, then I am Tranio;
But in all places else your master Lucentio.

speeches.speech: Tranio, let's go. One thing more rests, that thyself execute- To make one among these wooers. If thou ask me why- Sufficeth, my reasons are both good and weighty. Exeunt.
lines: Tranio, let's go.
One thing more rests, that thyself execute-
To make one among these wooers. If thou ask me why-
Sufficeth, my reasons are both good and weighty. Exeunt.

speeches.speech: My lord, you nod; you do not mind the play.
lines: My lord, you nod; you do not mind the play.

speeches.speech: Yes, by Saint Anne do I. A good matter, surely; comes there any more of it?
lines: Yes, by Saint Anne do I. A good matter, surely; comes there
any more of it?

speeches.speech: My lord, 'tis but begun.
lines: My lord, 'tis but begun.

speeches.speech: 'Tis a very excellent piece of work, madam lady Would 'twere done!                        [They sit and mark]
lines: 'Tis a very excellent piece of work, madam lady
Would 'twere done!                        [They sit and mark]

speeches.speech: Verona, for a while I take my leave, To see my friends in Padua; but of all My best beloved and approved friend, Hortensio; and I trow this is his house. Here, sirrah Grumio, knock, I say.
lines: Verona, for a while I take my leave,
To see my friends in Padua; but of all
My best beloved and approved friend,
Hortensio; and I trow this is his house.
Here, sirrah Grumio, knock, I say.

speeches.speech: Knock, sir! Whom should I knock? Is there any man has rebus'd your worship?
lines: Knock, sir! Whom should I knock?
Is there any man has rebus'd your worship?

speeches.speech: Villain, I say, knock me here soundly.
lines: Villain, I say, knock me here soundly.

speeches.speech: Knock you here, sir? Why, sir, what am I, sir, that I should knock you here, sir?
lines: Knock you here, sir? Why, sir, what am I, sir, that I
should knock you here, sir?

speeches.speech: Villain, I say, knock me at this gate, And rap me well, or I'll knock your knave's pate.
lines: Villain, I say, knock me at this gate,
And rap me well, or I'll knock your knave's pate.

speeches.speech: My master is grown quarrelsome. I should knock you first, And then I know after who comes by the worst.
lines: My master is grown quarrelsome. I should knock you first,
And then I know after who comes by the worst.

speeches.speech: Will it not be? Faith, sirrah, an you'll not knock I'll ring it; I'll try how you can sol-fa, and sing it.
lines: Will it not be?
Faith, sirrah, an you'll not knock I'll ring it;
I'll try how you can sol-fa, and sing it.

speeches.speech: Help, masters, help! My master is mad.
lines: Help, masters, help! My master is mad.

speeches.speech: Now knock when I bid you, sirrah villain!
lines: Now knock when I bid you, sirrah villain!

speeches.speech: How now! what's the matter? My old friend Grumio and my good friend Petruchio! How do you all at Verona?
lines: How now! what's the matter? My old friend Grumio and my
good friend Petruchio! How do you all at Verona?

speeches.speech: Signior Hortensio, come you to part the fray? 'Con tutto il cuore ben trovato' may I say.
lines: Signior Hortensio, come you to part the fray?
'Con tutto il cuore ben trovato' may I say.

speeches.speech: Alla nostra casa ben venuto, Molto honorato signor mio Petruchio. Rise, Grumio, rise; we will compound this quarrel.
lines: Alla nostra casa ben venuto,
Molto honorato signor mio Petruchio.
Rise, Grumio, rise; we will compound this quarrel.

speeches.speech: Nay, 'tis no matter, sir, what he 'leges in Latin. If this be not a lawful cause for me to leave his service- look you, sir: he bid me knock him and rap him soundly, sir. Well, was it fit for a servant to use his master so; being, perhaps, for aught I see, two and thirty, a pip out? Whom would to God I had well knock'd at first, Then had not Grumio come by the worst.
lines: Nay, 'tis no matter, sir, what he 'leges in Latin. If this
be not a lawful cause for me to leave his service- look you, sir:
he bid me knock him and rap him soundly, sir. Well, was it fit
for a servant to use his master so; being, perhaps, for aught I
see, two and thirty, a pip out?
Whom would to God I had well knock'd at first,
Then had not Grumio come by the worst.

speeches.speech: A senseless villain! Good Hortensio, I bade the rascal knock upon your gate, And could not get him for my heart to do it.
lines: A senseless villain! Good Hortensio,
I bade the rascal knock upon your gate,
And could not get him for my heart to do it.

speeches.speech: Knock at the gate? O heavens! Spake you not these words plain: 'Sirrah knock me here, rap me here, knock me well, and knock me soundly'? And come you now with 'knocking at the gate'?
lines: Knock at the gate? O heavens! Spake you not these words
plain: 'Sirrah knock me here, rap me here, knock me well, and
knock me soundly'? And come you now with 'knocking at the gate'?

speeches.speech: Sirrah, be gone, or talk not, I advise you.
lines: Sirrah, be gone, or talk not, I advise you.

speeches.speech: Petruchio, patience; I am Grumio's pledge; Why, this's a heavy chance 'twixt him and you, Your ancient, trusty, pleasant servant Grumio. And tell me now, sweet friend, what happy gale Blows you to Padua here from old Verona?
lines: Petruchio, patience; I am Grumio's pledge;
Why, this's a heavy chance 'twixt him and you,
Your ancient, trusty, pleasant servant Grumio.
And tell me now, sweet friend, what happy gale
Blows you to Padua here from old Verona?

speeches.speech: Such wind as scatters young men through the world To seek their fortunes farther than at home, Where small experience grows. But in a few, Signior Hortensio, thus it stands with me: Antonio, my father, is deceas'd, And I have thrust myself into this maze, Haply to wive and thrive as best I may; Crowns in my purse I have, and goods at home, And so am come abroad to see the world.
lines: Such wind as scatters young men through the world
To seek their fortunes farther than at home,
Where small experience grows. But in a few,
Signior Hortensio, thus it stands with me:
Antonio, my father, is deceas'd,
And I have thrust myself into this maze,
Haply to wive and thrive as best I may;
Crowns in my purse I have, and goods at home,
And so am come abroad to see the world.

speeches.speech: Petruchio, shall I then come roundly to thee And wish thee to a shrewd ill-favour'd wife? Thou'dst thank me but a little for my counsel, And yet I'll promise thee she shall be rich, And very rich; but th'art too much my friend, And I'll not wish thee to her.
lines: Petruchio, shall I then come roundly to thee
And wish thee to a shrewd ill-favour'd wife?
Thou'dst thank me but a little for my counsel,
And yet I'll promise thee she shall be rich,
And very rich; but th'art too much my friend,
And I'll not wish thee to her.

speeches.speech: Signior Hortensio, 'twixt such friends as we Few words suffice; and therefore, if thou know One rich enough to be Petruchio's wife, As wealth is burden of my wooing dance, Be she as foul as was Florentius' love, As old as Sibyl, and as curst and shrewd As Socrates' Xanthippe or a worse- She moves me not, or not removes, at least, Affection's edge in me, were she as rough As are the swelling Adriatic seas. I come to wive it wealthily in Padua; If wealthily, then happily in Padua.
lines: Signior Hortensio, 'twixt such friends as we
Few words suffice; and therefore, if thou know
One rich enough to be Petruchio's wife,
As wealth is burden of my wooing dance,
Be she as foul as was Florentius' love,
As old as Sibyl, and as curst and shrewd
As Socrates' Xanthippe or a worse-
She moves me not, or not removes, at least,
Affection's edge in me, were she as rough
As are the swelling Adriatic seas.
I come to wive it wealthily in Padua;
If wealthily, then happily in Padua.

speeches.speech: Nay, look you, sir, he tells you flatly what his mind is. Why, give him gold enough and marry him to a puppet or an aglet-baby, or an old trot with ne'er a tooth in her head, though she has as many diseases as two and fifty horses. Why, nothing comes amiss, so money comes withal.
lines: Nay, look you, sir, he tells you flatly what his mind is.
Why, give him gold enough and marry him to a puppet or an
aglet-baby, or an old trot with ne'er a tooth in her head, though
she has as many diseases as two and fifty horses. Why, nothing
comes amiss, so money comes withal.

speeches.speech: Petruchio, since we are stepp'd thus far in, I will continue that I broach'd in jest. I can, Petruchio, help thee to a wife With wealth enough, and young and beauteous; Brought up as best becomes a gentlewoman; Her only fault, and that is faults enough, Is- that she is intolerable curst, And shrewd and froward so beyond all measure That, were my state far worser than it is, I would not wed her for a mine of gold.
lines: Petruchio, since we are stepp'd thus far in,
I will continue that I broach'd in jest.
I can, Petruchio, help thee to a wife
With wealth enough, and young and beauteous;
Brought up as best becomes a gentlewoman;
Her only fault, and that is faults enough,
Is- that she is intolerable curst,
And shrewd and froward so beyond all measure
That, were my state far worser than it is,
I would not wed her for a mine of gold.

speeches.speech: Hortensio, peace! thou know'st not gold's effect. Tell me her father's name, and 'tis enough; For I will board her though she chide as loud As thunder when the clouds in autumn crack.
lines: Hortensio, peace! thou know'st not gold's effect.
Tell me her father's name, and 'tis enough;
For I will board her though she chide as loud
As thunder when the clouds in autumn crack.

speeches.speech: Her father is Baptista Minola, An affable and courteous gentleman; Her name is Katherina Minola, Renown'd in Padua for her scolding tongue.
lines: Her father is Baptista Minola,
An affable and courteous gentleman;
Her name is Katherina Minola,
Renown'd in Padua for her scolding tongue.

speeches.speech: I know her father, though I know not her; And he knew my deceased father well. I will not sleep, Hortensio, till I see her; And therefore let me be thus bold with you To give you over at this first encounter, Unless you will accompany me thither.
lines: I know her father, though I know not her;
And he knew my deceased father well.
I will not sleep, Hortensio, till I see her;
And therefore let me be thus bold with you
To give you over at this first encounter,
Unless you will accompany me thither.

speeches.speech: I pray you, sir, let him go while the humour lasts. O' my word, and she knew him as well as I do, she would think scolding would do little good upon him. She may perhaps call him half a score knaves or so. Why, that's nothing; and he begin once, he'll rail in his rope-tricks. I'll tell you what, sir: an she stand him but a little, he will throw a figure in her face, and so disfigure her with it that she shall have no more eyes to see withal than a cat. You know him not, sir.
lines: I pray you, sir, let him go while the humour lasts. O' my
word, and she knew him as well as I do, she would think scolding
would do little good upon him. She may perhaps call him half a
score knaves or so. Why, that's nothing; and he begin once, he'll
rail in his rope-tricks. I'll tell you what, sir: an she stand
him but a little, he will throw a figure in her face, and so
disfigure her with it that she shall have no more eyes to see
withal than a cat. You know him not, sir.

speeches.speech: Tarry, Petruchio, I must go with thee, For in Baptista's keep my treasure is. He hath the jewel of my life in hold, His youngest daughter, beautiful Bianca; And her withholds from me, and other more, Suitors to her and rivals in my love; Supposing it a thing impossible- For those defects I have before rehears'd- That ever Katherina will be woo'd. Therefore this order hath Baptista ta'en, That none shall have access unto Bianca Till Katherine the curst have got a husband.
lines: Tarry, Petruchio, I must go with thee,
For in Baptista's keep my treasure is.
He hath the jewel of my life in hold,
His youngest daughter, beautiful Bianca;
And her withholds from me, and other more,
Suitors to her and rivals in my love;
Supposing it a thing impossible-
For those defects I have before rehears'd-
That ever Katherina will be woo'd.
Therefore this order hath Baptista ta'en,
That none shall have access unto Bianca
Till Katherine the curst have got a husband.

speeches.speech: Katherine the curst! A title for a maid of all titles the worst.
lines: Katherine the curst!
A title for a maid of all titles the worst.

speeches.speech: Now shall my friend Petruchio do me grace, And offer me disguis'd in sober robes To old Baptista as a schoolmaster Well seen in music, to instruct Bianca; That so I may by this device at least Have leave and leisure to make love to her, And unsuspected court her by herself. Enter GREMIO with LUCENTIO disguised as CAMBIO
lines: Now shall my friend Petruchio do me grace,
And offer me disguis'd in sober robes
To old Baptista as a schoolmaster
Well seen in music, to instruct Bianca;
That so I may by this device at least
Have leave and leisure to make love to her,
And unsuspected court her by herself.
Enter GREMIO with LUCENTIO disguised as CAMBIO

speeches.speech: Here's no knavery! See, to beguile the old folks, how the young folks lay their heads together! Master, master, look about you. Who goes there, ha?
lines: Here's no knavery! See, to beguile the old folks, how the
young folks lay their heads together! Master, master, look about
you. Who goes there, ha?

speeches.speech: Peace, Grumio! It is the rival of my love. Petruchio, stand by awhile.
lines: Peace, Grumio! It is the rival of my love. Petruchio,
stand by awhile.

speeches.speech: A proper stripling, and an amorous!
lines: A proper stripling, and an amorous!

speeches.speech: O, very well; I have perus'd the note. Hark you, sir; I'll have them very fairly bound- All books of love, see that at any hand; And see you read no other lectures to her. You understand me- over and beside Signior Baptista's liberality, I'll mend it with a largess. Take your paper too, And let me have them very well perfum'd; For she is sweeter than perfume itself To whom they go to. What will you read to her?
lines: O, very well; I have perus'd the note.
Hark you, sir; I'll have them very fairly bound-
All books of love, see that at any hand;
And see you read no other lectures to her.
You understand me- over and beside
Signior Baptista's liberality,
I'll mend it with a largess. Take your paper too,
And let me have them very well perfum'd;
For she is sweeter than perfume itself
To whom they go to. What will you read to her?

speeches.speech: Whate'er I read to her, I'll plead for you As for my patron, stand you so assur'd, As firmly as yourself were still in place; Yea, and perhaps with more successful words Than you, unless you were a scholar, sir.
lines: Whate'er I read to her, I'll plead for you
As for my patron, stand you so assur'd,
As firmly as yourself were still in place;
Yea, and perhaps with more successful words
Than you, unless you were a scholar, sir.

speeches.speech: O this learning, what a thing it is!
lines: O this learning, what a thing it is!

speeches.speech: O this woodcock, what an ass it is!
lines: O this woodcock, what an ass it is!

speeches.speech: Peace, sirrah!
lines: Peace, sirrah!

speeches.speech: Grumio, mum!                       [Coming forward] God save you, Signior Gremio!
lines: Grumio, mum!                       [Coming forward]
God save you, Signior Gremio!

speeches.speech: And you are well met, Signior Hortensio. Trow you whither I am going? To Baptista Minola. I promis'd to enquire carefully About a schoolmaster for the fair Bianca; And by good fortune I have lighted well On this young man; for learning and behaviour Fit for her turn, well read in poetry And other books- good ones, I warrant ye.
lines: And you are well met, Signior Hortensio.
Trow you whither I am going? To Baptista Minola.
I promis'd to enquire carefully
About a schoolmaster for the fair Bianca;
And by good fortune I have lighted well
On this young man; for learning and behaviour
Fit for her turn, well read in poetry
And other books- good ones, I warrant ye.

speeches.speech: 'Tis well; and I have met a gentleman Hath promis'd me to help me to another, A fine musician to instruct our mistress; So shall I no whit be behind in duty To fair Bianca, so beloved of me.
lines: 'Tis well; and I have met a gentleman
Hath promis'd me to help me to another,
A fine musician to instruct our mistress;
So shall I no whit be behind in duty
To fair Bianca, so beloved of me.

speeches.speech: Beloved of me- and that my deeds shall prove.
lines: Beloved of me- and that my deeds shall prove.

speeches.speech: And that his bags shall prove.
lines: And that his bags shall prove.

speeches.speech: Gremio, 'tis now no time to vent our love. Listen to me, and if you speak me fair I'll tell you news indifferent good for either. Here is a gentleman whom by chance I met, Upon agreement from us to his liking, Will undertake to woo curst Katherine; Yea, and to marry her, if her dowry please.
lines: Gremio, 'tis now no time to vent our love.
Listen to me, and if you speak me fair
I'll tell you news indifferent good for either.
Here is a gentleman whom by chance I met,
Upon agreement from us to his liking,
Will undertake to woo curst Katherine;
Yea, and to marry her, if her dowry please.

speeches.speech: So said, so done, is well. Hortensio, have you told him all her faults?
lines: So said, so done, is well.
Hortensio, have you told him all her faults?

speeches.speech: I know she is an irksome brawling scold; If that be all, masters, I hear no harm.
lines: I know she is an irksome brawling scold;
If that be all, masters, I hear no harm.

speeches.speech: No, say'st me so, friend? What countryman?
lines: No, say'st me so, friend? What countryman?

speeches.speech: Born in Verona, old Antonio's son. My father dead, my fortune lives for me; And I do hope good days and long to see.
lines: Born in Verona, old Antonio's son.
My father dead, my fortune lives for me;
And I do hope good days and long to see.

speeches.speech: O Sir, such a life with such a wife were strange! But if you have a stomach, to't a God's name; You shall have me assisting you in all. But will you woo this wild-cat?
lines: O Sir, such a life with such a wife were strange!
But if you have a stomach, to't a God's name;
You shall have me assisting you in all.
But will you woo this wild-cat?

speeches.speech: Will I live?
lines: Will I live?

speeches.speech: Will he woo her? Ay, or I'll hang her.
lines: Will he woo her? Ay, or I'll hang her.

speeches.speech: Why came I hither but to that intent? Think you a little din can daunt mine ears? Have I not in my time heard lions roar? Have I not heard the sea, puff'd up with winds, Rage like an angry boar chafed with sweat? Have I not heard great ordnance in the field, And heaven's artillery thunder in the skies? Have I not in a pitched battle heard Loud 'larums, neighing steeds, and trumpets' clang? And do you tell me of a woman's tongue, That gives not half so great a blow to hear As will a chestnut in a farmer's fire? Tush! tush! fear boys with bugs.
lines: Why came I hither but to that intent?
Think you a little din can daunt mine ears?
Have I not in my time heard lions roar?
Have I not heard the sea, puff'd up with winds,
Rage like an angry boar chafed with sweat?
Have I not heard great ordnance in the field,
And heaven's artillery thunder in the skies?
Have I not in a pitched battle heard
Loud 'larums, neighing steeds, and trumpets' clang?
And do you tell me of a woman's tongue,
That gives not half so great a blow to hear
As will a chestnut in a farmer's fire?
Tush! tush! fear boys with bugs.

speeches.speech: For he fears none.
lines: For he fears none.

speeches.speech: Hortensio, hark: This gentleman is happily arriv'd, My mind presumes, for his own good and ours.
lines: Hortensio, hark:
This gentleman is happily arriv'd,
My mind presumes, for his own good and ours.

speeches.speech: I promis'd we would be contributors And bear his charge of wooing, whatsoe'er.
lines: I promis'd we would be contributors
And bear his charge of wooing, whatsoe'er.

speeches.speech: And so we will- provided that he win her.
lines: And so we will- provided that he win her.

speeches.speech: I would I were as sure of a good dinner. Enter TRANIO, bravely apparelled as LUCENTIO, and BIONDELLO
lines: I would I were as sure of a good dinner.
Enter TRANIO, bravely apparelled as LUCENTIO, and BIONDELLO

speeches.speech: Gentlemen, God save you! If I may be bold, Tell me, I beseech you, which is the readiest way To the house of Signior Baptista Minola?
lines: Gentlemen, God save you! If I may be bold,
Tell me, I beseech you, which is the readiest way
To the house of Signior Baptista Minola?

speeches.speech: He that has the two fair daughters; is't he you mean?
lines: He that has the two fair daughters; is't he you mean?

speeches.speech: Even he, Biondello.
lines: Even he, Biondello.

speeches.speech: Hark you, sir, you mean not her to-
lines: Hark you, sir, you mean not her to-

speeches.speech: Perhaps him and her, sir; what have you to do?
lines: Perhaps him and her, sir; what have you to do?

speeches.speech: Not her that chides, sir, at any hand, I pray.
lines: Not her that chides, sir, at any hand, I pray.

speeches.speech: I love no chiders, sir. Biondello, let's away.
lines: I love no chiders, sir. Biondello, let's away.

speeches.speech: [Aside]  Well begun, Tranio.
lines: [Aside]  Well begun, Tranio.

speeches.speech: Sir, a word ere you go. Are you a suitor to the maid you talk of, yea or no?
lines: Sir, a word ere you go.
Are you a suitor to the maid you talk of, yea or no?

speeches.speech: And if I be, sir, is it any offence?
lines: And if I be, sir, is it any offence?

speeches.speech: No; if without more words you will get you hence.
lines: No; if without more words you will get you hence.

speeches.speech: Why, sir, I pray, are not the streets as free For me as for you?
lines: Why, sir, I pray, are not the streets as free
For me as for you?

speeches.speech: But so is not she.
lines: But so is not she.

speeches.speech: For what reason, I beseech you?
lines: For what reason, I beseech you?

speeches.speech: For this reason, if you'll know, That she's the choice love of Signior Gremio.
lines: For this reason, if you'll know,
That she's the choice love of Signior Gremio.

speeches.speech: That she's the chosen of Signior Hortensio.
lines: That she's the chosen of Signior Hortensio.

speeches.speech: Softly, my masters! If you be gentlemen, Do me this right- hear me with patience. Baptista is a noble gentleman, To whom my father is not all unknown, And, were his daughter fairer than she is, She may more suitors have, and me for one. Fair Leda's daughter had a thousand wooers; Then well one more may fair Bianca have; And so she shall: Lucentio shall make one, Though Paris came in hope to speed alone.
lines: Softly, my masters! If you be gentlemen,
Do me this right- hear me with patience.
Baptista is a noble gentleman,
To whom my father is not all unknown,
And, were his daughter fairer than she is,
She may more suitors have, and me for one.
Fair Leda's daughter had a thousand wooers;
Then well one more may fair Bianca have;
And so she shall: Lucentio shall make one,
Though Paris came in hope to speed alone.

speeches.speech: What, this gentleman will out-talk us all!
lines: What, this gentleman will out-talk us all!

speeches.speech: Sir, give him head; I know he'll prove a jade.
lines: Sir, give him head; I know he'll prove a jade.

speeches.speech: Hortensio, to what end are all these words?
lines: Hortensio, to what end are all these words?

speeches.speech: Sir, let me be so bold as ask you, Did you yet ever see Baptista's daughter?
lines: Sir, let me be so bold as ask you,
Did you yet ever see Baptista's daughter?

speeches.speech: No, sir, but hear I do that he hath two: The one as famous for a scolding tongue As is the other for beauteous modesty.
lines: No, sir, but hear I do that he hath two:
The one as famous for a scolding tongue
As is the other for beauteous modesty.

speeches.speech: Sir, sir, the first's for me; let her go by.
lines: Sir, sir, the first's for me; let her go by.

speeches.speech: Yea, leave that labour to great Hercules, And let it be more than Alcides' twelve.
lines: Yea, leave that labour to great Hercules,
And let it be more than Alcides' twelve.

speeches.speech: Sir, understand you this of me, in sooth: The youngest daughter, whom you hearken for, Her father keeps from all access of suitors, And will not promise her to any man Until the elder sister first be wed. The younger then is free, and not before.
lines: Sir, understand you this of me, in sooth:
The youngest daughter, whom you hearken for,
Her father keeps from all access of suitors,
And will not promise her to any man
Until the elder sister first be wed.
The younger then is free, and not before.

speeches.speech: If it be so, sir, that you are the man Must stead us all, and me amongst the rest; And if you break the ice, and do this feat, Achieve the elder, set the younger free For our access- whose hap shall be to have her Will not so graceless be to be ingrate.
lines: If it be so, sir, that you are the man
Must stead us all, and me amongst the rest;
And if you break the ice, and do this feat,
Achieve the elder, set the younger free
For our access- whose hap shall be to have her
Will not so graceless be to be ingrate.

speeches.speech: Sir, you say well, and well you do conceive; And since you do profess to be a suitor, You must, as we do, gratify this gentleman, To whom we all rest generally beholding.
lines: Sir, you say well, and well you do conceive;
And since you do profess to be a suitor,
You must, as we do, gratify this gentleman,
To whom we all rest generally beholding.

speeches.speech: Sir, I shall not be slack; in sign whereof, Please ye we may contrive this afternoon, And quaff carouses to our mistress' health; And do as adversaries do in law- Strive mightily, but eat and drink as friends.
lines: Sir, I shall not be slack; in sign whereof,
Please ye we may contrive this afternoon,
And quaff carouses to our mistress' health;
And do as adversaries do in law-
Strive mightily, but eat and drink as friends.

speeches.speech: [with BIONDELLO:] O excellent motion! Fellows, let's be gone.
lines: [with BIONDELLO:] O excellent motion! Fellows, let's be gone.

speeches.speech: The motion's good indeed, and be it so. Petruchio, I shall be your ben venuto.           Exeunt
lines: The motion's good indeed, and be it so.
Petruchio, I shall be your ben venuto.           Exeunt

speeches.speech: Good sister, wrong me not, nor wrong yourself, To make a bondmaid and a slave of me- That I disdain; but for these other gawds, Unbind my hands, I'll pull them off myself, Yea, all my raiment, to my petticoat; Or what you will command me will I do, So well I know my duty to my elders.
lines: Good sister, wrong me not, nor wrong yourself,
To make a bondmaid and a slave of me-
That I disdain; but for these other gawds,
Unbind my hands, I'll pull them off myself,
Yea, all my raiment, to my petticoat;
Or what you will command me will I do,
So well I know my duty to my elders.

speeches.speech: Of all thy suitors here I charge thee tell Whom thou lov'st best. See thou dissemble not.
lines: Of all thy suitors here I charge thee tell
Whom thou lov'st best. See thou dissemble not.

speeches.speech: Believe me, sister, of all the men alive I never yet beheld that special face Which I could fancy more than any other.
lines: Believe me, sister, of all the men alive
I never yet beheld that special face
Which I could fancy more than any other.

speeches.speech: Minion, thou liest. Is't not Hortensio?
lines: Minion, thou liest. Is't not Hortensio?

speeches.speech: If you affect him, sister, here I swear I'll plead for you myself but you shall have him.
lines: If you affect him, sister, here I swear
I'll plead for you myself but you shall have him.

speeches.speech: O then, belike, you fancy riches more: You will have Gremio to keep you fair.
lines: O then, belike, you fancy riches more:
You will have Gremio to keep you fair.

speeches.speech: Is it for him you do envy me so? Nay, then you jest; and now I well perceive You have but jested with me all this while. I prithee, sister Kate, untie my hands.
lines: Is it for him you do envy me so?
Nay, then you jest; and now I well perceive
You have but jested with me all this while.
I prithee, sister Kate, untie my hands.

speeches.speech: [Strikes her]  If that be jest, then an the rest was so.
lines: [Strikes her]  If that be jest, then an the rest was so.

speeches.speech: Why, how now, dame! Whence grows this insolence? Bianca, stand aside- poor girl! she weeps. [He unbinds her] Go ply thy needle; meddle not with her. For shame, thou hilding of a devilish spirit, Why dost thou wrong her that did ne'er wrong thee? When did she cross thee with a bitter word?
lines: Why, how now, dame! Whence grows this insolence?
Bianca, stand aside- poor girl! she weeps.
[He unbinds her]
Go ply thy needle; meddle not with her.
For shame, thou hilding of a devilish spirit,
Why dost thou wrong her that did ne'er wrong thee?
When did she cross thee with a bitter word?

speeches.speech: Her silence flouts me, and I'll be reveng'd.
lines: Her silence flouts me, and I'll be reveng'd.

speeches.speech: What, in my sight? Bianca, get thee in.
lines: What, in my sight? Bianca, get thee in.

speeches.speech: What, will you not suffer me? Nay, now I see She is your treasure, she must have a husband; I must dance bare-foot on her wedding-day, And for your love to her lead apes in hell. Talk not to me; I will go sit and weep, Till I can find occasion of revenge.     Exit KATHERINA
lines: What, will you not suffer me? Nay, now I see
She is your treasure, she must have a husband;
I must dance bare-foot on her wedding-day,
And for your love to her lead apes in hell.
Talk not to me; I will go sit and weep,
Till I can find occasion of revenge.     Exit KATHERINA

speeches.speech: Was ever gentleman thus griev'd as I? But who comes here?
lines: Was ever gentleman thus griev'd as I?
But who comes here?

speeches.speech: Good morrow, neighbour Baptista.
lines: Good morrow, neighbour Baptista.

speeches.speech: Good morrow, neighbour Gremio. God save you, gentlemen!
lines: Good morrow, neighbour Gremio.
God save you, gentlemen!

speeches.speech: And you, good sir! Pray, have you not a daughter Call'd Katherina, fair and virtuous?
lines: And you, good sir! Pray, have you not a daughter
Call'd Katherina, fair and virtuous?

speeches.speech: I have a daughter, sir, call'd Katherina.
lines: I have a daughter, sir, call'd Katherina.

speeches.speech: You are too blunt; go to it orderly.
lines: You are too blunt; go to it orderly.

speeches.speech: You wrong me, Signior Gremio; give me leave. I am a gentleman of Verona, sir, That, hearing of her beauty and her wit, Her affability and bashful modesty, Her wondrous qualities and mild behaviour, Am bold to show myself a forward guest Within your house, to make mine eye the witness Of that report which I so oft have heard. And, for an entrance to my entertainment, I do present you with a man of mine, [Presenting HORTENSIO] Cunning in music and the mathematics, To instruct her fully in those sciences, Whereof I know she is not ignorant. Accept of him, or else you do me wrong- His name is Licio, born in Mantua.
lines: You wrong me, Signior Gremio; give me leave.
I am a gentleman of Verona, sir,
That, hearing of her beauty and her wit,
Her affability and bashful modesty,
