Her wondrous qualities and mild behaviour,
Am bold to show myself a forward guest
Within your house, to make mine eye the witness
Of that report which I so oft have heard.
And, for an entrance to my entertainment,
I do present you with a man of mine,
[Presenting HORTENSIO]
Cunning in music and the mathematics,
To instruct her fully in those sciences,
Whereof I know she is not ignorant.
Accept of him, or else you do me wrong-
His name is Licio, born in Mantua.

speeches.speech: Y'are welcome, sir, and he for your good sake; But for my daughter Katherine, this I know, She is not for your turn, the more my grief.
lines: Y'are welcome, sir, and he for your good sake;
But for my daughter Katherine, this I know,
She is not for your turn, the more my grief.

speeches.speech: I see you do not mean to part with her; Or else you like not of my company.
lines: I see you do not mean to part with her;
Or else you like not of my company.

speeches.speech: Mistake me not; I speak but as I find. Whence are you, sir? What may I call your name?
lines: Mistake me not; I speak but as I find.
Whence are you, sir? What may I call your name?

speeches.speech: Petruchio is my name, Antonio's son, A man well known throughout all Italy.
lines: Petruchio is my name, Antonio's son,
A man well known throughout all Italy.

speeches.speech: I know him well; you are welcome for his sake.
lines: I know him well; you are welcome for his sake.

speeches.speech: Saving your tale, Petruchio, I pray, Let us that are poor petitioners speak too. Bacare! you are marvellous forward.
lines: Saving your tale, Petruchio, I pray,
Let us that are poor petitioners speak too.
Bacare! you are marvellous forward.

speeches.speech: O, pardon me, Signior Gremio! I would fain be doing.
lines: O, pardon me, Signior Gremio! I would fain be doing.

speeches.speech: I doubt it not, sir; but you will curse your wooing. Neighbour, this is a gift very grateful, I am sure of it. To express the like kindness, myself, that have been more kindly beholding to you than any, freely give unto you this young scholar  [Presenting LUCENTIO]  that hath been long studying at Rheims; as cunning in Greek, Latin, and other languages, as the other in music and mathematics. His name is Cambio. Pray accept his service.
lines: I doubt it not, sir; but you will curse your wooing.
Neighbour, this is a gift very grateful, I am sure of it. To
express the like kindness, myself, that have been more kindly
beholding to you than any, freely give unto you this young
scholar  [Presenting LUCENTIO]  that hath been long studying at
Rheims; as cunning in Greek, Latin, and other languages, as the
other in music and mathematics. His name is Cambio. Pray accept
his service.

speeches.speech: A thousand thanks, Signior Gremio. Welcome, good Cambio. [To TRANIO]  But, gentle sir, methinks you walk like a stranger. May I be so bold to know the cause of your coming?
lines: A thousand thanks, Signior Gremio. Welcome, good Cambio.
[To TRANIO]  But, gentle sir, methinks you walk like a stranger.
May I be so bold to know the cause of your coming?

speeches.speech: Pardon me, sir, the boldness is mine own That, being a stranger in this city here, Do make myself a suitor to your daughter, Unto Bianca, fair and virtuous. Nor is your firm resolve unknown to me In the preferment of the eldest sister. This liberty is all that I request- That, upon knowledge of my parentage, I may have welcome 'mongst the rest that woo, And free access and favour as the rest. And toward the education of your daughters I here bestow a simple instrument, And this small packet of Greek and Latin books. If you accept them, then their worth is great.
lines: Pardon me, sir, the boldness is mine own
That, being a stranger in this city here,
Do make myself a suitor to your daughter,
Unto Bianca, fair and virtuous.
Nor is your firm resolve unknown to me
In the preferment of the eldest sister.
This liberty is all that I request-
That, upon knowledge of my parentage,
I may have welcome 'mongst the rest that woo,
And free access and favour as the rest.
And toward the education of your daughters
I here bestow a simple instrument,
And this small packet of Greek and Latin books.
If you accept them, then their worth is great.

speeches.speech: Lucentio is your name? Of whence, I pray?
lines: Lucentio is your name? Of whence, I pray?

speeches.speech: Of Pisa, sir; son to Vincentio.
lines: Of Pisa, sir; son to Vincentio.

speeches.speech: A mighty man of Pisa. By report I know him well. You are very welcome, sir. Take you the lute, and you the set of books; You shall go see your pupils presently. Holla, within! [Enter a SERVANT] Sirrah, lead these gentlemen To my daughters; and tell them both These are their tutors. Bid them use them well. [Exit SERVANT leading HORTENSIO carrying the lute and LUCENTIO with the books] We will go walk a little in the orchard, And then to dinner. You are passing welcome, And so I pray you all to think yourselves.
lines: A mighty man of Pisa. By report
I know him well. You are very welcome, sir.
Take you the lute, and you the set of books;
You shall go see your pupils presently.
Holla, within!
[Enter a SERVANT]
Sirrah, lead these gentlemen
To my daughters; and tell them both
These are their tutors. Bid them use them well.
[Exit SERVANT leading HORTENSIO carrying the lute and LUCENTIO with the books]
We will go walk a little in the orchard,
And then to dinner. You are passing welcome,
And so I pray you all to think yourselves.

speeches.speech: Signior Baptista, my business asketh haste, And every day I cannot come to woo. You knew my father well, and in him me, Left solely heir to all his lands and goods, Which I have bettered rather than decreas'd. Then tell me, if I get your daughter's love, What dowry shall I have with her to wife?
lines: Signior Baptista, my business asketh haste,
And every day I cannot come to woo.
You knew my father well, and in him me,
Left solely heir to all his lands and goods,
Which I have bettered rather than decreas'd.
Then tell me, if I get your daughter's love,
What dowry shall I have with her to wife?

speeches.speech: After my death, the one half of my lands And, in possession, twenty thousand crowns.
lines: After my death, the one half of my lands
And, in possession, twenty thousand crowns.

speeches.speech: And for that dowry, I'll assure her of Her widowhood, be it that she survive me, In all my lands and leases whatsoever. Let specialities be therefore drawn between us, That covenants may be kept on either hand.
lines: And for that dowry, I'll assure her of
Her widowhood, be it that she survive me,
In all my lands and leases whatsoever.
Let specialities be therefore drawn between us,
That covenants may be kept on either hand.

speeches.speech: Ay, when the special thing is well obtain'd, That is, her love; for that is all in all.
lines: Ay, when the special thing is well obtain'd,
That is, her love; for that is all in all.

speeches.speech: Why, that is nothing; for I tell you, father, I am as peremptory as she proud-minded; And where two raging fires meet together, They do consume the thing that feeds their fury. Though little fire grows great with little wind, Yet extreme gusts will blow out fire and all. So I to her, and so she yields to me; For I am rough, and woo not like a babe.
lines: Why, that is nothing; for I tell you, father,
I am as peremptory as she proud-minded;
And where two raging fires meet together,
They do consume the thing that feeds their fury.
Though little fire grows great with little wind,
Yet extreme gusts will blow out fire and all.
So I to her, and so she yields to me;
For I am rough, and woo not like a babe.

speeches.speech: Well mayst thou woo, and happy be thy speed But be thou arm'd for some unhappy words.
lines: Well mayst thou woo, and happy be thy speed
But be thou arm'd for some unhappy words.

speeches.speech: Ay, to the proof, as mountains are for winds, That shake not though they blow perpetually.
lines: Ay, to the proof, as mountains are for winds,
That shake not though they blow perpetually.

speeches.speech: How now, my friend! Why dost thou look so pale?
lines: How now, my friend! Why dost thou look so pale?

speeches.speech: For fear, I promise you, if I look pale.
lines: For fear, I promise you, if I look pale.

speeches.speech: What, will my daughter prove a good musician?
lines: What, will my daughter prove a good musician?

speeches.speech: I think she'll sooner prove a soldier: Iron may hold with her, but never lutes.
lines: I think she'll sooner prove a soldier:
Iron may hold with her, but never lutes.

speeches.speech: Why, then thou canst not break her to the lute?
lines: Why, then thou canst not break her to the lute?

speeches.speech: Why, no; for she hath broke the lute to me. I did but tell her she mistook her frets, And bow'd her hand to teach her fingering, When, with a most impatient devilish spirit, 'Frets, call you these?' quoth she 'I'll fume with them.' And with that word she struck me on the head, And through the instrument my pate made way; And there I stood amazed for a while, As on a pillory, looking through the lute, While she did call me rascal fiddler And twangling Jack, with twenty such vile terms, As she had studied to misuse me so.
lines: Why, no; for she hath broke the lute to me.
I did but tell her she mistook her frets,
And bow'd her hand to teach her fingering,
When, with a most impatient devilish spirit,
'Frets, call you these?' quoth she 'I'll fume with them.'
And with that word she struck me on the head,
And through the instrument my pate made way;
And there I stood amazed for a while,
As on a pillory, looking through the lute,
While she did call me rascal fiddler
And twangling Jack, with twenty such vile terms,
As she had studied to misuse me so.

speeches.speech: Now, by the world, it is a lusty wench; I love her ten times more than e'er I did. O, how I long to have some chat with her!
lines: Now, by the world, it is a lusty wench;
I love her ten times more than e'er I did.
O, how I long to have some chat with her!

speeches.speech: Well, go with me, and be not so discomfited; Proceed in practice with my younger daughter; She's apt to learn, and thankful for good turns. Signior Petruchio, will you go with us, Or shall I send my daughter Kate to you?
lines: Well, go with me, and be not so discomfited;
Proceed in practice with my younger daughter;
She's apt to learn, and thankful for good turns.
Signior Petruchio, will you go with us,
Or shall I send my daughter Kate to you?

speeches.speech: I pray you do.        Exeunt all but PETRUCHIO I'll attend her here, And woo her with some spirit when she comes. Say that she rail; why, then I'll tell her plain She sings as sweetly as a nightingale. Say that she frown; I'll say she looks as clear As morning roses newly wash'd with dew. Say she be mute, and will not speak a word; Then I'll commend her volubility, And say she uttereth piercing eloquence. If she do bid me pack, I'll give her thanks, As though she bid me stay by her a week; If she deny to wed, I'll crave the day When I shall ask the banns, and when be married. But here she comes; and now, Petruchio, speak. [Enter KATHERINA] Good morrow, Kate- for that's your name, I hear.
lines: I pray you do.        Exeunt all but PETRUCHIO
I'll attend her here,
And woo her with some spirit when she comes.
Say that she rail; why, then I'll tell her plain
She sings as sweetly as a nightingale.
Say that she frown; I'll say she looks as clear
As morning roses newly wash'd with dew.
Say she be mute, and will not speak a word;
Then I'll commend her volubility,
And say she uttereth piercing eloquence.
If she do bid me pack, I'll give her thanks,
As though she bid me stay by her a week;
If she deny to wed, I'll crave the day
When I shall ask the banns, and when be married.
But here she comes; and now, Petruchio, speak.
[Enter KATHERINA]
Good morrow, Kate- for that's your name, I hear.

speeches.speech: Well have you heard, but something hard of hearing: They call me Katherine that do talk of me.
lines: Well have you heard, but something hard of hearing:
They call me Katherine that do talk of me.

speeches.speech: You lie, in faith, for you are call'd plain Kate, And bonny Kate, and sometimes Kate the curst; But, Kate, the prettiest Kate in Christendom, Kate of Kate Hall, my super-dainty Kate, For dainties are all Kates, and therefore, Kate, Take this of me, Kate of my consolation- Hearing thy mildness prais'd in every town, Thy virtues spoke of, and thy beauty sounded, Yet not so deeply as to thee belongs, Myself am mov'd to woo thee for my wife.
lines: You lie, in faith, for you are call'd plain Kate,
And bonny Kate, and sometimes Kate the curst;
But, Kate, the prettiest Kate in Christendom,
Kate of Kate Hall, my super-dainty Kate,
For dainties are all Kates, and therefore, Kate,
Take this of me, Kate of my consolation-
Hearing thy mildness prais'd in every town,
Thy virtues spoke of, and thy beauty sounded,
Yet not so deeply as to thee belongs,
Myself am mov'd to woo thee for my wife.

speeches.speech: Mov'd! in good time! Let him that mov'd you hither Remove you hence. I knew you at the first You were a moveable.
lines: Mov'd! in good time! Let him that mov'd you hither
Remove you hence. I knew you at the first
You were a moveable.

speeches.speech: Why, what's a moveable?
lines: Why, what's a moveable?

speeches.speech: A join'd-stool.
lines: A join'd-stool.

speeches.speech: Thou hast hit it. Come, sit on me.
lines: Thou hast hit it. Come, sit on me.

speeches.speech: Asses are made to bear, and so are you.
lines: Asses are made to bear, and so are you.

speeches.speech: Women are made to bear, and so are you.
lines: Women are made to bear, and so are you.

speeches.speech: No such jade as you, if me you mean.
lines: No such jade as you, if me you mean.

speeches.speech: Alas, good Kate, I will not burden thee! For, knowing thee to be but young and light-
lines: Alas, good Kate, I will not burden thee!
For, knowing thee to be but young and light-

speeches.speech: Too light for such a swain as you to catch; And yet as heavy as my weight should be.
lines: Too light for such a swain as you to catch;
And yet as heavy as my weight should be.

speeches.speech: Should be! should- buzz!
lines: Should be! should- buzz!

speeches.speech: Well ta'en, and like a buzzard.
lines: Well ta'en, and like a buzzard.

speeches.speech: O, slow-wing'd turtle, shall a buzzard take thee?
lines: O, slow-wing'd turtle, shall a buzzard take thee?

speeches.speech: Ay, for a turtle, as he takes a buzzard.
lines: Ay, for a turtle, as he takes a buzzard.

speeches.speech: Come, come, you wasp; i' faith, you are too angry.
lines: Come, come, you wasp; i' faith, you are too angry.

speeches.speech: If I be waspish, best beware my sting.
lines: If I be waspish, best beware my sting.

speeches.speech: My remedy is then to pluck it out.
lines: My remedy is then to pluck it out.

speeches.speech: Ay, if the fool could find it where it lies.
lines: Ay, if the fool could find it where it lies.

speeches.speech: Who knows not where a wasp does wear his sting? In his tail.
lines: Who knows not where a wasp does wear his sting?
In his tail.

speeches.speech: In his tongue.
lines: In his tongue.

speeches.speech: Whose tongue?
lines: Whose tongue?

speeches.speech: Yours, if you talk of tales; and so farewell.
lines: Yours, if you talk of tales; and so farewell.

speeches.speech: What, with my tongue in your tail? Nay, come again, Good Kate; I am a gentleman.
lines: What, with my tongue in your tail? Nay, come again,
Good Kate; I am a gentleman.

speeches.speech: That I'll try.               [She strikes him]
lines: That I'll try.               [She strikes him]

speeches.speech: I swear I'll cuff you, if you strike again.
lines: I swear I'll cuff you, if you strike again.

speeches.speech: So may you lose your arms. If you strike me, you are no gentleman; And if no gentleman, why then no arms.
lines: So may you lose your arms.
If you strike me, you are no gentleman;
And if no gentleman, why then no arms.

speeches.speech: A herald, Kate? O, put me in thy books!
lines: A herald, Kate? O, put me in thy books!

speeches.speech: What is your crest- a coxcomb?
lines: What is your crest- a coxcomb?

speeches.speech: A combless cock, so Kate will be my hen.
lines: A combless cock, so Kate will be my hen.

speeches.speech: No cock of mine: you crow too like a craven.
lines: No cock of mine: you crow too like a craven.

speeches.speech: Nay, come, Kate, come; you must not look so sour.
lines: Nay, come, Kate, come; you must not look so sour.

speeches.speech: It is my fashion, when I see a crab.
lines: It is my fashion, when I see a crab.

speeches.speech: Why, here's no crab; and therefore look not sour.
lines: Why, here's no crab; and therefore look not sour.

speeches.speech: There is, there is.
lines: There is, there is.

speeches.speech: Then show it me.
lines: Then show it me.

speeches.speech: Had I a glass I would.
lines: Had I a glass I would.

speeches.speech: What, you mean my face?
lines: What, you mean my face?

speeches.speech: Well aim'd of such a young one.
lines: Well aim'd of such a young one.

speeches.speech: Now, by Saint George, I am too young for you.
lines: Now, by Saint George, I am too young for you.

speeches.speech: Yet you are wither'd.
lines: Yet you are wither'd.

speeches.speech: 'Tis with cares.
lines: 'Tis with cares.

speeches.speech: I care not.
lines: I care not.

speeches.speech: Nay, hear you, Kate- in sooth, you scape not so.
lines: Nay, hear you, Kate- in sooth, you scape not so.

speeches.speech: I chafe you, if I tarry; let me go.
lines: I chafe you, if I tarry; let me go.

speeches.speech: No, not a whit; I find you passing gentle. 'Twas told me you were rough, and coy, and sullen, And now I find report a very liar; For thou art pleasant, gamesome, passing courteous, But slow in speech, yet sweet as springtime flowers. Thou canst not frown, thou canst not look askance, Nor bite the lip, as angry wenches will, Nor hast thou pleasure to be cross in talk; But thou with mildness entertain'st thy wooers; With gentle conference, soft and affable. Why does the world report that Kate doth limp? O sland'rous world! Kate like the hazel-twig Is straight and slender, and as brown in hue As hazel-nuts, and sweeter than the kernels. O, let me see thee walk. Thou dost not halt.
lines: No, not a whit; I find you passing gentle.
'Twas told me you were rough, and coy, and sullen,
And now I find report a very liar;
For thou art pleasant, gamesome, passing courteous,
But slow in speech, yet sweet as springtime flowers.
Thou canst not frown, thou canst not look askance,
Nor bite the lip, as angry wenches will,
Nor hast thou pleasure to be cross in talk;
But thou with mildness entertain'st thy wooers;
With gentle conference, soft and affable.
Why does the world report that Kate doth limp?
O sland'rous world! Kate like the hazel-twig
Is straight and slender, and as brown in hue
As hazel-nuts, and sweeter than the kernels.
O, let me see thee walk. Thou dost not halt.

speeches.speech: Go, fool, and whom thou keep'st command.
lines: Go, fool, and whom thou keep'st command.

speeches.speech: Did ever Dian so become a grove As Kate this chamber with her princely gait? O, be thou Dian, and let her be Kate; And then let Kate be chaste, and Dian sportful!
lines: Did ever Dian so become a grove
As Kate this chamber with her princely gait?
O, be thou Dian, and let her be Kate;
And then let Kate be chaste, and Dian sportful!

speeches.speech: Where did you study all this goodly speech?
lines: Where did you study all this goodly speech?

speeches.speech: It is extempore, from my mother wit.
lines: It is extempore, from my mother wit.

speeches.speech: A witty mother! witless else her son.
lines: A witty mother! witless else her son.

speeches.speech: Am I not wise?
lines: Am I not wise?

speeches.speech: Yes, keep you warm.
lines: Yes, keep you warm.

speeches.speech: Marry, so I mean, sweet Katherine, in thy bed. And therefore, setting all this chat aside, Thus in plain terms: your father hath consented That you shall be my wife your dowry greed on; And will you, nill you, I will marry you. Now, Kate, I am a husband for your turn; For, by this light, whereby I see thy beauty, Thy beauty that doth make me like thee well, Thou must be married to no man but me; For I am he am born to tame you, Kate, And bring you from a wild Kate to a Kate Conformable as other household Kates. [Re-enter BAPTISTA, GREMIO, and TRANIO] Here comes your father. Never make denial; I must and will have Katherine to my wife.
lines: Marry, so I mean, sweet Katherine, in thy bed.
And therefore, setting all this chat aside,
Thus in plain terms: your father hath consented
That you shall be my wife your dowry greed on;
And will you, nill you, I will marry you.
Now, Kate, I am a husband for your turn;
For, by this light, whereby I see thy beauty,
Thy beauty that doth make me like thee well,
Thou must be married to no man but me;
For I am he am born to tame you, Kate,
And bring you from a wild Kate to a Kate
Conformable as other household Kates.
[Re-enter BAPTISTA, GREMIO, and TRANIO]
Here comes your father. Never make denial;
I must and will have Katherine to my wife.

speeches.speech: Now, Signior Petruchio, how speed you with my daughter?
lines: Now, Signior Petruchio, how speed you with my daughter?

speeches.speech: How but well, sir? how but well? It were impossible I should speed amiss.
lines: How but well, sir? how but well?
It were impossible I should speed amiss.

speeches.speech: Why, how now, daughter Katherine, in your dumps?
lines: Why, how now, daughter Katherine, in your dumps?

speeches.speech: Call you me daughter? Now I promise you You have show'd a tender fatherly regard To wish me wed to one half lunatic, A mad-cap ruffian and a swearing Jack, That thinks with oaths to face the matter out.
lines: Call you me daughter? Now I promise you
You have show'd a tender fatherly regard
To wish me wed to one half lunatic,
A mad-cap ruffian and a swearing Jack,
That thinks with oaths to face the matter out.

speeches.speech: Father, 'tis thus: yourself and all the world That talk'd of her have talk'd amiss of her. If she be curst, it is for policy, For,she's not froward, but modest as the dove; She is not hot, but temperate as the morn; For patience she will prove a second Grissel, And Roman Lucrece for her chastity. And, to conclude, we have 'greed so well together That upon Sunday is the wedding-day.
lines: Father, 'tis thus: yourself and all the world
That talk'd of her have talk'd amiss of her.
If she be curst, it is for policy,
For,she's not froward, but modest as the dove;
She is not hot, but temperate as the morn;
For patience she will prove a second Grissel,
And Roman Lucrece for her chastity.
And, to conclude, we have 'greed so well together
That upon Sunday is the wedding-day.

speeches.speech: I'll see thee hang'd on Sunday first.
lines: I'll see thee hang'd on Sunday first.

speeches.speech: Hark, Petruchio; she says she'll see thee hang'd first.
lines: Hark, Petruchio; she says she'll see thee hang'd first.

speeches.speech: Is this your speeding? Nay, then good-night our part!
lines: Is this your speeding? Nay, then good-night our part!

speeches.speech: Be patient, gentlemen. I choose her for myself; If she and I be pleas'd, what's that to you? 'Tis bargain'd 'twixt us twain, being alone, That she shall still be curst in company. I tell you 'tis incredible to believe. How much she loves me- O, the kindest Kate! She hung about my neck, and kiss on kiss She vied so fast, protesting oath on oath, That in a twink she won me to her love. O, you are novices! 'Tis a world to see, How tame, when men and women are alone, A meacock wretch can make the curstest shrew. Give me thy hand, Kate; I will unto Venice, To buy apparel 'gainst the wedding-day. Provide the feast, father, and bid the guests; I will be sure my Katherine shall be fine.
lines: Be patient, gentlemen. I choose her for myself;
If she and I be pleas'd, what's that to you?
'Tis bargain'd 'twixt us twain, being alone,
That she shall still be curst in company.
I tell you 'tis incredible to believe.
How much she loves me- O, the kindest Kate!
She hung about my neck, and kiss on kiss
She vied so fast, protesting oath on oath,
That in a twink she won me to her love.
O, you are novices! 'Tis a world to see,
How tame, when men and women are alone,
A meacock wretch can make the curstest shrew.
Give me thy hand, Kate; I will unto Venice,
To buy apparel 'gainst the wedding-day.
Provide the feast, father, and bid the guests;
I will be sure my Katherine shall be fine.

speeches.speech: I know not what to say; but give me your hands. God send you joy, Petruchio! 'Tis a match.
lines: I know not what to say; but give me your hands.
God send you joy, Petruchio! 'Tis a match.

speeches.speech: [with TRANIO:] Amen, say we; we will be witnesses.
lines: [with TRANIO:] Amen, say we; we will be witnesses.

speeches.speech: Father, and wife, and gentlemen, adieu. I will to Venice; Sunday comes apace; We will have rings and things, and fine array; And kiss me, Kate; we will be married a Sunday.
lines: Father, and wife, and gentlemen, adieu.
I will to Venice; Sunday comes apace;
We will have rings and things, and fine array;
And kiss me, Kate; we will be married a Sunday.

speeches.speech: Was ever match clapp'd up so suddenly?
lines: Was ever match clapp'd up so suddenly?

speeches.speech: Faith, gentlemen, now I play a merchant's part, And venture madly on a desperate mart.
lines: Faith, gentlemen, now I play a merchant's part,
And venture madly on a desperate mart.

speeches.speech: 'Twas a commodity lay fretting by you; 'Twill bring you gain, or perish on the seas.
lines: 'Twas a commodity lay fretting by you;
'Twill bring you gain, or perish on the seas.

speeches.speech: The gain I seek is quiet in the match.
lines: The gain I seek is quiet in the match.

speeches.speech: No doubt but he hath got a quiet catch. But now, Baptista, to your younger daughter: Now is the day we long have looked for; I am your neighbour, and was suitor first.
lines: No doubt but he hath got a quiet catch.
But now, Baptista, to your younger daughter:
Now is the day we long have looked for;
I am your neighbour, and was suitor first.

speeches.speech: And I am one that love Bianca more Than words can witness or your thoughts can guess.
lines: And I am one that love Bianca more
Than words can witness or your thoughts can guess.

speeches.speech: Youngling, thou canst not love so dear as I.
lines: Youngling, thou canst not love so dear as I.

speeches.speech: Greybeard, thy love doth freeze.
lines: Greybeard, thy love doth freeze.

speeches.speech: But thine doth fry. Skipper, stand back; 'tis age that nourisheth.
lines: But thine doth fry.
Skipper, stand back; 'tis age that nourisheth.

speeches.speech: But youth in ladies' eyes that flourisheth.
lines: But youth in ladies' eyes that flourisheth.

speeches.speech: Content you, gentlemen; I will compound this strife. 'Tis deeds must win the prize, and he of both That can assure my daughter greatest dower Shall have my Bianca's love. Say, Signior Gremio, what can you assure her?
lines: Content you, gentlemen; I will compound this strife.
'Tis deeds must win the prize, and he of both
That can assure my daughter greatest dower
Shall have my Bianca's love.
Say, Signior Gremio, what can you assure her?

speeches.speech: First, as you know, my house within the city Is richly furnished with plate and gold, Basins and ewers to lave her dainty hands; My hangings all of Tyrian tapestry; In ivory coffers I have stuff'd my crowns; In cypress chests my arras counterpoints, Costly apparel, tents, and canopies, Fine linen, Turkey cushions boss'd with pearl, Valance of Venice gold in needle-work; Pewter and brass, and all things that belongs To house or housekeeping. Then at my farm I have a hundred milch-kine to the pail, Six score fat oxen standing in my stalls, And all things answerable to this portion. Myself am struck in years, I must confess; And if I die to-morrow this is hers, If whilst I live she will be only mine.
lines: First, as you know, my house within the city
Is richly furnished with plate and gold,
Basins and ewers to lave her dainty hands;
My hangings all of Tyrian tapestry;
In ivory coffers I have stuff'd my crowns;
In cypress chests my arras counterpoints,
Costly apparel, tents, and canopies,
Fine linen, Turkey cushions boss'd with pearl,
Valance of Venice gold in needle-work;
Pewter and brass, and all things that belongs
To house or housekeeping. Then at my farm
I have a hundred milch-kine to the pail,
Six score fat oxen standing in my stalls,
And all things answerable to this portion.
Myself am struck in years, I must confess;
And if I die to-morrow this is hers,
If whilst I live she will be only mine.

speeches.speech: That 'only' came well in. Sir, list to me: I am my father's heir and only son; If I may have your daughter to my wife, I'll leave her houses three or four as good Within rich Pisa's walls as any one Old Signior Gremio has in Padua; Besides two thousand ducats by the year Of fruitful land, all which shall be her jointure. What, have I pinch'd you, Signior Gremio?
lines: That 'only' came well in. Sir, list to me:
I am my father's heir and only son;
If I may have your daughter to my wife,
I'll leave her houses three or four as good
Within rich Pisa's walls as any one
Old Signior Gremio has in Padua;
Besides two thousand ducats by the year
Of fruitful land, all which shall be her jointure.
What, have I pinch'd you, Signior Gremio?

speeches.speech: Two thousand ducats by the year of land! [Aside]  My land amounts not to so much in all.- That she shall have, besides an argosy That now is lying in Marseilles road. What, have I chok'd you with an argosy?
lines: Two thousand ducats by the year of land!
[Aside]  My land amounts not to so much in all.-
That she shall have, besides an argosy
That now is lying in Marseilles road.
What, have I chok'd you with an argosy?

speeches.speech: Gremio, 'tis known my father hath no less Than three great argosies, besides two galliasses, And twelve tight galleys. These I will assure her, And twice as much whate'er thou off'rest next.
lines: Gremio, 'tis known my father hath no less
Than three great argosies, besides two galliasses,
And twelve tight galleys. These I will assure her,
And twice as much whate'er thou off'rest next.

speeches.speech: Nay, I have off'red all; I have no more; And she can have no more than all I have; If you like me, she shall have me and mine.
lines: Nay, I have off'red all; I have no more;
And she can have no more than all I have;
If you like me, she shall have me and mine.

speeches.speech: Why, then the maid is mine from all the world By your firm promise; Gremio is out-vied.
lines: Why, then the maid is mine from all the world
By your firm promise; Gremio is out-vied.

speeches.speech: I must confess your offer is the best; And let your father make her the assurance, She is your own. Else, you must pardon me; If you should die before him, where's her dower?
lines: I must confess your offer is the best;
And let your father make her the assurance,
She is your own. Else, you must pardon me;
If you should die before him, where's her dower?

speeches.speech: That's but a cavil; he is old, I young.
lines: That's but a cavil; he is old, I young.

speeches.speech: And may not young men die as well as old?
lines: And may not young men die as well as old?

speeches.speech: Well, gentlemen, I am thus resolv'd: on Sunday next you know My daughter Katherine is to be married; Now, on the Sunday following shall Bianca Be bride to you, if you make this assurance; If not, to Signior Gremio. And so I take my leave, and thank you both.
lines: Well, gentlemen,
I am thus resolv'd: on Sunday next you know
My daughter Katherine is to be married;
Now, on the Sunday following shall Bianca
Be bride to you, if you make this assurance;
If not, to Signior Gremio.
And so I take my leave, and thank you both.

speeches.speech: Adieu, good neighbour.              Exit BAPTISTA Now, I fear thee not. Sirrah young gamester, your father were a fool To give thee all, and in his waning age Set foot under thy table. Tut, a toy! An old Italian fox is not so kind, my boy.         Exit
lines: Adieu, good neighbour.              Exit BAPTISTA
Now, I fear thee not.
Sirrah young gamester, your father were a fool
To give thee all, and in his waning age
Set foot under thy table. Tut, a toy!
An old Italian fox is not so kind, my boy.         Exit

speeches.speech: A vengeance on your crafty withered hide! Yet I have fac'd it with a card of ten. 'Tis in my head to do my master good: I see no reason but suppos'd Lucentio Must get a father, call'd suppos'd Vincentio; And that's a wonder- fathers commonly Do get their children; but in this case of wooing A child shall get a sire, if I fail not of my cunning.
lines: A vengeance on your crafty withered hide!
Yet I have fac'd it with a card of ten.
'Tis in my head to do my master good:
I see no reason but suppos'd Lucentio
Must get a father, call'd suppos'd Vincentio;
And that's a wonder- fathers commonly
Do get their children; but in this case of wooing
A child shall get a sire, if I fail not of my cunning.

speeches.speech: Fiddler, forbear; you grow too forward, sir. Have you so soon forgot the entertainment Her sister Katherine welcome'd you withal?
lines: Fiddler, forbear; you grow too forward, sir.
Have you so soon forgot the entertainment
Her sister Katherine welcome'd you withal?

speeches.speech: But, wrangling pedant, this is The patroness of heavenly harmony. Then give me leave to have prerogative; And when in music we have spent an hour, Your lecture shall have leisure for as much.
lines: But, wrangling pedant, this is
The patroness of heavenly harmony.
Then give me leave to have prerogative;
And when in music we have spent an hour,
Your lecture shall have leisure for as much.

speeches.speech: Preposterous ass, that never read so far To know the cause why music was ordain'd! Was it not to refresh the mind of man After his studies or his usual pain? Then give me leave to read philosophy, And while I pause serve in your harmony.
lines: Preposterous ass, that never read so far
To know the cause why music was ordain'd!
Was it not to refresh the mind of man
After his studies or his usual pain?
Then give me leave to read philosophy,
And while I pause serve in your harmony.

speeches.speech: Sirrah, I will not bear these braves of thine.
lines: Sirrah, I will not bear these braves of thine.

speeches.speech: Why, gentlemen, you do me double wrong To strive for that which resteth in my choice. I am no breeching scholar in the schools, I'll not be tied to hours nor 'pointed times, But learn my lessons as I please myself. And to cut off all strife: here sit we down; Take you your instrument, play you the whiles! His lecture will be done ere you have tun'd.
lines: Why, gentlemen, you do me double wrong
To strive for that which resteth in my choice.
I am no breeching scholar in the schools,
I'll not be tied to hours nor 'pointed times,
But learn my lessons as I please myself.
And to cut off all strife: here sit we down;
Take you your instrument, play you the whiles!
His lecture will be done ere you have tun'd.

speeches.speech: You'll leave his lecture when I am in tune?
lines: You'll leave his lecture when I am in tune?

speeches.speech: That will be never- tune your instrument.
lines: That will be never- tune your instrument.

speeches.speech: Where left we last?
lines: Where left we last?

speeches.speech: Here, madam: 'Hic ibat Simois, hic est Sigeia tellus, Hic steterat Priami regia celsa senis.'
lines: Here, madam:
'Hic ibat Simois, hic est Sigeia tellus,
Hic steterat Priami regia celsa senis.'

speeches.speech: Construe them.
lines: Construe them.

speeches.speech: 'Hic ibat' as I told you before- 'Simois' I am Lucentio- 'hic est' son unto Vincentio of Pisa- 'Sigeia tellus' disguised thus to get your love- 'Hic steterat' and that Lucentio that comes a-wooing- 'Priami' is my man Tranio- 'regia' bearing my port- 'celsa senis' that we might beguile the old pantaloon.
lines: 'Hic ibat' as I told you before- 'Simois' I am Lucentio-
'hic est' son unto Vincentio of Pisa- 'Sigeia tellus' disguised
thus to get your love- 'Hic steterat' and that Lucentio that
comes a-wooing- 'Priami' is my man Tranio- 'regia' bearing my
port- 'celsa senis' that we might beguile the old pantaloon.

speeches.speech: Madam, my instrument's in tune.
lines: Madam, my instrument's in tune.

speeches.speech: Let's hear. O fie! the treble jars.
lines: Let's hear. O fie! the treble jars.

speeches.speech: Spit in the hole, man, and tune again.
lines: Spit in the hole, man, and tune again.

speeches.speech: Now let me see if I can construe it: 'Hic ibat Simois' I know you not- 'hic est Sigeia tellus' I trust you not- 'Hic steterat Priami' take heed he hear us not- 'regia' presume not- 'celsa senis' despair not.
lines: Now let me see if I can construe it: 'Hic ibat Simois' I
know you not- 'hic est Sigeia tellus' I trust you not- 'Hic
steterat Priami' take heed he hear us not- 'regia' presume not-
'celsa senis' despair not.

speeches.speech: Madam, 'tis now in tune.
lines: Madam, 'tis now in tune.

speeches.speech: All but the bass.
lines: All but the bass.

speeches.speech: The bass is right; 'tis the base knave that jars. [Aside]  How fiery and forward our pedant is! Now, for my life, the knave doth court my love. Pedascule, I'll watch you better yet.
lines: The bass is right; 'tis the base knave that jars.
[Aside]  How fiery and forward our pedant is!
Now, for my life, the knave doth court my love.
Pedascule, I'll watch you better yet.

speeches.speech: In time I may believe, yet I mistrust.
lines: In time I may believe, yet I mistrust.

speeches.speech: Mistrust it not- for sure, AEacides Was Ajax, call'd so from his grandfather.
lines: Mistrust it not- for sure, AEacides
Was Ajax, call'd so from his grandfather.

speeches.speech: I must believe my master; else, I promise you, I should be arguing still upon that doubt; But let it rest. Now, Licio, to you. Good master, take it not unkindly, pray, That I have been thus pleasant with you both.
lines: I must believe my master; else, I promise you,
I should be arguing still upon that doubt;
But let it rest. Now, Licio, to you.
Good master, take it not unkindly, pray,
That I have been thus pleasant with you both.

speeches.speech: [To LUCENTIO]  You may go walk and give me leave awhile; My lessons make no music in three Parts.
lines: [To LUCENTIO]  You may go walk and give me leave
awhile;
My lessons make no music in three Parts.

speeches.speech: Are you so formal, sir? Well, I must wait, [Aside]  And watch withal; for, but I be deceiv'd, Our fine musician groweth amorous.
lines: Are you so formal, sir? Well, I must wait,
[Aside]  And watch withal; for, but I be deceiv'd,
Our fine musician groweth amorous.

speeches.speech: Madam, before you touch the instrument To learn the order of my fingering, I must begin with rudiments of art, To teach you gamut in a briefer sort, More pleasant, pithy, and effectual, Than hath been taught by any of my trade; And there it is in writing fairly drawn.
lines: Madam, before you touch the instrument
To learn the order of my fingering,
I must begin with rudiments of art,
To teach you gamut in a briefer sort,
More pleasant, pithy, and effectual,
Than hath been taught by any of my trade;
And there it is in writing fairly drawn.

speeches.speech: Why, I am past my gamut long ago.
lines: Why, I am past my gamut long ago.

speeches.speech: Yet read the gamut of Hortensio.
lines: Yet read the gamut of Hortensio.

speeches.speech: [Reads] '"Gamut" I am, the ground of all accord- "A re" to plead Hortensio's passion- "B mi" Bianca, take him for thy lord- "C fa ut" that loves with all affection- "D sol re" one clef, two notes have I- "E la mi" show pity or I die.' Call you this gamut? Tut, I like it not! Old fashions please me best; I am not so nice To change true rules for odd inventions.
lines: [Reads]
'"Gamut" I am, the ground of all accord-
"A re" to plead Hortensio's passion-
"B mi" Bianca, take him for thy lord-
"C fa ut" that loves with all affection-
"D sol re" one clef, two notes have I-
"E la mi" show pity or I die.'
Call you this gamut? Tut, I like it not!
Old fashions please me best; I am not so nice
To change true rules for odd inventions.

speeches.speech: Mistress, your father prays you leave your books And help to dress your sister's chamber up. You know to-morrow is the wedding-day.
lines: Mistress, your father prays you leave your books
And help to dress your sister's chamber up.
You know to-morrow is the wedding-day.

speeches.speech: Farewell, sweet masters, both; I must be gone.
lines: Farewell, sweet masters, both; I must be gone.

speeches.speech: Faith, mistress, then I have no cause to stay.
lines: Faith, mistress, then I have no cause to stay.

speeches.speech: But I have cause to pry into this pedant; Methinks he looks as though he were in love. Yet if thy thoughts, Bianca, be so humble To cast thy wand'ring eyes on every stale- Seize thee that list. If once I find thee ranging, Hortensio will be quit with thee by changing.        Exit
lines: But I have cause to pry into this pedant;
Methinks he looks as though he were in love.
Yet if thy thoughts, Bianca, be so humble
To cast thy wand'ring eyes on every stale-
Seize thee that list. If once I find thee ranging,
Hortensio will be quit with thee by changing.        Exit

speeches.speech: [To TRANIO]  Signior Lucentio, this is the 'pointed day That Katherine and Petruchio should be married, And yet we hear not of our son-in-law. What will be said? What mockery will it be To want the bridegroom when the priest attends To speak the ceremonial rites of marriage! What says Lucentio to this shame of ours?
lines: [To TRANIO]  Signior Lucentio, this is the 'pointed day
That Katherine and Petruchio should be married,
And yet we hear not of our son-in-law.
What will be said? What mockery will it be
To want the bridegroom when the priest attends
To speak the ceremonial rites of marriage!
What says Lucentio to this shame of ours?

speeches.speech: No shame but mine; I must, forsooth, be forc'd To give my hand, oppos'd against my heart, Unto a mad-brain rudesby, full of spleen, Who woo'd in haste and means to wed at leisure. I told you, I, he was a frantic fool, Hiding his bitter jests in blunt behaviour; And, to be noted for a merry man, He'll woo a thousand, 'point the day of marriage, Make friends invited, and proclaim the banns; Yet never means to wed where he hath woo'd. Now must the world point at poor Katherine, And say 'Lo, there is mad Petruchio's wife, If it would please him come and marry her!'
lines: No shame but mine; I must, forsooth, be forc'd
To give my hand, oppos'd against my heart,
Unto a mad-brain rudesby, full of spleen,
Who woo'd in haste and means to wed at leisure.
I told you, I, he was a frantic fool,
Hiding his bitter jests in blunt behaviour;
And, to be noted for a merry man,
He'll woo a thousand, 'point the day of marriage,
Make friends invited, and proclaim the banns;
Yet never means to wed where he hath woo'd.
Now must the world point at poor Katherine,
And say 'Lo, there is mad Petruchio's wife,
If it would please him come and marry her!'

speeches.speech: Patience, good Katherine, and Baptista too. Upon my life, Petruchio means but well, Whatever fortune stays him from his word. Though he be blunt, I know him passing wise; Though he be merry, yet withal he's honest.
lines: Patience, good Katherine, and Baptista too.
Upon my life, Petruchio means but well,
Whatever fortune stays him from his word.
Though he be blunt, I know him passing wise;
Though he be merry, yet withal he's honest.

speeches.speech: Would Katherine had never seen him though!
lines: Would Katherine had never seen him though!

speeches.speech: Go, girl, I cannot blame thee now to weep, For such an injury would vex a very saint; Much more a shrew of thy impatient humour. [Enter BIONDELLO] Master, master! News, and such old news as you never heard of!
lines: Go, girl, I cannot blame thee now to weep,
For such an injury would vex a very saint;
Much more a shrew of thy impatient humour.
[Enter BIONDELLO]
Master, master! News, and such old news as you never heard of!

speeches.speech: Is it new and old too? How may that be?
lines: Is it new and old too? How may that be?

speeches.speech: Why, is it not news to hear of Petruchio's coming?
lines: Why, is it not news to hear of Petruchio's coming?

speeches.speech: Is he come?
lines: Is he come?

speeches.speech: Why, no, sir.
lines: Why, no, sir.

speeches.speech: What then?
lines: What then?

speeches.speech: He is coming.
lines: He is coming.

speeches.speech: When will he be here?
lines: When will he be here?

speeches.speech: When he stands where I am and sees you there.
lines: When he stands where I am and sees you there.

speeches.speech: But, say, what to thine old news?
lines: But, say, what to thine old news?

speeches.speech: Why, Petruchio is coming- in a new hat and an old jerkin; a pair of old breeches thrice turn'd; a pair of boots that have been candle-cases, one buckled, another lac'd; an old rusty sword ta'en out of the town armoury, with a broken hilt, and chapeless; with two broken points; his horse hipp'd, with an old motley saddle and stirrups of no kindred; besides, possess'd with the glanders and like to mose in the chine, troubled with the lampass, infected with the fashions, full of windgalls, sped with spavins, rayed with the yellows, past cure of the fives, stark spoil'd with the staggers, begnawn with the bots, sway'd in the back and shoulder-shotten, near-legg'd before, and with a half-cheek'd bit, and a head-stall of sheep's leather which, being restrained to keep him from stumbling, hath been often burst, and now repaired with knots; one girth six times piec'd, and a woman's crupper of velure, which hath two letters for her name fairly set down in studs, and here and there piec'd with pack-thread.
lines: Why, Petruchio is coming- in a new hat and an old
jerkin; a pair of old breeches thrice turn'd; a pair of boots
that have been candle-cases, one buckled, another lac'd; an old
rusty sword ta'en out of the town armoury, with a broken hilt,
and chapeless; with two broken points; his horse hipp'd, with an
old motley saddle and stirrups of no kindred; besides, possess'd
with the glanders and like to mose in the chine, troubled with
the lampass, infected with the fashions, full of windgalls, sped
with spavins, rayed with the yellows, past cure of the fives,
stark spoil'd with the staggers, begnawn with the bots, sway'd in
the back and shoulder-shotten, near-legg'd before, and with a
half-cheek'd bit, and a head-stall of sheep's leather which,
being restrained to keep him from stumbling, hath been often
burst, and now repaired with knots; one girth six times piec'd,
and a woman's crupper of velure, which hath two letters for her
name fairly set down in studs, and here and there piec'd with
pack-thread.

speeches.speech: Who comes with him?
lines: Who comes with him?

speeches.speech: O, sir, his lackey, for all the world caparison'd like the horse- with a linen stock on one leg and a kersey boot-hose on the other, gart'red with a red and blue list; an old hat, and the humour of forty fancies prick'd in't for a feather; a monster, a very monster in apparel, and not like a Christian footboy or a gentleman's lackey.
lines: O, sir, his lackey, for all the world caparison'd like
the horse- with a linen stock on one leg and a kersey boot-hose
on the other, gart'red with a red and blue list; an old hat, and
the humour of forty fancies prick'd in't for a feather; a
monster, a very monster in apparel, and not like a Christian
footboy or a gentleman's lackey.

speeches.speech: 'Tis some odd humour pricks him to this fashion; Yet oftentimes lie goes but mean-apparell'd.
lines: 'Tis some odd humour pricks him to this fashion;
Yet oftentimes lie goes but mean-apparell'd.

speeches.speech: I am glad he's come, howsoe'er he comes.
lines: I am glad he's come, howsoe'er he comes.

speeches.speech: Why, sir, he comes not.
lines: Why, sir, he comes not.

speeches.speech: Didst thou not say he comes?
lines: Didst thou not say he comes?

speeches.speech: Who? that Petruchio came?
lines: Who? that Petruchio came?

speeches.speech: Ay, that Petruchio came.
lines: Ay, that Petruchio came.

speeches.speech: No, sir; I say his horse comes with him on his back.
lines: No, sir; I say his horse comes with him on his back.

speeches.speech: Why, that's all one.
lines: Why, that's all one.

speeches.speech: Nay, by Saint Jamy, I hold you a penny, A horse and a man Is more than one, And yet not many.
lines: Nay, by Saint Jamy,
I hold you a penny,
A horse and a man
Is more than one,
And yet not many.

speeches.speech: Come, where be these gallants? Who's at home?
lines: Come, where be these gallants? Who's at home?

speeches.speech: You are welcome, sir.
lines: You are welcome, sir.

speeches.speech: And yet I come not well.
lines: And yet I come not well.

speeches.speech: And yet you halt not.
lines: And yet you halt not.

speeches.speech: Not so well apparell'd As I wish you were.
lines: Not so well apparell'd
As I wish you were.

speeches.speech: Were it better, I should rush in thus. But where is Kate? Where is my lovely bride? How does my father? Gentles, methinks you frown; And wherefore gaze this goodly company As if they saw some wondrous monument, Some comet or unusual prodigy?
lines: Were it better, I should rush in thus.
But where is Kate? Where is my lovely bride?
How does my father? Gentles, methinks you frown;
And wherefore gaze this goodly company
As if they saw some wondrous monument,
Some comet or unusual prodigy?

speeches.speech: Why, sir, you know this is your wedding-day. First were we sad, fearing you would not come; Now sadder, that you come so unprovided. Fie, doff this habit, shame to your estate, An eye-sore to our solemn festival!
lines: Why, sir, you know this is your wedding-day.
First were we sad, fearing you would not come;
Now sadder, that you come so unprovided.
Fie, doff this habit, shame to your estate,
An eye-sore to our solemn festival!

speeches.speech: And tell us what occasion of import Hath all so long detain'd you from your wife, And sent you hither so unlike yourself?
lines: And tell us what occasion of import
Hath all so long detain'd you from your wife,
And sent you hither so unlike yourself?

speeches.speech: Tedious it were to tell, and harsh to hear; Sufficeth I am come to keep my word, Though in some part enforced to digress, Which at more leisure I will so excuse As you shall well be satisfied withal. But where is Kate? I stay too long from her; The morning wears, 'tis time we were at church.
lines: Tedious it were to tell, and harsh to hear;
Sufficeth I am come to keep my word,
Though in some part enforced to digress,
Which at more leisure I will so excuse
As you shall well be satisfied withal.
But where is Kate? I stay too long from her;
The morning wears, 'tis time we were at church.

speeches.speech: See not your bride in these unreverent robes; Go to my chamber, put on clothes of mine.
lines: See not your bride in these unreverent robes;
Go to my chamber, put on clothes of mine.

speeches.speech: Not I, believe me; thus I'll visit her.
lines: Not I, believe me; thus I'll visit her.

speeches.speech: But thus, I trust, you will not marry her.
lines: But thus, I trust, you will not marry her.

speeches.speech: Good sooth, even thus; therefore ha' done with words; To me she's married, not unto my clothes. Could I repair what she will wear in me As I can change these poor accoutrements, 'Twere well for Kate and better for myself. But what a fool am I to chat with you, When I should bid good-morrow to my bride And seal the title with a lovely kiss!
lines: Good sooth, even thus; therefore ha' done with words;
To me she's married, not unto my clothes.
Could I repair what she will wear in me
As I can change these poor accoutrements,
'Twere well for Kate and better for myself.
But what a fool am I to chat with you,
When I should bid good-morrow to my bride
And seal the title with a lovely kiss!

speeches.speech: He hath some meaning in his mad attire. We will persuade him, be it possible, To put on better ere he go to church.
lines: He hath some meaning in his mad attire.
We will persuade him, be it possible,
To put on better ere he go to church.

speeches.speech: I'll after him and see the event of this.
lines: I'll after him and see the event of this.

speeches.speech: But to her love concerneth us to add Her father's liking; which to bring to pass, As I before imparted to your worship, I am to get a man- whate'er he be It skills not much; we'll fit him to our turn- And he shall be Vincentio of Pisa, And make assurance here in Padua Of greater sums than I have promised. So shall you quietly enjoy your hope And marry sweet Bianca with consent.
lines: But to her love concerneth us to add
Her father's liking; which to bring to pass,
As I before imparted to your worship,
I am to get a man- whate'er he be
It skills not much; we'll fit him to our turn-
And he shall be Vincentio of Pisa,
And make assurance here in Padua
Of greater sums than I have promised.
So shall you quietly enjoy your hope
And marry sweet Bianca with consent.

speeches.speech: Were it not that my fellow schoolmaster Doth watch Bianca's steps so narrowly, 'Twere good, methinks, to steal our marriage; Which once perform'd, let all the world say no, I'll keep mine own despite of all the world.
lines: Were it not that my fellow schoolmaster
Doth watch Bianca's steps so narrowly,
'Twere good, methinks, to steal our marriage;
Which once perform'd, let all the world say no,
I'll keep mine own despite of all the world.

speeches.speech: That by degrees we mean to look into And watch our vantage in this business; We'll over-reach the greybeard, Gremio, The narrow-prying father, Minola, The quaint musician, amorous Licio- All for my master's sake, Lucentio. [Re-enter GREMIO] Signior Gremio, came you from the church?
lines: That by degrees we mean to look into
And watch our vantage in this business;
We'll over-reach the greybeard, Gremio,
The narrow-prying father, Minola,
The quaint musician, amorous Licio-
All for my master's sake, Lucentio.
[Re-enter GREMIO]
Signior Gremio, came you from the church?

speeches.speech: As willingly as e'er I came from school.
lines: As willingly as e'er I came from school.

speeches.speech: And is the bride and bridegroom coming home?
lines: And is the bride and bridegroom coming home?

speeches.speech: A bridegroom, say you? 'Tis a groom indeed, A grumbling groom, and that the girl shall find.
lines: A bridegroom, say you? 'Tis a groom indeed,
A grumbling groom, and that the girl shall find.

speeches.speech: Curster than she? Why, 'tis impossible.
lines: Curster than she? Why, 'tis impossible.

speeches.speech: Why, he's a devil, a devil, a very fiend.
lines: Why, he's a devil, a devil, a very fiend.

speeches.speech: Why, she's a devil, a devil, the devil's dam.
lines: Why, she's a devil, a devil, the devil's dam.

speeches.speech: Tut, she's a lamb, a dove, a fool, to him! I'll tell you, Sir Lucentio: when the priest Should ask if Katherine should be his wife, 'Ay, by gogs-wouns' quoth he, and swore so loud That, all amaz'd, the priest let fall the book; And as he stoop'd again to take it up, This mad-brain'd bridegroom took him such a cuff That down fell priest and book, and book and priest. 'Now take them up,' quoth he 'if any list.'
lines: Tut, she's a lamb, a dove, a fool, to him!
I'll tell you, Sir Lucentio: when the priest
Should ask if Katherine should be his wife,
'Ay, by gogs-wouns' quoth he, and swore so loud
That, all amaz'd, the priest let fall the book;
And as he stoop'd again to take it up,
This mad-brain'd bridegroom took him such a cuff
That down fell priest and book, and book and priest.
'Now take them up,' quoth he 'if any list.'

speeches.speech: What said the wench, when he rose again?
lines: What said the wench, when he rose again?

speeches.speech: Trembled and shook, for why he stamp'd and swore As if the vicar meant to cozen him. But after many ceremonies done He calls for wine: 'A health!' quoth he, as if He had been abroad, carousing to his mates After a storm; quaff'd off the muscadel, And threw the sops all in the sexton's face, Having no other reason But that his beard grew thin and hungerly And seem'd to ask him sops as he was drinking. This done, he took the bride about the neck, And kiss'd her lips with such a clamorous smack That at the parting all the church did echo. And I, seeing this, came thence for very shame; And after me, I know, the rout is coming. Such a mad marriage never was before. Hark, hark! I hear the minstrels play.    [Music plays] Enter PETRUCHIO, KATHERINA, BIANCA, BAPTISTA, HORTENSIO, GRUMIO, and train
lines: Trembled and shook, for why he stamp'd and swore
As if the vicar meant to cozen him.
But after many ceremonies done
He calls for wine: 'A health!' quoth he, as if
He had been abroad, carousing to his mates
After a storm; quaff'd off the muscadel,
And threw the sops all in the sexton's face,
Having no other reason
But that his beard grew thin and hungerly
And seem'd to ask him sops as he was drinking.
This done, he took the bride about the neck,
And kiss'd her lips with such a clamorous smack
That at the parting all the church did echo.
And I, seeing this, came thence for very shame;
And after me, I know, the rout is coming.
Such a mad marriage never was before.
Hark, hark! I hear the minstrels play.    [Music plays]
Enter PETRUCHIO, KATHERINA, BIANCA, BAPTISTA, HORTENSIO, GRUMIO, and train

speeches.speech: Gentlemen and friends, I thank you for your pains. I know you think to dine with me to-day, And have prepar'd great store of wedding cheer But so it is- my haste doth call me hence, And therefore here I mean to take my leave.
lines: Gentlemen and friends, I thank you for your pains.
I know you think to dine with me to-day,
And have prepar'd great store of wedding cheer
But so it is- my haste doth call me hence,
And therefore here I mean to take my leave.

speeches.speech: Is't possible you will away to-night?
lines: Is't possible you will away to-night?

speeches.speech: I must away to-day before night come. Make it no wonder; if you knew my business, You would entreat me rather go than stay. And, honest company, I thank you all That have beheld me give away myself To this most patient, sweet, and virtuous wife. Dine with my father, drink a health to me. For I must hence; and farewell to you all.
lines: I must away to-day before night come.
Make it no wonder; if you knew my business,
You would entreat me rather go than stay.
And, honest company, I thank you all
That have beheld me give away myself
To this most patient, sweet, and virtuous wife.
Dine with my father, drink a health to me.
For I must hence; and farewell to you all.

speeches.speech: Let us entreat you stay till after dinner.
lines: Let us entreat you stay till after dinner.

speeches.speech: It may not be.
lines: It may not be.

speeches.speech: Let me entreat you.
lines: Let me entreat you.

speeches.speech: It cannot be.
lines: It cannot be.

speeches.speech: Let me entreat you.
lines: Let me entreat you.

speeches.speech: I am content.
lines: I am content.

speeches.speech: Are you content to stay?
lines: Are you content to stay?

speeches.speech: I am content you shall entreat me stay; But yet not stay, entreat me how you can.
lines: I am content you shall entreat me stay;
But yet not stay, entreat me how you can.

speeches.speech: Now, if you love me, stay.
lines: Now, if you love me, stay.

speeches.speech: Grumio, my horse.
lines: Grumio, my horse.

speeches.speech: Ay, sir, they be ready; the oats have eaten the horses.
lines: Ay, sir, they be ready; the oats have eaten the horses.

speeches.speech: Nay, then, Do what thou canst, I will not go to-day; No, nor to-morrow, not till I please myself. The door is open, sir; there lies your way; You may be jogging whiles your boots are green; For me, I'll not be gone till I please myself. 'Tis like you'll prove a jolly surly groom That take it on you at the first so roundly.
lines: Nay, then,
Do what thou canst, I will not go to-day;
No, nor to-morrow, not till I please myself.
The door is open, sir; there lies your way;
You may be jogging whiles your boots are green;
For me, I'll not be gone till I please myself.
'Tis like you'll prove a jolly surly groom
That take it on you at the first so roundly.

speeches.speech: O Kate, content thee; prithee be not angry.
lines: O Kate, content thee; prithee be not angry.

speeches.speech: I will be angry; what hast thou to do? Father, be quiet; he shall stay my leisure.
lines: I will be angry; what hast thou to do?
Father, be quiet; he shall stay my leisure.

speeches.speech: Ay, marry, sir, now it begins to work.
lines: Ay, marry, sir, now it begins to work.

speeches.speech: Gentlemen, forward to the bridal dinner. I see a woman may be made a fool If she had not a spirit to resist.
lines: Gentlemen, forward to the bridal dinner.
I see a woman may be made a fool
If she had not a spirit to resist.

speeches.speech: They shall go forward, Kate, at thy command. Obey the bride, you that attend on her; Go to the feast, revel and domineer, Carouse full measure to her maidenhead; Be mad and merry, or go hang yourselves. But for my bonny Kate, she must with me. Nay, look not big, nor stamp, nor stare, nor fret; I will be master of what is mine own- She is my goods, my chattels, she is my house, My household stuff, my field, my barn, My horse, my ox, my ass, my any thing, And here she stands; touch her whoever dare; I'll bring mine action on the proudest he That stops my way in Padua. Grumio, Draw forth thy weapon; we are beset with thieves; Rescue thy mistress, if thou be a man. Fear not, sweet wench; they shall not touch thee, Kate; I'll buckler thee against a million.
lines: They shall go forward, Kate, at thy command.
Obey the bride, you that attend on her;
Go to the feast, revel and domineer,
Carouse full measure to her maidenhead;
Be mad and merry, or go hang yourselves.
But for my bonny Kate, she must with me.
Nay, look not big, nor stamp, nor stare, nor fret;
I will be master of what is mine own-
She is my goods, my chattels, she is my house,
My household stuff, my field, my barn,
My horse, my ox, my ass, my any thing,
And here she stands; touch her whoever dare;
I'll bring mine action on the proudest he
That stops my way in Padua. Grumio,
Draw forth thy weapon; we are beset with thieves;
Rescue thy mistress, if thou be a man.
Fear not, sweet wench; they shall not touch thee, Kate;
I'll buckler thee against a million.

speeches.speech: Nay, let them go, a couple of quiet ones.
lines: Nay, let them go, a couple of quiet ones.

speeches.speech: Went they not quickly, I should die with laughing.
lines: Went they not quickly, I should die with laughing.

speeches.speech: Of all mad matches, never was the like.
lines: Of all mad matches, never was the like.

speeches.speech: Mistress, what's your opinion of your sister?
lines: Mistress, what's your opinion of your sister?

speeches.speech: That, being mad herself, she's madly mated.
lines: That, being mad herself, she's madly mated.

speeches.speech: I warrant him, Petruchio is Kated.
lines: I warrant him, Petruchio is Kated.

speeches.speech: Neighbours and friends, though bride and bridegroom wants For to supply the places at the table, You know there wants no junkets at the feast. Lucentio, you shall supply the bridegroom's place; And let Bianca take her sister's room.
lines: Neighbours and friends, though bride and bridegroom wants
For to supply the places at the table,
You know there wants no junkets at the feast.
Lucentio, you shall supply the bridegroom's place;
And let Bianca take her sister's room.

speeches.speech: Shall sweet Bianca practise how to bride it?
lines: Shall sweet Bianca practise how to bride it?

speeches.speech: She shall, Lucentio. Come, gentlemen, let's go.
lines: She shall, Lucentio. Come, gentlemen, let's go.

speeches.speech: Fie, fie on all tired jades, on all mad masters, and all foul ways! Was ever man so beaten? Was ever man so ray'd? Was ever man so weary? I am sent before to make a fire, and they are coming after to warm them. Now were not I a little pot and soon hot, my very lips might freeze to my teeth, my tongue to the roof of my mouth, my heart in my belly, ere I should come by a fire to thaw me. But I with blowing the fire shall warm myself; for, considering the weather, a taller man than I will take cold. Holla, ho! Curtis!
lines: Fie, fie on all tired jades, on all mad masters, and all
foul ways! Was ever man so beaten? Was ever man so ray'd? Was
ever man so weary? I am sent before to make a fire, and they are
coming after to warm them. Now were not I a little pot and soon
hot, my very lips might freeze to my teeth, my tongue to the roof
of my mouth, my heart in my belly, ere I should come by a fire to
thaw me. But I with blowing the fire shall warm myself; for,
considering the weather, a taller man than I will take cold.
Holla, ho! Curtis!

speeches.speech: Who is that calls so coldly?
lines: Who is that calls so coldly?

speeches.speech: A piece of ice. If thou doubt it, thou mayst slide from my shoulder to my heel with no greater a run but my head and my neck. A fire, good Curtis.
lines: A piece of ice. If thou doubt it, thou mayst slide from my
shoulder to my heel with no greater a run but my head and my
neck. A fire, good Curtis.

speeches.speech: Is my master and his wife coming, Grumio?
lines: Is my master and his wife coming, Grumio?

speeches.speech: O, ay, Curtis, ay; and therefore fire, fire; cast on no water.
lines: O, ay, Curtis, ay; and therefore fire, fire; cast on no
water.

speeches.speech: Is she so hot a shrew as she's reported?
lines: Is she so hot a shrew as she's reported?

speeches.speech: She was, good Curtis, before this frost; but thou know'st winter tames man, woman, and beast; for it hath tam'd my old master, and my new mistress, and myself, fellow Curtis.
lines: She was, good Curtis, before this frost; but thou know'st
winter tames man, woman, and beast; for it hath tam'd my old
master, and my new mistress, and myself, fellow Curtis.

speeches.speech: Away, you three-inch fool! I am no beast.
lines: Away, you three-inch fool! I am no beast.

speeches.speech: Am I but three inches? Why, thy horn is a foot, and so long am I at the least. But wilt thou make a fire, or shall I complain on thee to our mistress, whose hand- she being now at hand- thou shalt soon feel, to thy cold comfort, for being slow in thy hot office?
lines: Am I but three inches? Why, thy horn is a foot, and so long
am I at the least. But wilt thou make a fire, or shall I complain
on thee to our mistress, whose hand- she being now at hand- thou
shalt soon feel, to thy cold comfort, for being slow in thy hot
office?

speeches.speech: I prithee, good Grumio, tell me how goes the world?
lines: I prithee, good Grumio, tell me how goes the world?

speeches.speech: A cold world, Curtis, in every office but thine; and therefore fire. Do thy duty, and have thy duty, for my master and mistress are almost frozen to death.
lines: A cold world, Curtis, in every office but thine; and
therefore fire. Do thy duty, and have thy duty, for my master and
mistress are almost frozen to death.

speeches.speech: There's fire ready; and therefore, good Grumio, the news?
lines: There's fire ready; and therefore, good Grumio, the news?

speeches.speech: Why, 'Jack boy! ho, boy!' and as much news as thou wilt.
lines: Why, 'Jack boy! ho, boy!' and as much news as thou wilt.

speeches.speech: Come, you are so full of cony-catching!
lines: Come, you are so full of cony-catching!

speeches.speech: Why, therefore, fire; for I have caught extreme cold. Where's the cook? Is supper ready, the house trimm'd, rushes strew'd, cobwebs swept, the serving-men in their new fustian, their white stockings, and every officer his wedding-garment on? Be the jacks fair within, the jills fair without, the carpets laid, and everything in order?
lines: Why, therefore, fire; for I have caught extreme cold.
Where's the cook? Is supper ready, the house trimm'd, rushes
strew'd, cobwebs swept, the serving-men in their new fustian,
their white stockings, and every officer his wedding-garment on?
Be the jacks fair within, the jills fair without, the carpets
laid, and everything in order?

speeches.speech: All ready; and therefore, I pray thee, news.
lines: All ready; and therefore, I pray thee, news.

speeches.speech: First know my horse is tired; my master and mistress fall'n out.
lines: First know my horse is tired; my master and mistress fall'n
out.

speeches.speech: How?
lines: How?

speeches.speech: Out of their saddles into the dirt; and thereby hangs a tale.
lines: Out of their saddles into the dirt; and thereby hangs a
tale.

speeches.speech: Let's ha't, good Grumio.
lines: Let's ha't, good Grumio.

speeches.speech: Lend thine ear.
lines: Lend thine ear.

speeches.speech: Here.
lines: Here.

speeches.speech: There.                             [Striking him]
lines: There.                             [Striking him]

speeches.speech: This 'tis to feel a tale, not to hear a tale.
lines: This 'tis to feel a tale, not to hear a tale.

speeches.speech: And therefore 'tis call'd a sensible tale; and this cuff was but to knock at your car and beseech list'ning. Now I begin: Imprimis, we came down a foul hill, my master riding behind my mistress-
lines: And therefore 'tis call'd a sensible tale; and this cuff
was but to knock at your car and beseech list'ning. Now I begin:
Imprimis, we came down a foul hill, my master riding behind my
mistress-

speeches.speech: Both of one horse?
lines: Both of one horse?

speeches.speech: What's that to thee?
lines: What's that to thee?

speeches.speech: Why, a horse.
lines: Why, a horse.

speeches.speech: Tell thou the tale. But hadst thou not cross'd me, thou shouldst have heard how her horse fell and she under her horse; thou shouldst have heard in how miry a place, how she was bemoil'd, how he left her with the horse upon her, how he beat me because her horse stumbled, how she waded through the dirt to pluck him off me, how he swore, how she pray'd that never pray'd before, how I cried, how the horses ran away, how her bridle was burst, how I lost my crupper- with many things of worthy memory, which now shall die in oblivion, and thou return unexperienc'd to thy grave.
lines: Tell thou the tale. But hadst thou not cross'd me, thou
shouldst have heard how her horse fell and she under her horse;
thou shouldst have heard in how miry a place, how she was
bemoil'd, how he left her with the horse upon her, how he beat me
because her horse stumbled, how she waded through the dirt to
pluck him off me, how he swore, how she pray'd that never pray'd
before, how I cried, how the horses ran away, how her bridle was
burst, how I lost my crupper- with many things of worthy memory,
which now shall die in oblivion, and thou return unexperienc'd to
thy grave.

speeches.speech: By this reck'ning he is more shrew than she.
lines: By this reck'ning he is more shrew than she.

speeches.speech: Ay, and that thou and the proudest of you all shall find when he comes home. But what talk I of this? Call forth Nathaniel, Joseph, Nicholas, Philip, Walter, Sugarsop, and the rest; let their heads be sleekly comb'd, their blue coats brush'd and their garters of an indifferent knit; let them curtsy with their left legs, and not presume to touch a hair of my mastcr's horse-tail till they kiss their hands. Are they all ready?
lines: Ay, and that thou and the proudest of you all shall find
when he comes home. But what talk I of this? Call forth
Nathaniel, Joseph, Nicholas, Philip, Walter, Sugarsop, and the
rest; let their heads be sleekly comb'd, their blue coats brush'd
and their garters of an indifferent knit; let them curtsy with
their left legs, and not presume to touch a hair of my mastcr's
horse-tail till they kiss their hands. Are they all ready?

speeches.speech: They are.
lines: They are.

speeches.speech: Call them forth.
lines: Call them forth.

speeches.speech: Do you hear, ho? You must meet my master, to countenance my mistress.
lines: Do you hear, ho? You must meet my master, to countenance my
mistress.

speeches.speech: Why, she hath a face of her own.
lines: Why, she hath a face of her own.

speeches.speech: Who knows not that?
lines: Who knows not that?

speeches.speech: Thou, it seems, that calls for company to countenance her.
lines: Thou, it seems, that calls for company to countenance her.

speeches.speech: I call them forth to credit her.
lines: I call them forth to credit her.

speeches.speech: Why, she comes to borrow nothing of them.
lines: Why, she comes to borrow nothing of them.

speeches.speech: Welcome home, Grumio!
lines: Welcome home, Grumio!

speeches.speech: How now, Grumio!
lines: How now, Grumio!

speeches.speech: What, Grumio!
lines: What, Grumio!

speeches.speech: Fellow Grumio!
lines: Fellow Grumio!

speeches.speech: How now, old lad!
lines: How now, old lad!

speeches.speech: Welcome, you!- how now, you!- what, you!- fellow, you!- and thus much for greeting. Now, my spruce companions, is all ready, and all things neat?
lines: Welcome, you!- how now, you!- what, you!- fellow, you!- and
thus much for greeting. Now, my spruce companions, is all ready,
and all things neat?

speeches.speech: All things is ready. How near is our master?
lines: All things is ready. How near is our master?

speeches.speech: E'en at hand, alighted by this; and therefore be not- Cock's passion, silence! I hear my master.
lines: E'en at hand, alighted by this; and therefore be not-
Cock's passion, silence! I hear my master.

speeches.speech: Where be these knaves? What, no man at door To hold my stirrup nor to take my horse! Where is Nathaniel, Gregory, Philip?
lines: Where be these knaves? What, no man at door
To hold my stirrup nor to take my horse!
Where is Nathaniel, Gregory, Philip?

speeches.speech: Here, here, sir; here, sir.
lines: Here, here, sir; here, sir.

speeches.speech: Here, sir! here, sir! here, sir! here, sir! You logger-headed and unpolish'd grooms! What, no attendance? no regard? no duty? Where is the foolish knave I sent before?
lines: Here, sir! here, sir! here, sir! here, sir!
You logger-headed and unpolish'd grooms!
What, no attendance? no regard? no duty?
Where is the foolish knave I sent before?

speeches.speech: Here, sir; as foolish as I was before.
lines: Here, sir; as foolish as I was before.

speeches.speech: YOU peasant swain! you whoreson malt-horse drudge! Did I not bid thee meet me in the park And bring along these rascal knaves with thee?
lines: YOU peasant swain! you whoreson malt-horse drudge!
Did I not bid thee meet me in the park
And bring along these rascal knaves with thee?

speeches.speech: Nathaniel's coat, sir, was not fully made, And Gabriel's pumps were all unpink'd i' th' heel; There was no link to colour Peter's hat, And Walter's dagger was not come from sheathing; There were none fine but Adam, Ralph, and Gregory; The rest were ragged, old, and beggarly; Yet, as they are, here are they come to meet you.
lines: Nathaniel's coat, sir, was not fully made,
And Gabriel's pumps were all unpink'd i' th' heel;
There was no link to colour Peter's hat,
And Walter's dagger was not come from sheathing;
There were none fine but Adam, Ralph, and Gregory;
The rest were ragged, old, and beggarly;
Yet, as they are, here are they come to meet you.

speeches.speech: Go, rascals, go and fetch my supper in. [Exeunt some of the SERVINGMEN] [Sings]  Where is the life that late I led? Where are those- Sit down, Kate, and welcome. Soud, soud, soud, soud! [Re-enter SERVANTS with supper] Why, when, I say? Nay, good sweet Kate, be merry. Off with my boots, you rogues! you villains, when? [Sings]  It was the friar of orders grey, As he forth walked on his way- Out, you rogue! you pluck my foot awry; Take that, and mend the plucking off the other. [Strikes him] Be merry, Kate. Some water, here, what, ho! [Enter one with water] Where's my spaniel Troilus? Sirrah, get you hence, And bid my cousin Ferdinand come hither: [Exit SERVINGMAN] One, Kate, that you must kiss and be acquainted with. Where are my slippers? Shall I have some water? Come, Kate, and wash, and welcome heartily. You whoreson villain! will you let it fall?    [Strikes him]
lines: Go, rascals, go and fetch my supper in.
[Exeunt some of the SERVINGMEN]
[Sings]  Where is the life that late I led?
Where are those-
Sit down, Kate, and welcome. Soud, soud, soud, soud!
[Re-enter SERVANTS with supper]
Why, when, I say? Nay, good sweet Kate, be merry.
Off with my boots, you rogues! you villains, when?
[Sings]  It was the friar of orders grey,
As he forth walked on his way-
Out, you rogue! you pluck my foot awry;
Take that, and mend the plucking off the other.
[Strikes him]
Be merry, Kate. Some water, here, what, ho!
[Enter one with water]
Where's my spaniel Troilus? Sirrah, get you hence,
And bid my cousin Ferdinand come hither:
[Exit SERVINGMAN]
One, Kate, that you must kiss and be acquainted with.
Where are my slippers? Shall I have some water?
Come, Kate, and wash, and welcome heartily.
You whoreson villain! will you let it fall?    [Strikes him]

speeches.speech: Patience, I pray you; 'twas a fault unwilling.
lines: Patience, I pray you; 'twas a fault unwilling.

speeches.speech: A whoreson, beetle-headed, flap-ear'd knave! Come, Kate, sit down; I know you have a stomach. Will you give thanks, sweet Kate, or else shall I? What's this? Mutton?
lines: A whoreson, beetle-headed, flap-ear'd knave!
Come, Kate, sit down; I know you have a stomach.
Will you give thanks, sweet Kate, or else shall I?
What's this? Mutton?

speeches.speech: Ay.
lines: Ay.

speeches.speech: Who brought it?
lines: Who brought it?

speeches.speech: I.
lines: I.

speeches.speech: 'Tis burnt; and so is all the meat. What dogs are these? Where is the rascal cook? How durst you villains bring it from the dresser And serve it thus to me that love it not? There, take it to you, trenchers, cups, and all; [Throws the meat, etc., at them] You heedless joltheads and unmanner'd slaves! What, do you grumble? I'll be with you straight.
lines: 'Tis burnt; and so is all the meat.
What dogs are these? Where is the rascal cook?
How durst you villains bring it from the dresser
And serve it thus to me that love it not?
There, take it to you, trenchers, cups, and all;
[Throws the meat, etc., at them]
You heedless joltheads and unmanner'd slaves!
What, do you grumble? I'll be with you straight.

speeches.speech: I pray you, husband, be not so disquiet; The meat was well, if you were so contented.
lines: I pray you, husband, be not so disquiet;
The meat was well, if you were so contented.

speeches.speech: I tell thee, Kate, 'twas burnt and dried away, And I expressly am forbid to touch it; For it engenders choler, planteth anger; And better 'twere that both of us did fast, Since, of ourselves, ourselves are choleric, Than feed it with such over-roasted flesh. Be patient; to-morrow 't shall be mended. And for this night we'll fast for company. Come, I will bring thee to thy bridal chamber.   Exeunt
