lines: I tell thee, Kate, 'twas burnt and dried away,
And I expressly am forbid to touch it;
For it engenders choler, planteth anger;
And better 'twere that both of us did fast,
Since, of ourselves, ourselves are choleric,
Than feed it with such over-roasted flesh.
Be patient; to-morrow 't shall be mended.
And for this night we'll fast for company.
Come, I will bring thee to thy bridal chamber.   Exeunt

speeches.speech: Peter, didst ever see the like?
lines: Peter, didst ever see the like?

speeches.speech: He kills her in her own humour.
lines: He kills her in her own humour.

speeches.speech: Where is he?
lines: Where is he?

speeches.speech: In her chamber. Making a sermon of continency to her, And rails, and swears, and rates, that she, poor soul, Knows not which way to stand, to look, to speak. And sits as one new risen from a dream. Away, away! for he is coming hither.             Exeunt
lines: In her chamber. Making a sermon of continency to her,
And rails, and swears, and rates, that she, poor soul,
Knows not which way to stand, to look, to speak.
And sits as one new risen from a dream.
Away, away! for he is coming hither.             Exeunt

speeches.speech: Thus have I politicly begun my reign, And 'tis my hope to end successfully. My falcon now is sharp and passing empty. And till she stoop she must not be full-gorg'd, For then she never looks upon her lure. Another way I have to man my haggard, To make her come, and know her keeper's call, That is, to watch her, as we watch these kites That bate and beat, and will not be obedient. She eat no meat to-day, nor none shall eat; Last night she slept not, nor to-night she shall not; As with the meat, some undeserved fault I'll find about the making of the bed; And here I'll fling the pillow, there the bolster, This way the coverlet, another way the sheets; Ay, and amid this hurly I intend That all is done in reverend care of her- And, in conclusion, she shall watch all night; And if she chance to nod I'll rail and brawl And with the clamour keep her still awake. This is a way to kill a wife with kindness, And thus I'll curb her mad and headstrong humour. He that knows better how to tame a shrew, Now let him speak; 'tis charity to show.           Exit
lines: Thus have I politicly begun my reign,
And 'tis my hope to end successfully.
My falcon now is sharp and passing empty.
And till she stoop she must not be full-gorg'd,
For then she never looks upon her lure.
Another way I have to man my haggard,
To make her come, and know her keeper's call,
That is, to watch her, as we watch these kites
That bate and beat, and will not be obedient.
She eat no meat to-day, nor none shall eat;
Last night she slept not, nor to-night she shall not;
As with the meat, some undeserved fault
I'll find about the making of the bed;
And here I'll fling the pillow, there the bolster,
This way the coverlet, another way the sheets;
Ay, and amid this hurly I intend
That all is done in reverend care of her-
And, in conclusion, she shall watch all night;
And if she chance to nod I'll rail and brawl
And with the clamour keep her still awake.
This is a way to kill a wife with kindness,
And thus I'll curb her mad and headstrong humour.
He that knows better how to tame a shrew,
Now let him speak; 'tis charity to show.           Exit

speeches.speech: Is 't possible, friend Licio, that Mistress Bianca Doth fancy any other but Lucentio? I tell you, sir, she bears me fair in hand.
lines: Is 't possible, friend Licio, that Mistress Bianca
Doth fancy any other but Lucentio?
I tell you, sir, she bears me fair in hand.

speeches.speech: Sir, to satisfy you in what I have said, Stand by and mark the manner of his teaching.
lines: Sir, to satisfy you in what I have said,
Stand by and mark the manner of his teaching.

speeches.speech: Now, mistress, profit you in what you read?
lines: Now, mistress, profit you in what you read?

speeches.speech: What, master, read you, First resolve me that.
lines: What, master, read you, First resolve me that.

speeches.speech: I read that I profess, 'The Art to Love.'
lines: I read that I profess, 'The Art to Love.'

speeches.speech: And may you prove, sir, master of your art!
lines: And may you prove, sir, master of your art!

speeches.speech: While you, sweet dear, prove mistress of my heart.
lines: While you, sweet dear, prove mistress of my heart.

speeches.speech: Quick proceeders, marry! Now tell me, I pray, You that durst swear that your Mistress Bianca Lov'd none in the world so well as Lucentio.
lines: Quick proceeders, marry! Now tell me, I pray,
You that durst swear that your Mistress Bianca
Lov'd none in the world so well as Lucentio.

speeches.speech: O despiteful love! unconstant womankind! I tell thee, Licio, this is wonderful.
lines: O despiteful love! unconstant womankind!
I tell thee, Licio, this is wonderful.

speeches.speech: Mistake no more; I am not Licio. Nor a musician as I seem to be; But one that scorn to live in this disguise For such a one as leaves a gentleman And makes a god of such a cullion. Know, sir, that I am call'd Hortensio.
lines: Mistake no more; I am not Licio.
Nor a musician as I seem to be;
But one that scorn to live in this disguise
For such a one as leaves a gentleman
And makes a god of such a cullion.
Know, sir, that I am call'd Hortensio.

speeches.speech: Signior Hortensio, I have often heard Of your entire affection to Bianca; And since mine eyes are witness of her lightness, I will with you, if you be so contented, Forswear Bianca and her love for ever.
lines: Signior Hortensio, I have often heard
Of your entire affection to Bianca;
And since mine eyes are witness of her lightness,
I will with you, if you be so contented,
Forswear Bianca and her love for ever.

speeches.speech: See, how they kiss and court! Signior Lucentio, Here is my hand, and here I firmly vow Never to woo her more, but do forswear her, As one unworthy all the former favours That I have fondly flatter'd her withal.
lines: See, how they kiss and court! Signior Lucentio,
Here is my hand, and here I firmly vow
Never to woo her more, but do forswear her,
As one unworthy all the former favours
That I have fondly flatter'd her withal.

speeches.speech: And here I take the like unfeigned oath, Never to marry with her though she would entreat; Fie on her! See how beastly she doth court him!
lines: And here I take the like unfeigned oath,
Never to marry with her though she would entreat;
Fie on her! See how beastly she doth court him!

speeches.speech: Would all the world but he had quite forsworn! For me, that I may surely keep mine oath, I will be married to a wealtlly widow Ere three days pass, which hath as long lov'd me As I have lov'd this proud disdainful haggard. And so farewell, Signior Lucentio. Kindness in women, not their beauteous looks, Shall win my love; and so I take my leave, In resolution as I swore before.                   Exit
lines: Would all the world but he had quite forsworn!
For me, that I may surely keep mine oath,
I will be married to a wealtlly widow
Ere three days pass, which hath as long lov'd me
As I have lov'd this proud disdainful haggard.
And so farewell, Signior Lucentio.
Kindness in women, not their beauteous looks,
Shall win my love; and so I take my leave,
In resolution as I swore before.                   Exit

speeches.speech: Mistress Bianca, bless you with such grace As 'longeth to a lover's blessed case! Nay, I have ta'en you napping, gentle love, And have forsworn you with Hortensio.
lines: Mistress Bianca, bless you with such grace
As 'longeth to a lover's blessed case!
Nay, I have ta'en you napping, gentle love,
And have forsworn you with Hortensio.

speeches.speech: Tranio, you jest; but have you both forsworn me?
lines: Tranio, you jest; but have you both forsworn me?

speeches.speech: Mistress, we have.
lines: Mistress, we have.

speeches.speech: Then we are rid of Licio.
lines: Then we are rid of Licio.

speeches.speech: I' faith, he'll have a lusty widow now, That shall be woo'd and wedded in a day.
lines: I' faith, he'll have a lusty widow now,
That shall be woo'd and wedded in a day.

speeches.speech: God give him joy!
lines: God give him joy!

speeches.speech: Ay, and he'll tame her.
lines: Ay, and he'll tame her.

speeches.speech: He says so, Tranio.
lines: He says so, Tranio.

speeches.speech: Faith, he is gone unto the taming-school.
lines: Faith, he is gone unto the taming-school.

speeches.speech: The taming-school! What, is there such a place?
lines: The taming-school! What, is there such a place?

speeches.speech: Ay, mistress; and Petruchio is the master, That teacheth tricks eleven and twenty long, To tame a shrew and charm her chattering tongue.
lines: Ay, mistress; and Petruchio is the master,
That teacheth tricks eleven and twenty long,
To tame a shrew and charm her chattering tongue.

speeches.speech: O master, master I have watch'd so long That I am dog-weary; but at last I spied An ancient angel coming down the hill Will serve the turn.
lines: O master, master I have watch'd so long
That I am dog-weary; but at last I spied
An ancient angel coming down the hill
Will serve the turn.

speeches.speech: What is he, Biondello?
lines: What is he, Biondello?

speeches.speech: Master, a mercatante or a pedant, I know not what; but formal in apparel, In gait and countenance surely like a father.
lines: Master, a mercatante or a pedant,
I know not what; but formal in apparel,
In gait and countenance surely like a father.

speeches.speech: And what of him, Tranio?
lines: And what of him, Tranio?

speeches.speech: If he be credulous and trust my tale, I'll make him glad to seem Vincentio, And give assurance to Baptista Minola As if he were the right Vincentio. Take in your love, and then let me alone.
lines: If he be credulous and trust my tale,
I'll make him glad to seem Vincentio,
And give assurance to Baptista Minola
As if he were the right Vincentio.
Take in your love, and then let me alone.

speeches.speech: God save you, sir!
lines: God save you, sir!

speeches.speech: And you, sir; you are welcome. Travel you far on, or are you at the farthest?
lines: And you, sir; you are welcome.
Travel you far on, or are you at the farthest?

speeches.speech: Sir, at the farthest for a week or two; But then up farther, and as far as Rome; And so to Tripoli, if God lend me life.
lines: Sir, at the farthest for a week or two;
But then up farther, and as far as Rome;
And so to Tripoli, if God lend me life.

speeches.speech: What countryman, I pray?
lines: What countryman, I pray?

speeches.speech: Of Mantua.
lines: Of Mantua.

speeches.speech: Of Mantua, sir? Marry, God forbid, And come to Padua, careless of your life!
lines: Of Mantua, sir? Marry, God forbid,
And come to Padua, careless of your life!

speeches.speech: My life, sir! How, I pray? For that goes hard.
lines: My life, sir! How, I pray? For that goes hard.

speeches.speech: 'Tis death for any one in Mantua To come to Padua. Know you not the cause? Your ships are stay'd at Venice; and the Duke, For private quarrel 'twixt your Duke and him, Hath publish'd and proclaim'd it openly. 'Tis marvel- but that you are but newly come, You might have heard it else proclaim'd about.
lines: 'Tis death for any one in Mantua
To come to Padua. Know you not the cause?
Your ships are stay'd at Venice; and the Duke,
For private quarrel 'twixt your Duke and him,
Hath publish'd and proclaim'd it openly.
'Tis marvel- but that you are but newly come,
You might have heard it else proclaim'd about.

speeches.speech: Alas, sir, it is worse for me than so! For I have bills for money by exchange From Florence, and must here deliver them.
lines: Alas, sir, it is worse for me than so!
For I have bills for money by exchange
From Florence, and must here deliver them.

speeches.speech: Well, sir, to do you courtesy, This will I do, and this I will advise you- First, tell me, have you ever been at Pisa?
lines: Well, sir, to do you courtesy,
This will I do, and this I will advise you-
First, tell me, have you ever been at Pisa?

speeches.speech: Ay, sir, in Pisa have I often been, Pisa renowned for grave citizens.
lines: Ay, sir, in Pisa have I often been,
Pisa renowned for grave citizens.

speeches.speech: Among them know you one Vincentio?
lines: Among them know you one Vincentio?

speeches.speech: I know him not, but I have heard of him, A merchant of incomparable wealth.
lines: I know him not, but I have heard of him,
A merchant of incomparable wealth.

speeches.speech: He is my father, sir; and, sooth to say, In count'nance somewhat doth resemble you.
lines: He is my father, sir; and, sooth to say,
In count'nance somewhat doth resemble you.

speeches.speech: [Aside]  As much as an apple doth an oyster, and all one.
lines: [Aside]  As much as an apple doth an oyster, and all
one.

speeches.speech: To save your life in this extremity, This favour will I do you for his sake; And think it not the worst of all your fortunes That you are like to Sir Vincentio. His name and credit shall you undertake, And in my house you shall be friendly lodg'd; Look that you take upon you as you should. You understand me, sir. So shall you stay Till you have done your business in the city. If this be court'sy, sir, accept of it.
lines: To save your life in this extremity,
This favour will I do you for his sake;
And think it not the worst of all your fortunes
That you are like to Sir Vincentio.
His name and credit shall you undertake,
And in my house you shall be friendly lodg'd;
Look that you take upon you as you should.
You understand me, sir. So shall you stay
Till you have done your business in the city.
If this be court'sy, sir, accept of it.

speeches.speech: O, sir, I do; and will repute you ever The patron of my life and liberty.
lines: O, sir, I do; and will repute you ever
The patron of my life and liberty.

speeches.speech: Then go with me to make the matter good. This, by the way, I let you understand: My father is here look'd for every day To pass assurance of a dow'r in marriage 'Twixt me and one Baptista's daughter here. In all these circumstances I'll instruct you. Go with me to clothe you as becomes you.         Exeunt
lines: Then go with me to make the matter good.
This, by the way, I let you understand:
My father is here look'd for every day
To pass assurance of a dow'r in marriage
'Twixt me and one Baptista's daughter here.
In all these circumstances I'll instruct you.
Go with me to clothe you as becomes you.         Exeunt

speeches.speech: No, no, forsooth; I dare not for my life.
lines: No, no, forsooth; I dare not for my life.

speeches.speech: The more my wrong, the more his spite appears. What, did he marry me to famish me? Beggars that come unto my father's door Upon entreaty have a present alms; If not, elsewhere they meet with charity; But I, who never knew how to entreat, Nor never needed that I should entreat, Am starv'd for meat, giddy for lack of sleep; With oaths kept waking, and with brawling fed; And that which spites me more than all these wants- He does it under name of perfect love; As who should say, if I should sleep or eat, 'Twere deadly sickness or else present death. I prithee go and get me some repast; I care not what, so it be wholesome food.
lines: The more my wrong, the more his spite appears.
What, did he marry me to famish me?
Beggars that come unto my father's door
Upon entreaty have a present alms;
If not, elsewhere they meet with charity;
But I, who never knew how to entreat,
Nor never needed that I should entreat,
Am starv'd for meat, giddy for lack of sleep;
With oaths kept waking, and with brawling fed;
And that which spites me more than all these wants-
He does it under name of perfect love;
As who should say, if I should sleep or eat,
'Twere deadly sickness or else present death.
I prithee go and get me some repast;
I care not what, so it be wholesome food.

speeches.speech: What say you to a neat's foot?
lines: What say you to a neat's foot?

speeches.speech: 'Tis passing good; I prithee let me have it.
lines: 'Tis passing good; I prithee let me have it.

speeches.speech: I fear it is too choleric a meat. How say you to a fat tripe finely broil'd?
lines: I fear it is too choleric a meat.
How say you to a fat tripe finely broil'd?

speeches.speech: I like it well; good Grumio, fetch it me.
lines: I like it well; good Grumio, fetch it me.

speeches.speech: I cannot tell; I fear 'tis choleric. What say you to a piece of beef and mustard?
lines: I cannot tell; I fear 'tis choleric.
What say you to a piece of beef and mustard?

speeches.speech: A dish that I do love to feed upon.
lines: A dish that I do love to feed upon.

speeches.speech: Ay, but the mustard is too hot a little.
lines: Ay, but the mustard is too hot a little.

speeches.speech: Why then the beef, and let the mustard rest.
lines: Why then the beef, and let the mustard rest.

speeches.speech: Nay, then I will not; you shall have the mustard, Or else you get no beef of Grumio.
lines: Nay, then I will not; you shall have the mustard,
Or else you get no beef of Grumio.

speeches.speech: Then both, or one, or anything thou wilt.
lines: Then both, or one, or anything thou wilt.

speeches.speech: Why then the mustard without the beef.
lines: Why then the mustard without the beef.

speeches.speech: Go, get thee gone, thou false deluding slave, [Beats him] That feed'st me with the very name of meat. Sorrow on thee and all the pack of you That triumph thus upon my misery! Go, get thee gone, I say.
lines: Go, get thee gone, thou false deluding slave,
[Beats him]
That feed'st me with the very name of meat.
Sorrow on thee and all the pack of you
That triumph thus upon my misery!
Go, get thee gone, I say.

speeches.speech: How fares my Kate? What, sweeting, all amort?
lines: How fares my Kate? What, sweeting, all amort?

speeches.speech: Mistress, what cheer?
lines: Mistress, what cheer?

speeches.speech: Faith, as cold as can be.
lines: Faith, as cold as can be.

speeches.speech: Pluck up thy spirits, look cheerfully upon me. Here, love, thou seest how diligent I am, To dress thy meat myself, and bring it thee. I am sure, sweet Kate, this kindness merits thanks. What, not a word? Nay, then thou lov'st it not, And all my pains is sorted to no proof. Here, take away this dish.
lines: Pluck up thy spirits, look cheerfully upon me.
Here, love, thou seest how diligent I am,
To dress thy meat myself, and bring it thee.
I am sure, sweet Kate, this kindness merits thanks.
What, not a word? Nay, then thou lov'st it not,
And all my pains is sorted to no proof.
Here, take away this dish.

speeches.speech: I pray you, let it stand.
lines: I pray you, let it stand.

speeches.speech: The poorest service is repaid with thanks; And so shall mine, before you touch the meat.
lines: The poorest service is repaid with thanks;
And so shall mine, before you touch the meat.

speeches.speech: I thank you, sir.
lines: I thank you, sir.

speeches.speech: Signior Petruchio, fie! you are to blame. Come, Mistress Kate, I'll bear you company.
lines: Signior Petruchio, fie! you are to blame.
Come, Mistress Kate, I'll bear you company.

speeches.speech: [Aside]  Eat it up all, Hortensio, if thou lovest me.- Much good do it unto thy gentle heart! Kate, eat apace. And now, my honey love, Will we return unto thy father's house And revel it as bravely as the best, With silken coats and caps, and golden rings, With ruffs and cuffs and farthingales and things, With scarfs and fans and double change of brav'ry. With amber bracelets, beads, and all this knav'ry. What, hast thou din'd? The tailor stays thy leisure, To deck thy body with his ruffling treasure. [Enter TAILOR] Come, tailor, let us see these ornaments; Lay forth the gown. [Enter HABERDASHER] What news with you, sir?
lines: [Aside]  Eat it up all, Hortensio, if thou lovest me.-
Much good do it unto thy gentle heart!
Kate, eat apace. And now, my honey love,
Will we return unto thy father's house
And revel it as bravely as the best,
With silken coats and caps, and golden rings,
With ruffs and cuffs and farthingales and things,
With scarfs and fans and double change of brav'ry.
With amber bracelets, beads, and all this knav'ry.
What, hast thou din'd? The tailor stays thy leisure,
To deck thy body with his ruffling treasure.
[Enter TAILOR]
Come, tailor, let us see these ornaments;
Lay forth the gown.
[Enter HABERDASHER]
What news with you, sir?

speeches.speech: Here is the cap your worship did bespeak.
lines: Here is the cap your worship did bespeak.

speeches.speech: Why, this was moulded on a porringer; A velvet dish. Fie, fie! 'tis lewd and filthy; Why, 'tis a cockle or a walnut-shell, A knack, a toy, a trick, a baby's cap. Away with it. Come, let me have a bigger.
lines: Why, this was moulded on a porringer;
A velvet dish. Fie, fie! 'tis lewd and filthy;
Why, 'tis a cockle or a walnut-shell,
A knack, a toy, a trick, a baby's cap.
Away with it. Come, let me have a bigger.

speeches.speech: I'll have no bigger; this doth fit the time, And gentlewomen wear such caps as these.
lines: I'll have no bigger; this doth fit the time,
And gentlewomen wear such caps as these.

speeches.speech: When you are gentle, you shall have one too, And not till then.
lines: When you are gentle, you shall have one too,
And not till then.

speeches.speech: [Aside]  That will not be in haste.
lines: [Aside]  That will not be in haste.

speeches.speech: Why, sir, I trust I may have leave to speak; And speak I will. I am no child, no babe. Your betters have endur'd me say my mind, And if you cannot, best you stop your ears. My tongue will tell the anger of my heart, Or else my heart, concealing it, will break; And rather than it shall, I will be free Even to the uttermost, as I please, in words.
lines: Why, sir, I trust I may have leave to speak;
And speak I will. I am no child, no babe.
Your betters have endur'd me say my mind,
And if you cannot, best you stop your ears.
My tongue will tell the anger of my heart,
Or else my heart, concealing it, will break;
And rather than it shall, I will be free
Even to the uttermost, as I please, in words.

speeches.speech: Why, thou say'st true; it is a paltry cap, A custard-coffin, a bauble, a silken pie; I love thee well in that thou lik'st it not.
lines: Why, thou say'st true; it is a paltry cap,
A custard-coffin, a bauble, a silken pie;
I love thee well in that thou lik'st it not.

speeches.speech: Love me or love me not, I like the cap; And it I will have, or I will have none. Exit HABERDASHER
lines: Love me or love me not, I like the cap;
And it I will have, or I will have none. Exit HABERDASHER

speeches.speech: Thy gown? Why, ay. Come, tailor, let us see't. O mercy, God! what masquing stuff is here? What's this? A sleeve? 'Tis like a demi-cannon. What, up and down, carv'd like an appletart? Here's snip and nip and cut and slish and slash, Like to a censer in a barber's shop. Why, what a devil's name, tailor, call'st thou this?
lines: Thy gown? Why, ay. Come, tailor, let us see't.
O mercy, God! what masquing stuff is here?
What's this? A sleeve? 'Tis like a demi-cannon.
What, up and down, carv'd like an appletart?
Here's snip and nip and cut and slish and slash,
Like to a censer in a barber's shop.
Why, what a devil's name, tailor, call'st thou this?

speeches.speech: [Aside]  I see she's like to have neither cap nor gown.
lines: [Aside]  I see she's like to have neither cap nor gown.

speeches.speech: You bid me make it orderly and well, According to the fashion and the time.
lines: You bid me make it orderly and well,
According to the fashion and the time.

speeches.speech: Marry, and did; but if you be rememb'red, I did not bid you mar it to the time. Go, hop me over every kennel home, For you shall hop without my custom, sir. I'll none of it; hence! make your best of it.
lines: Marry, and did; but if you be rememb'red,
I did not bid you mar it to the time.
Go, hop me over every kennel home,
For you shall hop without my custom, sir.
I'll none of it; hence! make your best of it.

speeches.speech: I never saw a better fashion'd gown, More quaint, more pleasing, nor more commendable; Belike you mean to make a puppet of me.
lines: I never saw a better fashion'd gown,
More quaint, more pleasing, nor more commendable;
Belike you mean to make a puppet of me.

speeches.speech: Why, true; he means to make a puppet of thee.
lines: Why, true; he means to make a puppet of thee.

speeches.speech: She says your worship means to make a puppet of her.
lines: She says your worship means to make a puppet of her.

speeches.speech: O monstrous arrogance! Thou liest, thou thread, thou thimble, Thou yard, three-quarters, half-yard, quarter, nail, Thou flea, thou nit, thou winter-cricket thou- Brav'd in mine own house with a skein of thread! Away, thou rag, thou quantity, thou remnant; Or I shall so bemete thee with thy yard As thou shalt think on prating whilst thou liv'st! I tell thee, I, that thou hast marr'd her gown.
lines: O monstrous arrogance! Thou liest, thou thread, thou
thimble,
Thou yard, three-quarters, half-yard, quarter, nail,
Thou flea, thou nit, thou winter-cricket thou-
Brav'd in mine own house with a skein of thread!
Away, thou rag, thou quantity, thou remnant;
Or I shall so bemete thee with thy yard
As thou shalt think on prating whilst thou liv'st!
I tell thee, I, that thou hast marr'd her gown.

speeches.speech: Your worship is deceiv'd; the gown is made Just as my master had direction. Grumio gave order how it should be done.
lines: Your worship is deceiv'd; the gown is made
Just as my master had direction.
Grumio gave order how it should be done.

speeches.speech: I gave him no order; I gave him the stuff.
lines: I gave him no order; I gave him the stuff.

speeches.speech: But how did you desire it should be made?
lines: But how did you desire it should be made?

speeches.speech: Marry, sir, with needle and thread.
lines: Marry, sir, with needle and thread.

speeches.speech: But did you not request to have it cut?
lines: But did you not request to have it cut?

speeches.speech: Thou hast fac'd many things.
lines: Thou hast fac'd many things.

speeches.speech: I have.
lines: I have.

speeches.speech: Face not me. Thou hast brav'd many men; brave not me. I will neither be fac'd nor brav'd. I say unto thee, I bid thy master cut out the gown; but I did not bid him cut it to pieces. Ergo, thou liest.
lines: Face not me. Thou hast brav'd many men; brave not me. I
will neither be fac'd nor brav'd. I say unto thee, I bid thy
master cut out the gown; but I did not bid him cut it to pieces.
Ergo, thou liest.

speeches.speech: Why, here is the note of the fashion to testify.
lines: Why, here is the note of the fashion to testify.

speeches.speech: Read it.
lines: Read it.

speeches.speech: The note lies in's throat, if he say I said so.
lines: The note lies in's throat, if he say I said so.

speeches.speech: [Reads]  'Imprimis, a loose-bodied gown'-
lines: [Reads]  'Imprimis, a loose-bodied gown'-

speeches.speech: Master, if ever I said loose-bodied gown, sew me in the skirts of it and beat me to death with a bottom of brown bread; I said a gown.
lines: Master, if ever I said loose-bodied gown, sew me in the
skirts of it and beat me to death with a bottom of brown bread; I
said a gown.

speeches.speech: Proceed.
lines: Proceed.

speeches.speech: [Reads]  'With a small compass'd cape'-
lines: [Reads]  'With a small compass'd cape'-

speeches.speech: I confess the cape.
lines: I confess the cape.

speeches.speech: [Reads]  'With a trunk sleeve'-
lines: [Reads]  'With a trunk sleeve'-

speeches.speech: I confess two sleeves.
lines: I confess two sleeves.

speeches.speech: [Reads]  'The sleeves curiously cut.'
lines: [Reads]  'The sleeves curiously cut.'

speeches.speech: Ay, there's the villainy.
lines: Ay, there's the villainy.

speeches.speech: Error i' th' bill, sir; error i' th' bill! I commanded the sleeves should be cut out, and sew'd up again; and that I'll prove upon thee, though thy little finger be armed in a thimble.
lines: Error i' th' bill, sir; error i' th' bill! I commanded the
sleeves should be cut out, and sew'd up again; and that I'll
prove upon thee, though thy little finger be armed in a thimble.

speeches.speech: This is true that I say; an I had thee in place where, thou shouldst know it.
lines: This is true that I say; an I had thee in place where, thou
shouldst know it.

speeches.speech: I am for thee straight; take thou the bill, give me thy meteyard, and spare not me.
lines: I am for thee straight; take thou the bill, give me thy
meteyard, and spare not me.

speeches.speech: God-a-mercy, Grumio! Then he shall have no odds.
lines: God-a-mercy, Grumio! Then he shall have no odds.

speeches.speech: Well, sir, in brief, the gown is not for me.
lines: Well, sir, in brief, the gown is not for me.

speeches.speech: You are i' th' right, sir; 'tis for my mistress.
lines: You are i' th' right, sir; 'tis for my mistress.

speeches.speech: Go, take it up unto thy master's use.
lines: Go, take it up unto thy master's use.

speeches.speech: Villain, not for thy life! Take up my mistress' gown for thy master's use!
lines: Villain, not for thy life! Take up my mistress' gown for
thy master's use!

speeches.speech: Why, sir, what's your conceit in that?
lines: Why, sir, what's your conceit in that?

speeches.speech: O, sir, the conceit is deeper than you think for. Take up my mistress' gown to his master's use! O fie, fie, fie!
lines: O, sir, the conceit is deeper than you think for.
Take up my mistress' gown to his master's use!
O fie, fie, fie!

speeches.speech: [Aside]  Hortensio, say thou wilt see the tailor paid.- Go take it hence; be gone, and say no more.
lines: [Aside]  Hortensio, say thou wilt see the tailor paid.-
Go take it hence; be gone, and say no more.

speeches.speech: Tailor, I'll pay thee for thy gown to-morrow; Take no unkindness of his hasty words. Away, I say; commend me to thy master.      Exit TAILOR
lines: Tailor, I'll pay thee for thy gown to-morrow;
Take no unkindness of his hasty words.
Away, I say; commend me to thy master.      Exit TAILOR

speeches.speech: Well, come, my Kate; we will unto your father's Even in these honest mean habiliments; Our purses shall be proud, our garments poor; For 'tis the mind that makes the body rich; And as the sun breaks through the darkest clouds, So honour peereth in the meanest habit. What, is the jay more precious than the lark Because his feathers are more beautiful? Or is the adder better than the eel Because his painted skin contents the eye? O no, good Kate; neither art thou the worse For this poor furniture and mean array. If thou account'st it shame, lay it on me; And therefore frolic; we will hence forthwith To feast and sport us at thy father's house. Go call my men, and let us straight to him; And bring our horses unto Long-lane end; There will we mount, and thither walk on foot. Let's see; I think 'tis now some seven o'clock, And well we may come there by dinner-time.
lines: Well, come, my Kate; we will unto your father's
Even in these honest mean habiliments;
Our purses shall be proud, our garments poor;
For 'tis the mind that makes the body rich;
And as the sun breaks through the darkest clouds,
So honour peereth in the meanest habit.
What, is the jay more precious than the lark
Because his feathers are more beautiful?
Or is the adder better than the eel
Because his painted skin contents the eye?
O no, good Kate; neither art thou the worse
For this poor furniture and mean array.
If thou account'st it shame, lay it on me;
And therefore frolic; we will hence forthwith
To feast and sport us at thy father's house.
Go call my men, and let us straight to him;
And bring our horses unto Long-lane end;
There will we mount, and thither walk on foot.
Let's see; I think 'tis now some seven o'clock,
And well we may come there by dinner-time.

speeches.speech: I dare assure you, sir, 'tis almost two, And 'twill be supper-time ere you come there.
lines: I dare assure you, sir, 'tis almost two,
And 'twill be supper-time ere you come there.

speeches.speech: It shall be seven ere I go to horse. Look what I speak, or do, or think to do, You are still crossing it. Sirs, let 't alone; I will not go to-day; and ere I do, It shall be what o'clock I say it is.
lines: It shall be seven ere I go to horse.
Look what I speak, or do, or think to do,
You are still crossing it. Sirs, let 't alone;
I will not go to-day; and ere I do,
It shall be what o'clock I say it is.

speeches.speech: Why, so this gallant will command the sun.
lines: Why, so this gallant will command the sun.

speeches.speech: Sir, this is the house; please it you that I call?
lines: Sir, this is the house; please it you that I call?

speeches.speech: Ay, what else? And, but I be deceived, Signior Baptista may remember me Near twenty years ago in Genoa, Where we were lodgers at the Pegasus.
lines: Ay, what else? And, but I be deceived,
Signior Baptista may remember me
Near twenty years ago in Genoa,
Where we were lodgers at the Pegasus.

speeches.speech: 'Tis well; and hold your own, in any case, With such austerity as longeth to a father.
lines: 'Tis well; and hold your own, in any case,
With such austerity as longeth to a father.

speeches.speech: I warrant you. But, sir, here comes your boy; 'Twere good he were school'd.
lines: I warrant you. But, sir, here comes your boy;
'Twere good he were school'd.

speeches.speech: Fear you not him. Sirrah Biondello, Now do your duty throughly, I advise you. Imagine 'twere the right Vincentio.
lines: Fear you not him. Sirrah Biondello,
Now do your duty throughly, I advise you.
Imagine 'twere the right Vincentio.

speeches.speech: Tut, fear not me.
lines: Tut, fear not me.

speeches.speech: But hast thou done thy errand to Baptista?
lines: But hast thou done thy errand to Baptista?

speeches.speech: I told him that your father was at Venice, And that you look'd for him this day in Padua.
lines: I told him that your father was at Venice,
And that you look'd for him this day in Padua.

speeches.speech: Th'art a tall fellow; hold thee that to drink. Here comes Baptista. Set your countenance, sir. [Enter BAPTISTA, and LUCENTIO as CAMBIO] Signior Baptista, you are happily met. [To To the PEDANT] Sir, this is the gentleman I told you of; I pray you stand good father to me now; Give me Bianca for my patrimony.
lines: Th'art a tall fellow; hold thee that to drink.
Here comes Baptista. Set your countenance, sir.
[Enter BAPTISTA, and LUCENTIO as CAMBIO]
Signior Baptista, you are happily met.
[To To the PEDANT] Sir, this is the gentleman I told you of;
I pray you stand good father to me now;
Give me Bianca for my patrimony.

speeches.speech: Soft, son! Sir, by your leave: having come to Padua To gather in some debts, my son Lucentio Made me acquainted with a weighty cause Of love between your daughter and himself; And- for the good report I hear of you, And for the love he beareth to your daughter, And she to him- to stay him not too long, I am content, in a good father's care, To have him match'd; and, if you please to like No worse than I, upon some agreement Me shall you find ready and willing With one consent to have her so bestow'd; For curious I cannot be with you, Signior Baptista, of whom I hear so well.
lines: Soft, son!
Sir, by your leave: having come to Padua
To gather in some debts, my son Lucentio
Made me acquainted with a weighty cause
Of love between your daughter and himself;
And- for the good report I hear of you,
And for the love he beareth to your daughter,
And she to him- to stay him not too long,
I am content, in a good father's care,
To have him match'd; and, if you please to like
No worse than I, upon some agreement
Me shall you find ready and willing
With one consent to have her so bestow'd;
For curious I cannot be with you,
Signior Baptista, of whom I hear so well.

speeches.speech: Sir, pardon me in what I have to say. Your plainness and your shortness please me well. Right true it is your son Lucentio here Doth love my daughter, and she loveth him, Or both dissemble deeply their affections; And therefore, if you say no more than this, That like a father you will deal with him, And pass my daughter a sufficient dower, The match is made, and all is done- Your son shall have my daughter with consent.
lines: Sir, pardon me in what I have to say.
Your plainness and your shortness please me well.
Right true it is your son Lucentio here
Doth love my daughter, and she loveth him,
Or both dissemble deeply their affections;
And therefore, if you say no more than this,
That like a father you will deal with him,
And pass my daughter a sufficient dower,
The match is made, and all is done-
Your son shall have my daughter with consent.

speeches.speech: I thank you, sir. Where then do you know best We be affied, and such assurance ta'en As shall with either part's agreement stand?
lines: I thank you, sir. Where then do you know best
We be affied, and such assurance ta'en
As shall with either part's agreement stand?

speeches.speech: Not in my house, Lucentio, for you know Pitchers have ears, and I have many servants; Besides, old Gremio is heark'ning still, And happily we might be interrupted.
lines: Not in my house, Lucentio, for you know
Pitchers have ears, and I have many servants;
Besides, old Gremio is heark'ning still,
And happily we might be interrupted.

speeches.speech: Then at my lodging, an it like you. There doth my father lie; and there this night We'll pass the business privately and well. Send for your daughter by your servant here; My boy shall fetch the scrivener presently. The worst is this, that at so slender warning You are like to have a thin and slender pittance.
lines: Then at my lodging, an it like you.
There doth my father lie; and there this night
We'll pass the business privately and well.
Send for your daughter by your servant here;
My boy shall fetch the scrivener presently.
The worst is this, that at so slender warning
You are like to have a thin and slender pittance.

speeches.speech: It likes me well. Cambio, hie you home, And bid Bianca make her ready straight; And, if you will, tell what hath happened- Lucentio's father is arriv'd in Padua, And how she's like to be Lucentio's wife. Exit LUCENTIO
lines: It likes me well. Cambio, hie you home,
And bid Bianca make her ready straight;
And, if you will, tell what hath happened-
Lucentio's father is arriv'd in Padua,
And how she's like to be Lucentio's wife. Exit LUCENTIO

speeches.speech: I pray the gods she may, with all my heart.
lines: I pray the gods she may, with all my heart.

speeches.speech: Dally not with the gods, but get thee gone. [Exit BIONDELLO] Signior Baptista, shall I lead the way? Welcome! One mess is like to be your cheer; Come, sir; we will better it in Pisa.
lines: Dally not with the gods, but get thee gone.
[Exit BIONDELLO]
Signior Baptista, shall I lead the way?
Welcome! One mess is like to be your cheer;
Come, sir; we will better it in Pisa.

speeches.speech: I follow you.                            Exeunt
lines: I follow you.                            Exeunt

speeches.speech: Cambio.
lines: Cambio.

speeches.speech: What say'st thou, Biondello?
lines: What say'st thou, Biondello?

speeches.speech: You saw my master wink and laugh upon you?
lines: You saw my master wink and laugh upon you?

speeches.speech: Biondello, what of that?
lines: Biondello, what of that?

speeches.speech: Faith, nothing; but has left me here behind to expound the meaning or moral of his signs and tokens.
lines: Faith, nothing; but has left me here behind to expound
the meaning or moral of his signs and tokens.

speeches.speech: I pray thee moralize them.
lines: I pray thee moralize them.

speeches.speech: Then thus: Baptista is safe, talking with the deceiving father of a deceitful son.
lines: Then thus: Baptista is safe, talking with the deceiving
father of a deceitful son.

speeches.speech: And what of him?
lines: And what of him?

speeches.speech: His daughter is to be brought by you to the supper.
lines: His daughter is to be brought by you to the supper.

speeches.speech: And then?
lines: And then?

speeches.speech: The old priest at Saint Luke's church is at your command at all hours.
lines: The old priest at Saint Luke's church is at your command
at all hours.

speeches.speech: And what of all this?
lines: And what of all this?

speeches.speech: I cannot tell, except they are busied about a counterfeit assurance. Take your assurance of her, cum privilegio ad imprimendum solum; to th' church take the priest, clerk, and some sufficient honest witnesses. If this be not that you look for, I have more to say, But bid Bianca farewell for ever and a day.
lines: I cannot tell, except they are busied about a
counterfeit assurance. Take your assurance of her, cum privilegio
ad imprimendum solum; to th' church take the priest, clerk, and
some sufficient honest witnesses.
If this be not that you look for, I have more to say,
But bid Bianca farewell for ever and a day.

speeches.speech: Hear'st thou, Biondello?
lines: Hear'st thou, Biondello?

speeches.speech: I cannot tarry. I knew a wench married in an afternoon as she went to the garden for parsley to stuff a rabbit; and so may you, sir; and so adieu, sir. My master hath appointed me to go to Saint Luke's to bid the priest be ready to come against you come with your appendix.
lines: I cannot tarry. I knew a wench married in an afternoon
as she went to the garden for parsley to stuff a rabbit; and so
may you, sir; and so adieu, sir. My master hath appointed me to
go to Saint Luke's to bid the priest be ready to come against you
come with your appendix.

speeches.speech: I may and will, if she be so contented. She will be pleas'd; then wherefore should I doubt? Hap what hap may, I'll roundly go about her; It shall go hard if Cambio go without her.         Exit
lines: I may and will, if she be so contented.
She will be pleas'd; then wherefore should I doubt?
Hap what hap may, I'll roundly go about her;
It shall go hard if Cambio go without her.         Exit

speeches.speech: Come on, a God's name; once more toward our father's. Good Lord, how bright and goodly shines the moon!
lines: Come on, a God's name; once more toward our father's.
Good Lord, how bright and goodly shines the moon!

speeches.speech: The moon? The sun! It is not moonlight now.
lines: The moon? The sun! It is not moonlight now.

speeches.speech: I say it is the moon that shines so bright.
lines: I say it is the moon that shines so bright.

speeches.speech: I know it is the sun that shines so bright.
lines: I know it is the sun that shines so bright.

speeches.speech: Now by my mother's son, and that's myself, It shall be moon, or star, or what I list, Or ere I journey to your father's house. Go on and fetch our horses back again. Evermore cross'd and cross'd; nothing but cross'd!
lines: Now by my mother's son, and that's myself,
It shall be moon, or star, or what I list,
Or ere I journey to your father's house.
Go on and fetch our horses back again.
Evermore cross'd and cross'd; nothing but cross'd!

speeches.speech: Say as he says, or we shall never go.
lines: Say as he says, or we shall never go.

speeches.speech: Forward, I pray, since we have come so far, And be it moon, or sun, or what you please; And if you please to call it a rush-candle, Henceforth I vow it shall be so for me.
lines: Forward, I pray, since we have come so far,
And be it moon, or sun, or what you please;
And if you please to call it a rush-candle,
Henceforth I vow it shall be so for me.

speeches.speech: I say it is the moon.
lines: I say it is the moon.

speeches.speech: I know it is the moon.
lines: I know it is the moon.

speeches.speech: Nay, then you lie; it is the blessed sun.
lines: Nay, then you lie; it is the blessed sun.

speeches.speech: Then, God be bless'd, it is the blessed sun; But sun it is not, when you say it is not; And the moon changes even as your mind. What you will have it nam'd, even that it is, And so it shall be so for Katherine.
lines: Then, God be bless'd, it is the blessed sun;
But sun it is not, when you say it is not;
And the moon changes even as your mind.
What you will have it nam'd, even that it is,
And so it shall be so for Katherine.

speeches.speech: Petruchio, go thy ways, the field is won.
lines: Petruchio, go thy ways, the field is won.

speeches.speech: Well, forward, forward! thus the bowl should run, And not unluckily against the bias. But, soft! Company is coming here. [Enter VINCENTIO] [To VINCENTIO]  Good-morrow, gentle mistress; where away?- Tell me, sweet Kate, and tell me truly too, Hast thou beheld a fresher gentlewoman? Such war of white and red within her cheeks! What stars do spangle heaven with such beauty As those two eyes become that heavenly face? Fair lovely maid, once more good day to thee. Sweet Kate, embrace her for her beauty's sake.
lines: Well, forward, forward! thus the bowl should run,
And not unluckily against the bias.
But, soft! Company is coming here.
[Enter VINCENTIO]
[To VINCENTIO]  Good-morrow, gentle mistress; where away?-
Tell me, sweet Kate, and tell me truly too,
Hast thou beheld a fresher gentlewoman?
Such war of white and red within her cheeks!
What stars do spangle heaven with such beauty
As those two eyes become that heavenly face?
Fair lovely maid, once more good day to thee.
Sweet Kate, embrace her for her beauty's sake.

speeches.speech: 'A will make the man mad, to make a woman of him.
lines: 'A will make the man mad, to make a woman of him.

speeches.speech: Young budding virgin, fair and fresh and sweet, Whither away, or where is thy abode? Happy the parents of so fair a child; Happier the man whom favourable stars Allots thee for his lovely bed-fellow.
lines: Young budding virgin, fair and fresh and sweet,
Whither away, or where is thy abode?
Happy the parents of so fair a child;
Happier the man whom favourable stars
Allots thee for his lovely bed-fellow.

speeches.speech: Why, how now, Kate, I hope thou art not mad! This is a man, old, wrinkled, faded, withered, And not a maiden, as thou sayst he is.
lines: Why, how now, Kate, I hope thou art not mad!
This is a man, old, wrinkled, faded, withered,
And not a maiden, as thou sayst he is.

speeches.speech: Pardon, old father, my mistaking eyes, That have been so bedazzled with the sun That everything I look on seemeth green; Now I perceive thou art a reverend father. Pardon, I pray thee, for my mad mistaking.
lines: Pardon, old father, my mistaking eyes,
That have been so bedazzled with the sun
That everything I look on seemeth green;
Now I perceive thou art a reverend father.
Pardon, I pray thee, for my mad mistaking.

speeches.speech: Do, good old grandsire, and withal make known Which way thou travellest- if along with us, We shall be joyful of thy company.
lines: Do, good old grandsire, and withal make known
Which way thou travellest- if along with us,
We shall be joyful of thy company.

speeches.speech: Fair sir, and you my merry mistress, That with your strange encounter much amaz'd me, My name is call'd Vincentio, my dwelling Pisa, And bound I am to Padua, there to visit A son of mine, which long I have not seen.
lines: Fair sir, and you my merry mistress,
That with your strange encounter much amaz'd me,
My name is call'd Vincentio, my dwelling Pisa,
And bound I am to Padua, there to visit
A son of mine, which long I have not seen.

speeches.speech: What is his name?
lines: What is his name?

speeches.speech: Lucentio, gentle sir.
lines: Lucentio, gentle sir.

speeches.speech: Happily met; the happier for thy son. And now by law, as well as reverend age, I may entitle thee my loving father: The sister to my wife, this gentlewoman, Thy son by this hath married. Wonder not, Nor be not grieved- she is of good esteem, Her dowry wealthy, and of worthy birth; Beside, so qualified as may beseem The spouse of any noble gentleman. Let me embrace with old Vincentio; And wander we to see thy honest son, Who will of thy arrival be full joyous.
lines: Happily met; the happier for thy son.
And now by law, as well as reverend age,
I may entitle thee my loving father:
The sister to my wife, this gentlewoman,
Thy son by this hath married. Wonder not,
Nor be not grieved- she is of good esteem,
Her dowry wealthy, and of worthy birth;
Beside, so qualified as may beseem
The spouse of any noble gentleman.
Let me embrace with old Vincentio;
And wander we to see thy honest son,
Who will of thy arrival be full joyous.

speeches.speech: But is this true; or is it else your pleasure, Like pleasant travellers, to break a jest Upon the company you overtake?
lines: But is this true; or is it else your pleasure,
Like pleasant travellers, to break a jest
Upon the company you overtake?

speeches.speech: I do assure thee, father, so it is.
lines: I do assure thee, father, so it is.

speeches.speech: Come, go along, and see the truth hereof; For our first merriment hath made thee jealous.
lines: Come, go along, and see the truth hereof;
For our first merriment hath made thee jealous.

speeches.speech: Well, Petruchio, this has put me in heart. Have to my widow; and if she be froward, Then hast thou taught Hortensio to be untoward.    Exit
lines: Well, Petruchio, this has put me in heart.
Have to my widow; and if she be froward,
Then hast thou taught Hortensio to be untoward.    Exit

speeches.speech: Softly and swiftly, sir, for the priest is ready.
lines: Softly and swiftly, sir, for the priest is ready.

speeches.speech: I fly, Biondello; but they may chance to need the at home, therefore leave us.
lines: I fly, Biondello; but they may chance to need the at
home, therefore leave us.

speeches.speech: Nay, faith, I'll see the church a your back, and then come back to my master's as soon as I can.
lines: Nay, faith, I'll see the church a your back, and then
come back to my master's as soon as I can.

speeches.speech: I marvel Cambio comes not all this while.
lines: I marvel Cambio comes not all this while.

speeches.speech: Sir, here's the door; this is Lucentio's house; My father's bears more toward the market-place; Thither must I, and here I leave you, sir.
lines: Sir, here's the door; this is Lucentio's house;
My father's bears more toward the market-place;
Thither must I, and here I leave you, sir.

speeches.speech: You shall not choose but drink before you go; I think I shall command your welcome here, And by all likelihood some cheer is toward.    [Knocks]
lines: You shall not choose but drink before you go;
I think I shall command your welcome here,
And by all likelihood some cheer is toward.    [Knocks]

speeches.speech: They're busy within; you were best knock louder.
lines: They're busy within; you were best knock louder.

speeches.speech: What's he that knocks as he would beat down the gate?
lines: What's he that knocks as he would beat down the gate?

speeches.speech: Is Signior Lucentio within, sir?
lines: Is Signior Lucentio within, sir?

speeches.speech: He's within, sir, but not to be spoken withal.
lines: He's within, sir, but not to be spoken withal.

speeches.speech: What if a man bring him a hundred pound or two to make merry withal?
lines: What if a man bring him a hundred pound or two to make
merry withal?

speeches.speech: Keep your hundred pounds to yourself; he shall need none so long as I live.
lines: Keep your hundred pounds to yourself; he shall need none so
long as I live.

speeches.speech: Nay, I told you your son was well beloved in Padua. Do you hear, sir? To leave frivolous circumstances, I pray you tell Signior Lucentio that his father is come from Pisa, and is here at the door to speak with him.
lines: Nay, I told you your son was well beloved in Padua. Do
you hear, sir? To leave frivolous circumstances, I pray you tell
Signior Lucentio that his father is come from Pisa, and is here
at the door to speak with him.

speeches.speech: Thou liest: his father is come from Padua, and here looking out at the window.
lines: Thou liest: his father is come from Padua, and here looking
out at the window.

speeches.speech: Art thou his father?
lines: Art thou his father?

speeches.speech: Ay, sir; so his mother says, if I may believe her.
lines: Ay, sir; so his mother says, if I may believe her.

speeches.speech: [To VINCENTIO]  Why, how now, gentleman! Why, this is flat knavery to take upon you another man's name.
lines: [To VINCENTIO]  Why, how now, gentleman!
Why, this is flat knavery to take upon you another man's name.

speeches.speech: Lay hands on the villain; I believe 'a means to cozen somebody in this city under my countenance.
lines: Lay hands on the villain; I believe 'a means to cozen
somebody in this city under my countenance.

speeches.speech: I have seen them in the church together. God send 'em good shipping! But who is here? Mine old master, Vincentio! Now we are undone and brought to nothing.
lines: I have seen them in the church together. God send 'em
good shipping! But who is here? Mine old master, Vincentio! Now we
are undone and brought to nothing.

speeches.speech: [Seeing BIONDELLO]  Come hither, crack-hemp.
lines: [Seeing BIONDELLO]  Come hither, crack-hemp.

speeches.speech: I hope I may choose, sir.
lines: I hope I may choose, sir.

speeches.speech: Come hither, you rogue. What, have you forgot me?
lines: Come hither, you rogue. What, have you forgot me?

speeches.speech: Forgot you! No, sir. I could not forget you, for I never saw you before in all my life.
lines: Forgot you! No, sir. I could not forget you, for I never
saw you before in all my life.

speeches.speech: What, you notorious villain, didst thou never see thy master's father, Vincentio?
lines: What, you notorious villain, didst thou never see thy
master's father, Vincentio?

speeches.speech: What, my old worshipful old master? Yes, marry, sir; see where he looks out of the window.
lines: What, my old worshipful old master? Yes, marry, sir; see
where he looks out of the window.

speeches.speech: Is't so, indeed?               [He beats BIONDELLO]
lines: Is't so, indeed?               [He beats BIONDELLO]

speeches.speech: Help, help, help! Here's a madman will murder me.
lines: Help, help, help! Here's a madman will murder me.

speeches.speech: Help, son! help, Signior Baptista!     Exit from above
lines: Help, son! help, Signior Baptista!     Exit from above

speeches.speech: Prithee, Kate, let's stand aside and see the end of this controversy.                         [They stand aside] Re-enter PEDANT below; BAPTISTA, TRANIO, and SERVANTS
lines: Prithee, Kate, let's stand aside and see the end of this
controversy.                         [They stand aside]
Re-enter PEDANT below; BAPTISTA, TRANIO, and SERVANTS

speeches.speech: Sir, what are you that offer to beat my servant?
lines: Sir, what are you that offer to beat my servant?

speeches.speech: What am I, sir? Nay, what are you, sir? O immortal gods! O fine villain! A silken doublet, a velvet hose, a scarlet cloak, and a copatain hat! O, I am undone! I am undone! While I play the good husband at home, my son and my servant spend all at the university.
lines: What am I, sir? Nay, what are you, sir? O immortal gods!
O fine villain! A silken doublet, a velvet hose, a scarlet cloak,
and a copatain hat! O, I am undone! I am undone! While I play the
good husband at home, my son and my servant spend all at the
university.

speeches.speech: How now! what's the matter?
lines: How now! what's the matter?

speeches.speech: What, is the man lunatic?
lines: What, is the man lunatic?

speeches.speech: Sir, you seem a sober ancient gentleman by your habit, but your words show you a madman. Why, sir, what 'cerns it you if I wear pearl and gold? I thank my good father, I am able to maintain it.
lines: Sir, you seem a sober ancient gentleman by your habit, but
your words show you a madman. Why, sir, what 'cerns it you if I
wear pearl and gold? I thank my good father, I am able to
maintain it.

speeches.speech: Thy father! O villain! he is a sailmaker in Bergamo.
lines: Thy father! O villain! he is a sailmaker in Bergamo.

speeches.speech: You mistake, sir; you mistake, sir. Pray, what do you think is his name?
lines: You mistake, sir; you mistake, sir. Pray, what do you
think is his name?

speeches.speech: His name! As if I knew not his name! I have brought him up ever since he was three years old, and his name is Tranio.
lines: His name! As if I knew not his name! I have brought him
up ever since he was three years old, and his name is Tranio.

speeches.speech: Away, away, mad ass! His name is Lucentio; and he is mine only son, and heir to the lands of me, Signior Vicentio.
lines: Away, away, mad ass! His name is Lucentio; and he is mine
only son, and heir to the lands of me, Signior Vicentio.

speeches.speech: Lucentio! O, he hath murd'red his master! Lay hold on him, I charge you, in the Duke's name. O, my son, my son! Tell me, thou villain, where is my son, Lucentio?
lines: Lucentio! O, he hath murd'red his master! Lay hold on
him, I charge you, in the Duke's name. O, my son, my son! Tell
me, thou villain, where is my son, Lucentio?

speeches.speech: Call forth an officer. [Enter one with an OFFICER] Carry this mad knave to the gaol. Father Baptista, I charge you see that he be forthcoming.
lines: Call forth an officer.
[Enter one with an OFFICER]
Carry this mad knave to the gaol. Father Baptista, I charge you
see that he be forthcoming.

speeches.speech: Carry me to the gaol!
lines: Carry me to the gaol!

speeches.speech: Stay, Officer; he shall not go to prison.
lines: Stay, Officer; he shall not go to prison.

speeches.speech: Talk not, Signior Gremio; I say he shall go to prison.
lines: Talk not, Signior Gremio; I say he shall go to prison.

speeches.speech: Take heed, Signior Baptista, lest you be cony-catch'd in this business; I dare swear this is the right Vincentio.
lines: Take heed, Signior Baptista, lest you be cony-catch'd in
this business; I dare swear this is the right Vincentio.

speeches.speech: Swear if thou dar'st.
lines: Swear if thou dar'st.

speeches.speech: Nay, I dare not swear it.
lines: Nay, I dare not swear it.

speeches.speech: Then thou wert best say that I am not Lucentio.
lines: Then thou wert best say that I am not Lucentio.

speeches.speech: Yes, I know thee to be Signior Lucentio.
lines: Yes, I know thee to be Signior Lucentio.

speeches.speech: Away with the dotard; to the gaol with him!
lines: Away with the dotard; to the gaol with him!

speeches.speech: Thus strangers may be hal'd and abus'd. O monstrous villain!
lines: Thus strangers may be hal'd and abus'd. O monstrous
villain!

speeches.speech: O, we are spoil'd; and yonder he is! Deny him, forswear him, or else we are all undone.
lines: O, we are spoil'd; and yonder he is! Deny him, forswear
him, or else we are all undone.

speeches.speech: [Kneeling]  Pardon, sweet father.
lines: [Kneeling]  Pardon, sweet father.

speeches.speech: Lives my sweet son?
lines: Lives my sweet son?

speeches.speech: Pardon, dear father.
lines: Pardon, dear father.

speeches.speech: How hast thou offended? Where is Lucentio?
lines: How hast thou offended?
Where is Lucentio?

speeches.speech: Here's Lucentio, Right son to the right Vincentio, That have by marriage made thy daughter mine, While counterfeit supposes blear'd thine eyne.
lines: Here's Lucentio,
Right son to the right Vincentio,
That have by marriage made thy daughter mine,
While counterfeit supposes blear'd thine eyne.

speeches.speech: Here's packing, with a witness, to deceive us all!
lines: Here's packing, with a witness, to deceive us all!

speeches.speech: Where is that damned villain, Tranio, That fac'd and brav'd me in this matter so?
lines: Where is that damned villain, Tranio,
That fac'd and brav'd me in this matter so?

speeches.speech: Why, tell me, is not this my Cambio?
lines: Why, tell me, is not this my Cambio?

speeches.speech: Cambio is chang'd into Lucentio.
lines: Cambio is chang'd into Lucentio.

speeches.speech: Love wrought these miracles. Bianca's love Made me exchange my state with Tranio, While he did bear my countenance in the town; And happily I have arrived at the last Unto the wished haven of my bliss. What Tranio did, myself enforc'd him to; Then pardon him, sweet father, for my sake.
lines: Love wrought these miracles. Bianca's love
Made me exchange my state with Tranio,
While he did bear my countenance in the town;
And happily I have arrived at the last
Unto the wished haven of my bliss.
What Tranio did, myself enforc'd him to;
Then pardon him, sweet father, for my sake.

speeches.speech: I'll slit the villain's nose that would have sent me to the gaol.
lines: I'll slit the villain's nose that would have sent me to
the gaol.

speeches.speech: [To LUCENTIO]  But do you hear, sir? Have you married my daughter without asking my good will?
lines: [To LUCENTIO]  But do you hear, sir? Have you married my
daughter without asking my good will?

speeches.speech: Fear not, Baptista; we will content you, go to; but I will in to be revenged for this villainy.          Exit
lines: Fear not, Baptista; we will content you, go to; but I
will in to be revenged for this villainy.          Exit

speeches.speech: And I to sound the depth of this knavery.  Exit
lines: And I to sound the depth of this knavery.  Exit

speeches.speech: Look not pale, Bianca; thy father will not frown.
lines: Look not pale, Bianca; thy father will not frown.

speeches.speech: My cake is dough, but I'll in among the rest; Out of hope of all but my share of the feast.      Exit
lines: My cake is dough, but I'll in among the rest;
Out of hope of all but my share of the feast.      Exit

speeches.speech: Husband, let's follow to see the end of this ado.
lines: Husband, let's follow to see the end of this ado.

speeches.speech: First kiss me, Kate, and we will.
lines: First kiss me, Kate, and we will.

speeches.speech: What, in the midst of the street?
lines: What, in the midst of the street?

speeches.speech: What, art thou asham'd of me?
lines: What, art thou asham'd of me?

speeches.speech: No, sir; God forbid; but asham'd to kiss.
lines: No, sir; God forbid; but asham'd to kiss.

speeches.speech: Why, then, let's home again. Come, sirrah, let's away.
lines: Why, then, let's home again. Come, sirrah, let's away.

speeches.speech: Nay, I will give thee a kiss; now pray thee, love, stay.
lines: Nay, I will give thee a kiss; now pray thee, love, stay.

speeches.speech: Is not this well? Come, my sweet Kate: Better once than never, for never too late.      Exeunt
lines: Is not this well? Come, my sweet Kate:
Better once than never, for never too late.      Exeunt

speeches.speech: At last, though long, our jarring notes agree; And time it is when raging war is done To smile at scapes and perils overblown. My fair Bianca, bid my father welcome, While I with self-same kindness welcome thine. Brother Petruchio, sister Katherina, And thou, Hortensio, with thy loving widow, Feast with the best, and welcome to my house. My banquet is to close our stomachs up After our great good cheer. Pray you, sit down; For now we sit to chat as well as eat.       [They sit]
lines: At last, though long, our jarring notes agree;
And time it is when raging war is done
To smile at scapes and perils overblown.
My fair Bianca, bid my father welcome,
While I with self-same kindness welcome thine.
Brother Petruchio, sister Katherina,
And thou, Hortensio, with thy loving widow,
Feast with the best, and welcome to my house.
My banquet is to close our stomachs up
After our great good cheer. Pray you, sit down;
For now we sit to chat as well as eat.       [They sit]

speeches.speech: Nothing but sit and sit, and eat and eat!
lines: Nothing but sit and sit, and eat and eat!

speeches.speech: Padua affords this kindness, son Petruchio.
lines: Padua affords this kindness, son Petruchio.

speeches.speech: Padua affords nothing but what is kind.
lines: Padua affords nothing but what is kind.

speeches.speech: For both our sakes I would that word were true.
lines: For both our sakes I would that word were true.

speeches.speech: Now, for my life, Hortensio fears his widow.
lines: Now, for my life, Hortensio fears his widow.

speeches.speech: Then never trust me if I be afeard.
lines: Then never trust me if I be afeard.

speeches.speech: YOU are very sensible, and yet you miss my sense: I mean Hortensio is afeard of you.
lines: YOU are very sensible, and yet you miss my sense:
I mean Hortensio is afeard of you.

speeches.speech: He that is giddy thinks the world turns round.
lines: He that is giddy thinks the world turns round.

speeches.speech: Roundly replied.
lines: Roundly replied.

speeches.speech: Mistress, how mean you that?
lines: Mistress, how mean you that?

speeches.speech: Thus I conceive by him.
lines: Thus I conceive by him.

speeches.speech: Conceives by me! How likes Hortensio that?
lines: Conceives by me! How likes Hortensio that?

speeches.speech: My widow says thus she conceives her tale.
lines: My widow says thus she conceives her tale.

speeches.speech: Very well mended. Kiss him for that, good widow.
lines: Very well mended. Kiss him for that, good widow.

speeches.speech: 'He that is giddy thinks the world turns round.' I pray you tell me what you meant by that.
lines: 'He that is giddy thinks the world turns round.'
I pray you tell me what you meant by that.

speeches.speech: Your husband, being troubled with a shrew, Measures my husband's sorrow by his woe; And now you know my meaning.
lines: Your husband, being troubled with a shrew,
Measures my husband's sorrow by his woe;
And now you know my meaning.

speeches.speech: A very mean meaning.
lines: A very mean meaning.

speeches.speech: Right, I mean you.
lines: Right, I mean you.

speeches.speech: And I am mean, indeed, respecting you.
lines: And I am mean, indeed, respecting you.

speeches.speech: To her, Kate!
lines: To her, Kate!

speeches.speech: To her, widow!
lines: To her, widow!

speeches.speech: A hundred marks, my Kate does put her down.
lines: A hundred marks, my Kate does put her down.

speeches.speech: That's my office.
lines: That's my office.

speeches.speech: Spoke like an officer- ha' to thee, lad.
lines: Spoke like an officer- ha' to thee, lad.

speeches.speech: How likes Gremio these quick-witted folks?
lines: How likes Gremio these quick-witted folks?

speeches.speech: Believe me, sir, they butt together well.
lines: Believe me, sir, they butt together well.

speeches.speech: Head and butt! An hasty-witted body Would say your head and butt were head and horn.
lines: Head and butt! An hasty-witted body
Would say your head and butt were head and horn.

speeches.speech: Ay, mistress bride, hath that awakened you?
lines: Ay, mistress bride, hath that awakened you?

speeches.speech: Ay, but not frighted me; therefore I'll sleep again.
lines: Ay, but not frighted me; therefore I'll sleep again.

speeches.speech: Nay, that you shall not; since you have begun, Have at you for a bitter jest or two.
lines: Nay, that you shall not; since you have begun,
Have at you for a bitter jest or two.

speeches.speech: Am I your bird? I mean to shift my bush, And then pursue me as you draw your bow. You are welcome all.
lines: Am I your bird? I mean to shift my bush,
And then pursue me as you draw your bow.
You are welcome all.

speeches.speech: She hath prevented me. Here, Signior Tranio, This bird you aim'd at, though you hit her not; Therefore a health to all that shot and miss'd.
lines: She hath prevented me. Here, Signior Tranio,
This bird you aim'd at, though you hit her not;
Therefore a health to all that shot and miss'd.

speeches.speech: O, sir, Lucentio slipp'd me like his greyhound, Which runs himself, and catches for his master.
lines: O, sir, Lucentio slipp'd me like his greyhound,
Which runs himself, and catches for his master.

speeches.speech: A good swift simile, but something currish.
lines: A good swift simile, but something currish.

speeches.speech: 'Tis well, sir, that you hunted for yourself; 'Tis thought your deer does hold you at a bay.
lines: 'Tis well, sir, that you hunted for yourself;
'Tis thought your deer does hold you at a bay.

speeches.speech: O, O, Petruchio! Tranio hits you now.
lines: O, O, Petruchio! Tranio hits you now.

speeches.speech: I thank thee for that gird, good Tranio.
lines: I thank thee for that gird, good Tranio.

speeches.speech: Confess, confess; hath he not hit you here?
lines: Confess, confess; hath he not hit you here?

speeches.speech: 'A has a little gall'd me, I confess; And, as the jest did glance away from me, 'Tis ten to one it maim'd you two outright.
lines: 'A has a little gall'd me, I confess;
And, as the jest did glance away from me,
'Tis ten to one it maim'd you two outright.

speeches.speech: Now, in good sadness, son Petruchio, I think thou hast the veriest shrew of all.
lines: Now, in good sadness, son Petruchio,
I think thou hast the veriest shrew of all.

speeches.speech: Well, I say no; and therefore, for assurance, Let's each one send unto his wife, And he whose wife is most obedient, To come at first when he doth send for her, Shall win the wager which we will propose.
lines: Well, I say no; and therefore, for assurance,
Let's each one send unto his wife,
And he whose wife is most obedient,
To come at first when he doth send for her,
Shall win the wager which we will propose.

speeches.speech: Content. What's the wager?
lines: Content. What's the wager?

speeches.speech: Twenty crowns.
lines: Twenty crowns.

speeches.speech: Twenty crowns? I'll venture so much of my hawk or hound, But twenty times so much upon my wife.
lines: Twenty crowns?
I'll venture so much of my hawk or hound,
But twenty times so much upon my wife.

speeches.speech: A hundred then.
lines: A hundred then.

speeches.speech: Content.
lines: Content.

speeches.speech: A match! 'tis done.
lines: A match! 'tis done.

speeches.speech: Who shall begin?
lines: Who shall begin?

speeches.speech: That will I. Go, Biondello, bid your mistress come to me.
lines: That will I.
Go, Biondello, bid your mistress come to me.

speeches.speech: I go.                                     Exit
lines: I go.                                     Exit

speeches.speech: Son, I'll be your half Bianca comes.
lines: Son, I'll be your half Bianca comes.

speeches.speech: I'll have no halves; I'll bear it all myself. [Re-enter BIONDELLO] How now! what news?
lines: I'll have no halves; I'll bear it all myself.
[Re-enter BIONDELLO]
How now! what news?

speeches.speech: Sir, my mistress sends you word That she is busy and she cannot come.
lines: Sir, my mistress sends you word
That she is busy and she cannot come.

speeches.speech: How! She's busy, and she cannot come! Is that an answer?
lines: How! She's busy, and she cannot come!
Is that an answer?

speeches.speech: Ay, and a kind one too. Pray God, sir, your wife send you not a worse.
lines: Ay, and a kind one too.
Pray God, sir, your wife send you not a worse.

speeches.speech: I hope better.
lines: I hope better.

speeches.speech: Sirrah Biondello, go and entreat my wife To come to me forthwith.                 Exit BIONDELLO
lines: Sirrah Biondello, go and entreat my wife
To come to me forthwith.                 Exit BIONDELLO

speeches.speech: O, ho! entreat her! Nay, then she must needs come.
lines: O, ho! entreat her!
Nay, then she must needs come.

speeches.speech: I am afraid, sir, Do what you can, yours will not be entreated. [Re-enter BIONDELLO] Now, where's my wife?
lines: I am afraid, sir,
Do what you can, yours will not be entreated.
[Re-enter BIONDELLO]
Now, where's my wife?

speeches.speech: She says you have some goodly jest in hand: She will not come; she bids you come to her.
lines: She says you have some goodly jest in hand:
She will not come; she bids you come to her.

speeches.speech: Worse and worse; she will not come! O vile, Intolerable, not to be endur'd! Sirrah Grumio, go to your mistress; Say I command her come to me.               Exit GRUMIO
lines: Worse and worse; she will not come! O vile,
Intolerable, not to be endur'd!
Sirrah Grumio, go to your mistress;
Say I command her come to me.               Exit GRUMIO

speeches.speech: I know her answer.
lines: I know her answer.

speeches.speech: What?
lines: What?

speeches.speech: She will not.
lines: She will not.

speeches.speech: The fouler fortune mine, and there an end.
lines: The fouler fortune mine, and there an end.

speeches.speech: Now, by my holidame, here comes Katherina!
lines: Now, by my holidame, here comes Katherina!

speeches.speech: What is your sir, that you send for me?
lines: What is your sir, that you send for me?

speeches.speech: Where is your sister, and Hortensio's wife?
lines: Where is your sister, and Hortensio's wife?

speeches.speech: They sit conferring by the parlour fire.
lines: They sit conferring by the parlour fire.

speeches.speech: Go, fetch them hither; if they deny to come. Swinge me them soundly forth unto their husbands. Away, I say, and bring them hither straight.
lines: Go, fetch them hither; if they deny to come.
Swinge me them soundly forth unto their husbands.
Away, I say, and bring them hither straight.

speeches.speech: Here is a wonder, if you talk of a wonder.
lines: Here is a wonder, if you talk of a wonder.

speeches.speech: And so it is. I wonder what it bodes.
lines: And so it is. I wonder what it bodes.

speeches.speech: Marry, peace it bodes, and love, and quiet life, An awful rule, and right supremacy; And, to be short, what not that's sweet and happy.
lines: Marry, peace it bodes, and love, and quiet life,
An awful rule, and right supremacy;
And, to be short, what not that's sweet and happy.

speeches.speech: Now fair befall thee, good Petruchio! The wager thou hast won; and I will add Unto their losses twenty thousand crowns; Another dowry to another daughter, For she is chang'd, as she had never been.
lines: Now fair befall thee, good Petruchio!
The wager thou hast won; and I will add
Unto their losses twenty thousand crowns;
Another dowry to another daughter,
For she is chang'd, as she had never been.

speeches.speech: Nay, I will win my wager better yet, And show more sign of her obedience, Her new-built virtue and obedience. [Re-enter KATHERINA with BIANCA and WIDOW] See where she comes, and brings your froward wives As prisoners to her womanly persuasion. Katherine, that cap of yours becomes you not: Off with that bauble, throw it underfoot.
lines: Nay, I will win my wager better yet,
And show more sign of her obedience,
Her new-built virtue and obedience.
[Re-enter KATHERINA with BIANCA and WIDOW]
See where she comes, and brings your froward wives
As prisoners to her womanly persuasion.
Katherine, that cap of yours becomes you not:
Off with that bauble, throw it underfoot.

speeches.speech: Lord, let me never have a cause to sigh Till I be brought to such a silly pass!
lines: Lord, let me never have a cause to sigh
Till I be brought to such a silly pass!

speeches.speech: Fie! what a foolish duty call you this?
lines: Fie! what a foolish duty call you this?

speeches.speech: I would your duty were as foolish too; The wisdom of your duty, fair Bianca, Hath cost me a hundred crowns since supper-time!
lines: I would your duty were as foolish too;
The wisdom of your duty, fair Bianca,
Hath cost me a hundred crowns since supper-time!

speeches.speech: The more fool you for laying on my duty.
lines: The more fool you for laying on my duty.

speeches.speech: Katherine, I charge thee, tell these headstrong women What duty they do owe their lords and husbands.
lines: Katherine, I charge thee, tell these headstrong women
What duty they do owe their lords and husbands.

speeches.speech: Come, come, you're mocking; we will have no telling.
lines: Come, come, you're mocking; we will have no telling.

speeches.speech: Come on, I say; and first begin with her.
lines: Come on, I say; and first begin with her.

speeches.speech: She shall not.
lines: She shall not.

speeches.speech: I say she shall. And first begin with her.
lines: I say she shall. And first begin with her.

speeches.speech: Fie, fie! unknit that threatening unkind brow, And dart not scornful glances from those eyes To wound thy lord, thy king, thy governor. It blots thy beauty as frosts do bite the meads, Confounds thy fame as whirlwinds shake fair buds, And in no sense is meet or amiable. A woman mov'd is like a fountain troubled- Muddy, ill-seeming, thick, bereft of beauty; And while it is so, none so dry or thirsty Will deign to sip or touch one drop of it. Thy husband is thy lord, thy life, thy keeper, Thy head, thy sovereign; one that cares for thee, And for thy maintenance commits his body To painful labour both by sea and land, To watch the night in storms, the day in cold, Whilst thou liest warm at home, secure and safe; And craves no other tribute at thy hands But love, fair looks, and true obedience- Too little payment for so great a debt. Such duty as the subject owes the prince, Even such a woman oweth to her husband; And when she is froward, peevish, sullen, sour, And not obedient to his honest will, What is she but a foul contending rebel And graceless traitor to her loving lord? I am asham'd that women are so simple To offer war where they should kneel for peace; Or seek for rule, supremacy, and sway, When they are bound to serve, love, and obey. Why are our bodies soft and weak and smooth, Unapt to toil and trouble in the world, But that our soft conditions and our hearts Should well agree with our external parts? Come, come, you forward and unable worms! My mind hath been as big as one of yours, My heart as great, my reason haply more, To bandy word for word and frown for frown; But now I see our lances are but straws, Our strength as weak, our weakness past compare, That seeming to be most which we indeed least are. Then vail your stomachs, for it is no boot, And place your hands below your husband's foot; In token of which duty, if he please, My hand is ready, may it do him ease.
lines: Fie, fie! unknit that threatening unkind brow,
And dart not scornful glances from those eyes
To wound thy lord, thy king, thy governor.
It blots thy beauty as frosts do bite the meads,
Confounds thy fame as whirlwinds shake fair buds,
And in no sense is meet or amiable.
A woman mov'd is like a fountain troubled-
Muddy, ill-seeming, thick, bereft of beauty;
And while it is so, none so dry or thirsty
Will deign to sip or touch one drop of it.
Thy husband is thy lord, thy life, thy keeper,
Thy head, thy sovereign; one that cares for thee,
And for thy maintenance commits his body
To painful labour both by sea and land,
To watch the night in storms, the day in cold,
Whilst thou liest warm at home, secure and safe;
And craves no other tribute at thy hands
But love, fair looks, and true obedience-
Too little payment for so great a debt.
Such duty as the subject owes the prince,
Even such a woman oweth to her husband;
And when she is froward, peevish, sullen, sour,
And not obedient to his honest will,
What is she but a foul contending rebel
And graceless traitor to her loving lord?
I am asham'd that women are so simple
To offer war where they should kneel for peace;
Or seek for rule, supremacy, and sway,
When they are bound to serve, love, and obey.
Why are our bodies soft and weak and smooth,
Unapt to toil and trouble in the world,
But that our soft conditions and our hearts
Should well agree with our external parts?
Come, come, you forward and unable worms!
My mind hath been as big as one of yours,
My heart as great, my reason haply more,
To bandy word for word and frown for frown;
But now I see our lances are but straws,
Our strength as weak, our weakness past compare,
That seeming to be most which we indeed least are.
Then vail your stomachs, for it is no boot,
And place your hands below your husband's foot;
In token of which duty, if he please,
My hand is ready, may it do him ease.

speeches.speech: Why, there's a wench! Come on, and kiss me, Kate.
lines: Why, there's a wench! Come on, and kiss me, Kate.

speeches.speech: Well, go thy ways, old lad, for thou shalt ha't.
lines: Well, go thy ways, old lad, for thou shalt ha't.

speeches.speech: 'Tis a good hearing when children are toward.
lines: 'Tis a good hearing when children are toward.

speeches.speech: But a harsh hearing when women are froward.
lines: But a harsh hearing when women are froward.

speeches.speech: Come, Kate, we'll to bed. We three are married, but you two are sped. [To LUCENTIO]  'Twas I won the wager, though you hit the white; And being a winner, God give you good night!
lines: Come, Kate, we'll to bed.
We three are married, but you two are sped.
[To LUCENTIO]  'Twas I won the wager, though you hit the white;
And being a winner, God give you good night!

speeches.speech: Now go thy ways; thou hast tam'd a curst shrow.
lines: Now go thy ways; thou hast tam'd a curst shrow.

speeches.speech: 'Tis a wonder, by your leave, she will be tam'd so.
lines: 'Tis a wonder, by your leave, she will be tam'd so.
